author: Chih-Yuan Chang
paragraph: What is weaponized light? When used intentionally to damage, light can become a weapon. What does it mean to ​weaponize light?​ The verb ​To Weaponize​ originated as a technical jargon in the U.S. military, and led to a broader use of the word ​Weaponization. The act of using something as a weapon to inflict harm on people. Every practical and virtual medium can apply to this word and convert a thing that was unharmful to fatal. For example, current popular issues such as viruses, genetic code, cyberterrorism, and economy, finance, and so on, have become a tool to cause damage against another. “Weaponize” is its verb. It originated as a technical jargon in the U.S. military. During the Cold War, scientists weaponized rockets, implementing them with nuclear material, and equipping them for launch. The Oxford English Dictionary first attests “weaponize” in 1957, citing the controversial aerospace pioneer Wernher von Braun, who used neologism in the New York Times concerning ballistic missiles.

author: Chih-Yuan Chang
paragraph: 13 Google Ngram- “Weaponized” It refers to the act of using something as a weapon to inflict harm; it can mean to convert something that was harmless to harmful. The Oxford English Dictionary first attests the use of the word “weaponize” in 1957, by controversial aerospace pioneer Wernher von Braun, who used it as a neologism in the New York Times. Many of our everyday technologies were initially invented, developed, and employed for military use. For example, the original computer technology was commenced with punched cards and mechanical looms to figure out problems at the beginning of the 20th century. The technology improved to an electronic digital programmable computer, called Colossus, during World War II to decipher messages from Nazi encryption machines. (Heath, 2017) Moreover, the modern Internet for connecting computers worldwide was also originally developed and employed by militaries. Mostly, the light applies as the form of laser, which emits a narrow coherent high-powered laser beam of visible light intended to be used to highlight something. For example, the military considerably uses laser pointers or dazzlers as a weapon for targeting, ranging, communicating, and defending against enemies. As laser technology is broadly accessible and affordable it has been used outside the police force. The pro-democracy demonstrators in Hong Kong used point lasers against street surveillance cameras and the to protect themselves from being seen or recorded. (ABC News, 2019)

author: Chih-Yuan Chang
paragraph: 14 Over and above the employment of weaponized light in the real world, there are abundant examples of weaponized light in fictional movies and novels. For example, one of the most classic movies with light as a weapon is the Star Wars saga. The lightsaber, similar medium to a laser sword, is a distinctive weapon to uphold justice and peace throughout the galaxy in the movie. Other movies involving weaponized lights to destroy and defend, such as the movie “The Day the Earth Stood Still,” “Ultraman,” and “Iron Man,” contain robots to launch lasers as the main characteristic. Movies such as “Resident Evil II” and “Final Destination” have scenes that utilize light or laser to cut human flesh in pieces and burn human skin to death. Furthermore, in the novel “It” and “The Dark Tower,” both written by Stephen King, were established with the core of the mysterious evil spirit called Deadlights. They were writing forms that once humans have eye contact with the bright lights would cause death, permanent insanity, or disturb by nightmares afterward. The movie “It” conveys the essence of Deadlights as an actual figure of the deadly extra-dimensional beast, which is the crown. (Ting, 2019)

author: Chih-Yuan Chang
paragraph: Self-Experimental works Geometric shape of light This research collects examples of weaponized light in popular media. It reveals that weaponized lights can be analysed by their sources, their applications, and their forms.

author: Chih-Yuan Chang
paragraph: 15 Geometric Shape of Light Weaponized lights can be better understood using fundamental concepts of geometry: the Point, the Line, and the Plane. The different forms light takes are symbolized in different shapes. The smallest unit of light is the point. A point in geometry is a location, which has no size, width, or length. In this project, it stands for a dominant entity. For example, strobe lights and the sun are the single sources of weaponized light; hence they are categorized as points. Also, although Steven King’s Deadlights is composed of three lights, it is issued in the Point because they represent an evil spirit altogether. A Line is the second category, which implies the extension of two points. When light is formed by two or more parts, or it creates a rectangular shape. The bug zapper uses ultraviolet light and a grid. The third category is the Plane, which is a two-dimensional flat surface. In this project, the background is overlaid with four colors, each of which represents where weaponized light belongs: both reality and irreality.

author: Chih-Yuan Chang
paragraph: 16 Top Left: Robots Top Right: Deadlights Bottom Left: Strobe lights Bottom Right: Spaceships Understanding the logicality allows people to decode the relationships between the lights and their origin. For example, the Deadlights set on the black cotton; it indicates this light is unreal and is from the non-reality world, which might exist in a novel or a movie or more. Furthermore, I connect all of them with red cotton, which describes the meaning of strength and danger, to signify the power of weaponized light from my research. Microscopic discovery During one of the design studio classes, I explored the perspective of shaping light and the manipulative potentialities through photography. In this project, I considered how to cast light and shadow under the microscopic patterns with two opposed materials: an aluminum foil and a pencil-lead box. They have contrasting attribution that the foil reflects the light regardless of its shape, while

author: Chih-Yuan Chang
paragraph: 17 the transparent rhombus pencil-lead box allows the light to penetrate and shade shadows.

author: Chih-Yuan Chang
paragraph: Microscopic discovery Due to the distinct characteristics, it is intriguing to discover the methodology of applying light and the subtle world of the objects. The aluminum foil has its uneven surface that could reflect diverse patterns as the shape varies. On the other hand, the transparent pencil-lead box creates a fantastic kaleidoscope vision with the light accessing through its hollow-rhombus case. With the aid of the phone camera, it could capture the clarity and the blurriness of the objects, which motivated me to discover the protection against weaponized light.

author: Chih-Yuan Chang
paragraph: I’m Stunned In the meanwhile, I intended to undergo how it feels like enduring in front of the intense strobing light with the three primary colors of light, which are red, green, blue.

author: Chih-Yuan Chang
paragraph: I’m Stunned Considering capturing my reaction, I set up the phone-tripod facing me and utilize my phone camera to record each color for minutes. It was extremely hurtful to my eyes and mentally dizzy for abiding half a minute in front of the strobing light. I had strong after-effects starring the light that it seized my response to the surroundings.

author: Chih-Yuan Chang
paragraph: 19 I’m Stunned - Photo Manipulations To display the video, I built a black-acrylic box as a projector. Viewing through the box, it doesn’t directly project the video to the viewer but uses the reflection from the back acrylic. The purpose of this reflecting idea is to perform in the protection of weaponized light. With the help of the representation and colored acrylic boards as the filters, the perspective of appreciating the video is becoming various. Furthermore, after recording them as video footage, I screenshotted some postures to manipulate the reactions into static images. I supplement filters, color effects, and photo overlays to present the physical and mental after-effects from exposing in front of the harsh light.

author: Chih-Yuan Chang
paragraph: 20 I’m Stunned - Weaponization and Protection

author: Chih-Yuan Chang
paragraph: The Capstone This research aims to demonstrate dangerous uses of light. It focuses on the light emitted from the sun, which is also called the electromagnetic spectrum, from the Radio rate to Gamma rate, and on the artificial lights, such as LED, laser point, and blue light.

author: Chih-Yuan Chang
paragraph: To demonstrate the abstract idea of the weaponization of light, I use the Holga camera to photograph since photos are the outcome of capturing the light. The Holga camera is a low-cost construction made of plastic and simple meniscus lens, which yields images to display vignetting, light leaks, overlaps, and other distortion probability. The use of the Holga camera metaphorical indicates that the meaning of weaponized light is unpredictable and easily-accessible. Thus, with the inherent features of this phenomenal camera, I create a series of 120mm negatives to display the connection between the danger of lights and their consequences through artistic photography.

author: Chih-Yuan Chang
paragraph: Besides the astonishing outcome of the negatives, I also create a table of the physically and mentally corresponding reactions to the light spectrum. The electromagnetic spectrum is the most common classification of the invisible and visible light, which are types of radiation classified in the increasing order of wavelength from Gamma-rays to Radio waves, as a measurement. Before enjoying the photos, there is a description decoding the variation between negatives and positives corresponding to the lights and reactions in the table: the negative images stand for the lights that are invisible like infrared and ultraviolet 22 light. In contrast, the positives stand for the visible. Also, each reaction has a similar characteristic or highlight of the photos. For example, the obscure and trailed images are for the “Blurry vision,” and the corrupted pieces appearing in the pictures are for the reaction of “Organ damage.” In addition, there is a hidden meaning between the spaces around each photo: the more area on a page, the more dangerous the light is. That is, the more extreme the light is, the smaller the image will be because of it, the less likely it is for us to see and discover.

author: Chih-Yuan Chang
paragraph: Contribution and conclusion Light is an overwhelmed entity that we have been exposing every day and night. Designers in different fields have been employing light as a tool to shape environments, atmospheres, or objects. However, too much or too extreme use of light can be harmful physically and mentally, for example, if a bright light is pointed directly at the eyes, we would feel physically irritated to our eyes and further psychologically discomfort our brain and mood. Some relevant examples such as the flashlights at the back of every phone, the projectors in classrooms, the laser from the laser pointers and laser-cut machine which when they are illuminated intentionally hurt someone by causing blind, dizziness, and hallucinations. That is to say, light could more easily be weaponized than we think, which light seems to be a positive medium, when the purpose is hostile.

You powered me through my moments of frustration and ignited not only this thesis, but also me and I will always appreciate you for that.

paragraph: I want to thank my thesis professors for helping me ideate and contextualize my ideas. Thank you, Gaia Hwang, for encouraging me to continue forward. Thank you, Jean Brennan, for always proposing new ways to approach this process during our meetings. Thank you, Francis Pharr, for your insight in the preliminary stages of this thesis, it was instrumental to the formation of my argument. Thank you, Corwin Green, for helping me shift Welcome Home to work within both digital and physical ​ ​ realms.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: Other professors crucial to the development of this thesis include Karen Dunn, Katya Moorman, and Joseph Cuillier. You all encouraged me to be explorative and dynamic during this process to push the limits of what is expected.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: Finally, I would like to thank my moral supporters and those who contributed to the development of Welcome Home. These include my family, Sandra King, Jordan ​ ​ King, William Clarke, Anndraya Ashburn, Aaliyah Sims, Elaine Shepherd, Anja Shepherd, and Destiny Decosta, friends, Maya Coleman, Jatori Washington, Corey Johnson, Keith Garrett Wright, Sebastian Mills, Denari Purdy, Amber Henry-Scott, Josh Reed, and Pratt sisters, Christina Burroughs, Sierra Wheeler, Sekai Hurdle, Taia Pollock, Zoe Priest. RESEARCH QUESTIONS How can a historically displaced community build home wherever they are? How can the African American community utilize home-creating to help acknowledge and begin to undo systemic injustices and their consequential byproducts? How can African Americans maintain and honor our cultural identity and visibility in an America that historically erases them?

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: ABSTRACT For the African American community, a population alienated from our continent of origin, home is a difficult concept to define. Since the first slave ship arrived in Jamestown in 1619, black people have been treated as outsiders in "our own country." Here in America, most physical, cultural, or social institutions are built without our input and rarely provide any stability or positive representation for us to hold on to and own (Baldwin, Bennett, West). Injustices throughout history, ranging from slavery to racist Jim Crow era laws to numerous recent cases of police brutality, paired with our limited understanding of our genealogy and ancestry illuminates the pervasive systems used to ensure our rootlessness as a community. And those who return to Africa in hopes of a homecoming 400 years in the making are often met with further cultural alienation and the ravages of colonialism. This inherent sense of displacement has followed African Americans through generations and has solidified for us the idiom, "You can never go home."

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: 6 Every physical landmark and monument built in America to commemorate and celebrate the black community reminds us of our struggle for equality, acceptance, and inclusion in this country. We live in a country that undermines and discounts our engagement and contribution to its very being. Moreover, countless physical, mental, and emotional tactics and traumas have been used to disorient the black community over the last 400 years. ​​​​​​​

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: Our default approach to rectifying these injustices, to making America a home for African Americans, is to build physical places and structures that serve very limited purposes; they are either informational, memorial, or congregational, and all seem to reference the historical traumas inflicted upon us. This is the antithesis of home. By removing the physical limitations on home-creating, conversational spaces are forged that will force us to readdress our interactions with each other and external forces, our suppressed emotional vulnerabilities, and collective identity and culture. Additionally, by curating our home, as collaborative spaces that encourage discourse, introspection, and celebration, African Americans can manifest and enact home for ourselves in any context.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: 7 INTRODUCTION Defining Home "Home is a way of organizing space within our minds" (Klinkenborg). This mental space allows people to actively compartmentalize what is home and what is not. For many, this method of organization spills over into their physical environment, as they can easily distinguish the people and possessions that remind them of their home. One way they do this is to categorize home objects and spaces by connecting them to lineage, family, and origin.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: When someone is incapable of attaching their home to physical landmarks or objects, they create an abstract understanding of this concept. They begin to correlate their home with what it provides them. Their home becomes safety, security, representation, self-reflection, etc. They find home in boundless ways, as they have removed the physical limitations on this space.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: Home is now a sense of belonging and being, rather than a physical environment. Home is representation in the ordinary; home is cathartic, judgment-free expression; home is collaborating with like-minded individuals. The importance of home is being able to connect to something bigger than yourself. Home presents a place of solace and reliability without constraint or fear of alienation. Home becomes collective identity and culture. Home can be found in literature, mass media, or art, and/or

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: 8 concretely by building spaces. This allows people to always feel connected to home, regardless of their physical location or immediate surroundings.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: Chronicling Black Home-Creating and Systemic Displacement ​ This timeline provides an overview of important events that integrally shaped African American's sense of home (or lack thereof) throughout American history. It begins in 1619, when the first slave ships arrived in America, and ends with a recent design project slated for 2019/2020.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: The left side of the timeline showcases the moments in history when African Americans created home in America and the right highlights times when external forces displaced the black community.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: The most notable events are shown in a different color that corresponds with the key below. The key explains the bars placed over each event. Each event is categorized as follows:

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: 9 10 11 12 13 14 15 16 17 Historical Trauma in the African American Community African Americans of modern-day still suffer from the transgenerational trauma inflicted during slavery, Jim Crow, and the Civil Rights Movement. Specifically, "historical trauma can be observed in African-Americans' unresolved grief, expressed as depression and despair and their harboring of unexplained anger expressed as aggression and rage" (Rowe, Woodson). Moreover, we've internalized the feeling of self-hatred and self-loathing fueled by the lie of black inferiority that powers American society and politics. This has impacted not only our ability to feel at home among our own people and culture but within our own bodies. Psychologists argue that "manifesting resilient, powerful stories can help black ​ people negotiate the distress of historical trauma" (Rowe, Woodson). Specifically, ​ collecting and disseminating accurate compelling stories about our individual and collective identity helps us understand the strength of our community, restore our pride and values in ourselves, and create home.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: THE MULTIPLICITY OF BLACK STORYTELLING The Challenge of Blackness "The challenge of blackness is the challenge of living today in tomorrow's truth" (Bennett, 43). Lerone Bennett Jr. uses this essay as an opportunity to explore what blackness is and who defines it. In one instance, Bennett asserts that blackness is "the universe of values and attitudes and orientations which rises, like dew, from the depths of our ancestral experience and pulls us toward the distant shores of our"

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: 18 destiny" (34). Bennett states that black people have control of our narrative and how we are perceived, and must communally define blackness. In six ways, we are moving as a community to meet the challenge to define blackness: through black studies, through independent thought, by redefining what an intellectual is and should do, by crafting "a cadre of organic intellectuals who live and think within a perspective of blood and pain and want," by "making blackness a presence in the land," and by reevaluating the American experience and American society (Bennett, 38).

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: Bennett emphasizes the importance of black progression based on our own definitions and beliefs and not those of the white majority. He claims that every concept present in America, including the definition of man and American history, must be revised to include the African and Native communities. Bennett also expresses the importance of uniting as a community "to do what must be done" (42). Bennett's affirmations about the salience of exploring the multiplicity of blackness through education, discourse, and dissemination are integral to home-creating within the African American community. Specifically, a few of Bennett's proposals for defining blackness, including reevaluating our presence in America, can aid in building the black home. By reexamining our opportunities for growth, stability, and visibility as African Americans, we are provided the space to build stronger connections to our shared experiences, each other, and our home in America.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: 19 I Am Not Your Negro The documentary, I Am Not Your Negro, showcases a compilation of James ​ ​ Baldwin's notes and letters from the 1970s for his unfinished book, Remember the ​ House. Baldwin's writings are narrated by Samuel L. Jackson and showcased ​ alongside potent imagery, as well as speeches and interviews Baldwin gave during his life. The documentary weaves many linguistic elements to highlight the black struggle for equality, understanding, and acceptance in America. The film's juxtaposition of notable moments in African American history during Baldwin's era, including the assassinations of Martin Luther King Jr., Medgar Evers, and Malcolm X, against those of modern-day, such as the Obama election and Ferguson demonstrations, underscores the accuracy of Baldwin's acute assumptions and assertions about race relations in America. Although Baldwin died in 1987, his words remain relevant.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: In many of his documented speeches and interviews, Baldwin speaks directly to a mostly white audience. For the white majority, Baldwin is the "token" black representative, a feeling that many black people have experienced. Although he was usually the lone black voice, Baldwin was not intimidated or apprehensive about speaking candidly and honestly about the realities of being black in America. Some of the uncomfortable truths Baldwin confronts in his appearances include achieving the American Dream as black people "because [we] are denied a participation in it by [our] very presence will wreck it" and the disregard for black bodies because

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: 20 white people no longer "need us to pick the cotton" (Baldwin). Baldwin's assertions underscore African Americans' exclusion and alienation in American social, political, and economic institutions and our difficulty creating home in America.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: Specifically, the writing Witness stood as the most salient, as it addresses the ​ ​ different roles black people play in the community. Likewise, Baldwin states "that the line which separates a witness and an actor is very thin, indeed, nevertheless the line is real" (Baldwin). He also explains how his inability to establish organizational ties to any African American group or fight alongside his brethren in the South affected his morale and sense of purpose in the movement. Baldwins understands "that part of his responsibility as a witness was… to write the story and to get it out" (Baldwin). This particular writing speaks to my role as a communication designer in aiding my community. Although I may not be the traditional social activist, my design sensibilities and insight allow me to address the multiple aspects of black home-creating in a new way. Overall, I Am Not Your Negro highlights the salience of ​ ​ Baldwin's writing and his ability to address many facets of the black collective experience and home, regardless of the time period or ever-changing world.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: Notes of the Native Son In Notes of the Native Son, James Baldwin juxtaposes his experiences with his ​ ​ blackness against that of his father. Baldwin details his present life, as he reflects on his feelings about burying his father and attending his funeral, their estranged

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: 21 relationship during childhood, his father's upbringing, and how everything affected him. Specifically, when discussing his father, Baldwin asserts that "he claimed to be proud of his blackness, but it had also been the cause of much humiliation, and it had fixed bleak boundaries to his life" (Baldwin 87). Baldwin's continued analysis of his father exemplifies his bitterness and uncertainty as a black man and his unwillingness to engage with white people and interact with his own children. In many instances, Baldwin's father attempts to warn his son about the malice present in the world, but Baldwin is incapable of fully understanding his father's apprehensions until he experiences life himself. Moreover, Baldwin recalls his time living in New Jersey, where he learned that to "be a Negro meant, precisely, that one was never looked at but was simply at the mercy of the reflexes the color of one's skin caused in other people" (Baldwin 93).

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: Additionally, Baldwin details the occasions when diverse members of the black community have convened during times of upheaval. He explains how World War II affected the black community and how "everybody felt a directionless, hopeless bitterness, as well as that panic" as they waited for their loved ones to return safely home (Baldwin 101). Lastly, his recount of his father's funeral speaks volumes about the hatred people harbor instead of addressing the pain they feel and a black parent's desire to prepare their children for the world.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: 22 This essay highlights the thought processes pertinent to black people as we navigate life and traditional white spaces. Moreover, by detailing the complexity of black familial ties and our connection to our collective pain and despair, Baldwin explores the vulnerability of human interaction. He explores the dimensionality of black trauma and its impact on communal home building, as it can cause disdain and resentment of one's identity or encourage solidarity and collaboration among a group.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: In the Wake, On Blackness and Being Christina Sharpe's In the Wake: On Blackness and Being explores the complexity of ​ ​ living in the wake as a black person, teaching/ learning in the wake, and performing wake work. Sharpe juxtaposes personal anecdotes from her adolescence, adult life, and relationships with deceased loved ones against cited academic research to underscore her reasonings. Moreover, Sharpe recounts her sister, nephew, mother, and brother's deaths to explore the various definitions of "wake" pertinent to her book. The definition of wake shifts significantly throughout the first chapter, as she examines the term through multiple lenses including as "the track left on the water's surface by a ship," "the state of wakefulness; consciousness," and "a watch or vigil held beside the body of someone who has died" (3, 4, 10). The ever-changing definition supports Sharpe's argument that black people are currently living in the "wake," as it is a "conceptual framework of and for living blackness in the diaspora in the still unfolding aftermaths of Atlantic chattel slavery (2)."

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: 23 Sharpe details the trauma inflicted by many of the social, political, and economic hindrances imposed in America to examine how living in the wake affects the black masses. These include the policies of President Barack Obama, stop-and-frisk, Operation Clean Halls, and the disproportioned high Black incarceration rates. These systemic injustices further support her assertion that "anything and everything can be and is done" to black people (16).

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: Sharpe continues her analysis of blackness in the wake by juxtaposing her experiences teaching about the Holocaust and slavery in her college course, Memory for Forgetting. Moreover, when examining the tragedies of the Holocaust, her students were empathic and considerate. Contradictorily, they were somewhat apathetic when learning about the impact slavery, and its aftermaths have on the current black life, as many questioned what the enslaved would have done otherwise (11). To convey the severity and desolateness of slavery to her students, Sharpe compared Simon Srebnik's, a Polish Holocaust survivor of the Chelmno extermination camp, experience returning to Chelmo, Poland post-WWII, with that of the formerly enslaved Africans and their descendants post- Civil War United States. As shown in Claude Lanzmann's Shoah, Srebnik is met with resentment and anger ​ ​ upon his return. However, unlike Srebnik, who successfully emigrated to Israel after the war, the formerly enslaved Africans were forced to remain in the country of their oppressors, as they had nowhere else to go. The apparent disconnect underscores her assertion that "the means and modes of"

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: 24 Black subjection may have changed, but the fact and structure of that subjection remain" (12).

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: Sharpe also uses this vignette to explain how black scholars interested in learning, processing, and educating others about slavery gain insight through not only academic studies but also personal day to day interactions with the byproducts of systemic injustices. She states that we must reevaluate our own relationship with and forced skewed conditioning towards the topic in order to accurately dissect it. Sharpe also suggests that any work done to "continue to imagine new ways to live in the wake of slavery, in slavery's afterlives, to survive… the afterlife of property" should be considered wake work (18). Moreover, Sharpe differentiates wake work from "the work of melancholia or mourning" by posing a series of questions that "trouble the ways most museums and memorials take up trauma and memory" (19-20). She ponders how institutions built to educate, memorialize, and recognize the events of slavery and its aftermaths accurately represent the wake of the present and how black lives are supposed to come to terms with the ongoing quotidian atrocities. She positions wake work as a way to address these issues. Sharpe also reminds those interested in pursuing wake work about the many concepts they must consider to maximize its impact. These include "thinking through containment, regulation, punishment, capture, and captivity and the ways the manifold representations of blackness become the symbol… for the less-than-human being condemned to death," producing concretized black work, and considering the

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: 25 implications of antiblackness (21). By recognizing these concepts, among others, black people will be able to adequately address black life, black suffering, and unforeseeable problems that will arise.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: In the context of this thesis, all work done to explore its central premise, creating a limitless black home that can be sustained in any environment, is wake work. In home-creating for African Americans, all aspects of Sharpe's argument, specifically that we are living in the wake and must address our internalized trauma resulting from systemic injustices and feelings of desolation as American citizens, hold precedence.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: DESIGNING THE BLACK HOME The biggest oversight in many approaches to creating home within the black community, by both designers and non-designers alike, is a valid critical discussion about what home must provide for us. What are we looking for, and why do we need it?

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: Historically, civil rights activists have proposed creating home by returning to Africa or through assimilation to the white majority without taking into account what is being gained or lost in these proposals (Garvey; Washington). Moreover, Garvey's approach suggests returning to a country that many members of the African American community are unfamiliar with and cannot trace our ancestry back to.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: 26 Similarly, this approach would further displace African Americans and force them to learn to navigate a foreign country as outsiders. On the contrary, Washington's suggestion of assimilation results in a loss of culture, collective identity, and community.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: Several decades later, many designers are addressing this same problem by creating physical spaces where the African American community can convene to feel included and represented. Although placemaking projects are important, they serve limited purposes, as they are either informative, memorial, or congregative. As I reflect on these varied approaches, I wonder how we, as a people, truly benefit from them and what can I do to broaden the understanding of home. ​​​​​​​ My approach aims to counter the limited scope around creating home within the design community. Instead of focusing on building home in physical places, I explore the concept of home as a mental space.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: Removing the physical limitations on home allows for more abstraction and creativity in the design process and future application. This allows the African American community to engage in conversations that explore our cultural identity, relationship with each other and external forces, and emotional vulnerabilities. In doing so, I am able to develop an alternative methodology for creating and sustaining home within the black community.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: 27 Ultimately, my approach adds to the enclave of experimental works currently being produced by other creatives, including Iyapo Repository, the Black School, Alexandra Bell, and Hank Willis Thomas, committed to making home in unexpected ways and contributes to the grander idea of diversity in design.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: Preliminary Explorations The following projects, Blacademics, Endgoal, and The C Pod, are preliminary ​ ​ ​ ​ explorations into the multi-facets of black home-creating in the academic, economic, and sociopolitical spheres. They approach building our home through introspective discourse, collaborative action, and hyper-visibility of the black plight.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: Blacademics Blacademics focused on providing black art students at predominantly white ​ institutions a space to conduct meaningful conversations about issues that are important to us.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: 28 This cultural probe is the preliminary stage of a larger project, Blacademics. The ​ ​ probe prompted students to complete the following fill in the blank sentence: My classmates will never understand… and then asked them to explain why. Roughly 40 probes were collected from two prominent art universities in NYC, The Fashion Institute of Technology and Pratt Institute. The responses garnered ranged in specificity and context. In order to determine what topics black students were most interested in, the cards were sectioned into nine categories: My Skin, My Logo, Being the Only One, My Personal Hindrances, Classroom Struggles, Individual Thought, Their Privilege, We're Not the Same, and I Don't Know Where I'm From, and Other Issues. This project highlights the oversight in many PWI (Predominately White Institution) classroom settings, as black students find it difficult to speak candidly about their race and culture, diversity, and tokenism with their peers.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: Following the cultural probe, I led several participatory workshops, in the form of roundtable discussions with black art students at PWIs. The topic for the table, My Skin, My logo, encompassed a large bulk of the cultural probe responses, as most

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: 29 referred to the need for more representation, conversation, and inclusion of our black identity and culture in academic spaces.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: The discussion format was inspired by the first roundtable AIGA NY held during their Call for Ideas initiative. During the event, facilitators asked participants to express their visions for a diverse and inclusive design field, the obstacles they've encountered attempting to achieve their vision, and the opportunities for social change in the design community.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: Moreover, students participating in the Blacademics roundtable were given 15 ​ ​ minutes to brainstorm about visions, obstacles, and opportunities their blackness affords them. They were then asked to share why they wrote certain answers and discuss them. This roundtable ran for roughly 45 mins and generated a cathartic, necessary dialogue. For many, this was their first time expressing their frustrations with not only their respective programs and peers but themselves. They expressed their difficulty having nuanced, productive conversations about a range of topics, including the lack of inclusion and understanding in the art and design field, cultural dissimilarities with their other classmates, and the socioeconomic and sociopolitical climate of America. The roundtable discussion also provided them the space to be critical of their blackness, artistic voice, and experiences.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: The last stage of the Blacademics project was to create a toolkit and disseminate it ​ ​ through a social media account. The issues expressed in the cultural probe and roundtable discussions are universal to many black academics across PWI campuses. The Blacademics toolkit equips other black students with a set of ​ ​ guidelines to follow in order to facilitate their own dialogue about their identities, deterrents, and successes. The accompanying Instagram account features quotes from the roundtables conducted and examples of visions, opportunities, and obstacles. It provides visuals of the process and makes the information more digestible. In the bio of the Blacademics Instagram account, there is a link to ​ ​ download the toolkit and instructions detailing how to use it. It was important to me that the toolkit was accessible and approachable.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: Endgoal is a speculative participatory platform that allows HBCU alumni to donate directly to HBCU students. The project began as an exploration into how HBCUs are funded federally and privately. The avid secondary research collected concurred that Historically Black colleges and universities are disproportionately underfunded when compared to their predominantly white counterparts. However, the research also showed that the percentage of alumni donations at HBCUs is significantly higher than the national average. To coincide with the secondary data, six in-depth interviews were conducted with current and former HBCU students. During the interviews, the students expressed pride and admiration for their alma maters, highlighted the financial inadequacies in their college experiences due to the lack of funding, and stated they would donate as alumni. The information collected from the primary and secondary research determined the layout and features of the platform. Similar to Kickstarter, the platform allows student

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: 32 and faculty-led organizations from all 101 HBCUs to submit their projects for funding. Alumni may then donate directly to those organizations. This platform builds home as a space for community engagement, collaboration, and sustainability.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: The C Pod is a project designed to combat the commodification of black trauma. ​ Moreover, the commodification of black trauma denies the victims agency over their image, repeatedly traumatizes the viewers, and fuels the algorithm. The imagery itself is exploitative, manipulative, and considered illegitimate by most. The project began as a speculative teaching tool that would pair with AI learning. Its primary purpose is to simulate the emotions felt by victims of police brutality through the use of sound and scents.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: 33 When creating a prototype of the C Pod, I used a long black column design that was subsequently hung from a tree to reference the lynching of black men throughout history. I wanted the project to feel culturally and historically relevant.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: Likewise, I chose the slaying of Michael Brown as the focal point of the project. The audio selected details the moment Lesley McSpadden, Michael Brown's mother, found out the police officer who murdered her son was acquitted. The soundscape features her wails juxtaposed against the popping of shattering glass, the voice of the police officers telling mourners to move from the streets of Ferguson, where they have been protesting for weeks and fire crackling. Once a participant steps inside, he is engulfed in damp black fabric that simulates the wetness of McSpadden's tears and is met with the smell of her cigarettes. Additionally, the participant can see the outside world through the fabric, but the outside world is incapable of seeing them as anything other than a black fixture, a direct reference to racial profiling, the white gaze, and the concept of double consciousness.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: 34 WELCOME HOME: AN AFRICAN AMERICAN EXPERIENCE

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: The Welcome Home: An African American Experience toolkit challenges the way ​ ​ African Americans define, enact, and manifest home for ourselves in all spaces and contexts. It explores the multiple facets of black home-creating by engaging our community in discourse, education, and celebration. It dispels preconceived notions about how home should be built and why it's necessary by removing its physical limitations. It provides the tools to create conversational spaces that allow us to reevaluate our relationships with our emotional vulnerabilities, each other, external forces, and collective culture.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: Instructions for the Toolkit Ideally, all parts of the toolkit function in tandem with each other, although each piece can be used individually. Firstly, one participant will read the call to action

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: 35 aloud to their group; this step begins the observance and establishes any space as our home. Then participants will use the discussion cards and videos to begin a necessary discourse about the function of the black home, the totality of black life, and our culture. Lastly, participants will use the audio playlists and recipe book to celebrate our food, music, and dance.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: Discourse/ Education Segment This discourse space facilitates necessary conversation about the totality of the African American experience, as well as collect anecdotal information about what makes us feel at home. By utilizing the discourse-based artifacts provided in the toolkit, I will engage participants and allow them to share their current stories, without dictating the expectations of the space. In this space, black people are welcome to speak candidly about not only our grievances and traumas, but also the happiness and sanctuary we feel when home. Ideally, the information gathered through this discourse will aid in further defining what is needed to sustain home, inform future observances, and expand the totality and impact of the toolkit.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: The discussion cards prompt participants to evaluate their understanding of what home can provide and why it's necessary.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: The discussion video features audio from a televised interview between Toni Morrison and Charlie Rose. The selected audio clip is intended to feel reminiscent of the conversations African Americans have while convening at the dinner table or in the kitchen and prompt participants to engage in similar discourse. The audio is accompanied by visuals of notable moments in black history, such as the Black Panther movement, Ferguson protest, and Barack Obama's presidency. The imagery shown depicts black progression in America through a multitude of lenses and how it affects our understanding of mobility, self-determination, and suffering. It

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: 37 also explores the varying levels of visibility African Americans experience in mainstream society and our desire for representation, authenticity, and multiplicity in our blackness.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: Celebration Segment Celebration is integral to building the black home because it allows us to acknowledge our achievements, culture, and resilience. In most contexts, we are not afforded the space to freely express admiration for our blackness and collective identity. We are often reminded that our blackness is a hindrance, an obstacle we must overcome to succeed and be represented in the mainstream. African Americans deserve the space to express our enthusiasm, happiness, and pride without judgment or fear of persecution. Specifically, this segment of the toolkit examines how celebrating our food, dance, and music can aid black home-creating.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: Our Food is a recipe book that examines how specific meals are connected to our ​

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: 38 collective culture and understanding of home as African Americans. By surveying my loved ones, I compiled a list of meals most reminiscent of the black home and featured the most popular responses in the cookbook. The photography and recipes featured in the recipe are of my own family and friends.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: Dance & Music Playlists By surveying my family and friends and using my own experiences and intuition, I compiled two audio playlists, Our Music and Our Dance, that are reminiscent of the ​ ​ ​ ​​ ​ black home.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: Our Music is a playlist that features 80 songs from four different genres and reflects ​ the expansive lineage and sonic progression of our music. It showcases music from many regions of the United States and spans many decades. It begins with gospel, the music sang by our ancestors during slavery and grandparents in the church, and ends with recent hip-hop songs. The playlist feels nostalgic and familial, yet current.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: 39 The order of the playlist is as follows: gospel is tracks 1 through 7, soul is tracks 8 through 19, rhythm and blues is tracks 20 through 46, and hiphop is tracks 47 through 80. The playlist can either be listened to in order or on shuffle.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: Similar to Our Music, the dance playlist spans several decades. It showcases a ​ ​ mixture of line-dances, customarily done at our cookouts and family reunions, instructional dance tracks, songs culturally synonymous with particular dances, and videos highlighting various regional forms of black dance.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: CONCLUSION Since beginning at Pratt, I have had this unwavering desire to create work that speaks to, for, and about my community. It is intrinsic to my experience and my very being as a black scholar, student, and designer. Over the last year and a half of research, development, and execution of this thesis, I have used this opportunity to

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: 40 question not only the necessities of home and why it's built, but also my connection to my race, identity, and culture.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: Building the Welcome Home toolkit allowed me to delve more deeply into these ​ ​ inquiries. It explores the dimensionality of the African American perspective and how it affects our home-creating, as it reflects not only my own viewpoints but also those of my loved ones, scholars, and intellectuals. It confronts the current confines associated with home building by removing its physical limitations and establishing home as a collaborative space for discourse, introspection, and celebration among a community.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: However, the black home is multifaceted and far exceeds the current realm of this thesis and capstone project. Although the Welcome Home toolkit addresses many ​ ​ aspects of the black home, I am aware that it will develop and adopt new forms, as my community engages with it. Particularly, the current pandemic has shifted the scope of the toolkit. Ideally, it would be used during home observances held face-to-face and provide immediate insight in person. However, the toolkit can function independently.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: 41 Its artifacts are easily accessible and distributable through the Welcome Home ​ website, as many of them can function digitally and in print. Interested groups or organizations can interact with all parts of the toolkit remotely and transform their current surroundings into a black home by following the outlined instructions. This allows the toolkit to operate under any circumstance and within all realms.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: 42 GLOSSARY Home a familiar or usual setting: congenial environment This first definition of home is the third definition provided by Merriam-Webster. It most closely resembles what home means in the traditional sense.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: Rootlessness a feeling of not having a home to return to In the context of this thesis, rootlessness for the black community results from exclusion and displacement by external forces and social prejudices (racism, classism, bigotry, etc). African Americans can experience rootlessness through several lenses, including spiritually, emotionally, mentally, or physically.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: Displacement the forced movement of people from their locality or environment and occupational activities There are two types of population displacement that relate to this thesis. Direct displacement refers to the displacement of people from their original location (ex. Trans Atlantic Slave Trade). Indirect Displacement refers to the loss of livelihood as a result of being forced to migrate from the home region which one is attached (United Nations Educational, Scientific, and Cultural Organization).

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: 43 Blackness the historical, cultural, and social heritage considered common to black people collectively Defining blackness is difficult because each black person's perception of his or her blackness is different. Moreover, Lerone Bennett defines blackness as "the universe of values and attitudes and orientations which rises, like dew, from the depths of our ancestral experience and pulls us toward the distant shores of our destiny." Although Lerone's definition is correct, for the purpose of this thesis, the above definition best contextualizes the fundamental elements of blackness.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: African Americans descendants of enslaved Africans living in the United States The influx of foreign-born blacks who have emigrated to the United States in recent decades continues to shift what it means to be African American (Forson). However, in order to trace the lineage of African Americans in the United States over the last 400 years, I limited the scope of what it means to be African American with this definition. Collective Identity the shared definition of a group that derives from its members' common interests, experiences, and solidarities In the context of this thesis, the collective identity African American share is

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: 44 fundamental in establishing home. Creating home is not a solitary process, but a collaborative experience that engages every member of the black community.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: Home-creating the process of building necessary spaces for transformative conversations and interactions central to enact home I coined this word and subsequent definition because I feel there is not a preexisting linguistic term to describe my desired design intentions. This definition derives from a traditional definition of placemaking as presented by Robert Steuteville of the Congress of New Urbanism, "the process of creating quality places that people want to live, work, play, and learn in."

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: Wake 1. The conceptual frame of and for living blackness in the diaspora in the still unfolding aftermaths of Atlantic chattel slavery 2. The state of wakefulness; consciousness Both definitions derive from the book, In the Wake. The first definition positions the ​ ​ wake as a larger, more concretized framework, as something black people function within. The second definition defines wake in a more literal context, as something black people must do; a sense of awareness.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: 45 Wake Work imagining new ways to live in and survive the wake of slavery, its afterlives, and the afterlife of property This term derives from Christina Sharpe's book, In the Wake. The book argues that ​ ​ wake work is necessary for black progression, survival, and being.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home
paragraph: Historical Trauma a complex and collective trauma experienced over time and across generations by a group of people who share an identity, affiliation, or circumstance This term was first coined to describe the experienced faced by the children of Holocaust survivors. Within the last two decades, it has been applied to several minority groups, including African Americans.

author: Christian King
thesis_title: Making Space For The Black Home

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu

paragraph: By Hsiao-Wen Hu Bachelor of Design, Commercial Design, National Taiwan University of Science and Technology, June 2017

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
