author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: Craft is a technology that has been passed down through history. It is an art form that communicates ideas, a form of expression of the maker, and a documentation of values, norms, and civilizations of human society.

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: The long-lasting characteristics of crafts enable people to revisit the work and its message over and over again. Because of this physicality and materiality, identities and values can be formed, preserved, and passed on through craft. We learn from studying artifacts, then preserve and exhibit them in museums and cultural institutions for educational purposes as well as community solidarity.

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: Craft has always been a reflection of culture and identity. As our lives transitioned from physical activities to virtual interactions, new needs and forms of engagement were prompted. The culture of screens and digital spaces is disparate from traditional mediums. It loses the intimacy and becomes universal. The gap existing between the physical and virtual experience distance ourselves from engaging. This thesis aims to investigate how identities and values could be retained and expressed through postdigital presentation, looking at the transition between the physical and virtual, material and data, user engagement and experience from the perspective of craft. Through extensive research on craft and preservation, this thesis will explore postdigital preservation through various design projects.

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: To begin this investigation, it is necessary to clarify the definition of craft. Contemporary craft is no longer limited to its physical form and association with nostalgia and romanticism. Merriam-Webster Learner's Dictionary defines craft as an activity, job, or object that involves making in a skillful way by using human hands. The idea of craft is so deeply associated with hand-making that we automatically connect it with traditional techniques or obsolete production methods that require human operation. What I found fundamentally wrong with such a stereotype is that craft comes with history but is not only about the past, but rather its process. What distinguishes crafts from pure technology1 is its requirement for specific skill sets and its unique characteristics that transfer uniqueness from the maker to the product. Sennett defines craftsmen as those who dedicate their time and effort to good work for its own sake." (20-23) This idea can be applied to any profession, such as lab technicians who conduct perfect experiments, conductors who pursue the best performance, or cooks who seek precision in taste. The maker's diligence and caring for the product is what makes an ordinary act extraordinary. To have a satisfying result, even machines also have to run through a series of evaluations and tests to generate the one that meets the criteria. This process of curating and selecting can also be seen as an act of care.

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: Keywords 1. Technology: It refers to modern devices, machinery, and production methods that are automatically calculated and produced by computers, and are widely adopted in the industry in the current digital age.

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: Crafts, including coding, physical computation, and 3D printing, can be done both by humans and machines. Amit Zoran proposes that digital technology is not an enemy against creative making. Instead, it can be a set of design tools that stimulate imaginations and break through the limitation of contemporary making practices, enriching creative thinkers and makers' capabilities of innovation. (384) Digital fabrication can be seen as a method and procedure of making, which does not equal mass automatic production. It simply suggests the involvement of machines and technologies in the creative process in which the product can still retain its own characteristics and innovative features; while mass production pursues quality control on making every product exactly the same.

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: The craftsmanship lies in the construction of the larger system, not necessarily in the individual product. 7 2020 MFA Thesis Hsiao-Wen Hu

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: Additionally, craft is a practice that requires skills from the maker. This idea can be interpreted both from the perspective of makers as humans and makers as machines. For humans, skills can be developed over time by absorbing knowledge, repeating actions, and practicing; and the performance of skills in crafts reflects the value of time, effort, and hard work. When the activity is purely a choice for the sake of the object's own good, it is deeply appreciated and valued. The definition of skills for machines is different. Skills can be interpreted as a highly specific procedure, workflow, or process that is particularly designed and programmed for the making. By running through the flow and process, analyzing collected information, altering possible outcomes, and performing the codes and demands, machines can also "learn" a skill, even though the "learning" process might only be less than seconds. For example, the Drawing Machine created by Damien Borowik is programmed through codes to draw patterns in a certain way. The machine could not do it until it learned the skill of pattern making by running the data given by the artist.

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: One of the most important core concepts of crafts is the demonstration of uniqueness. The process of making must demonstrate creativity, and it can be in any kind of form. Sennett believes that the perfect model done flawlessly in a certain standard by machines is only a proposal instead of a command. The perfect model can be an option or a tool, but it is over-promoted in the age of mass production. (101-104) In other words, if the products are all required to meet a standard, none of them will be special or unique. The variations and individuality are the key values in crafts.

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: Manifesto of Craft Craft, a technique, an art form, a documentation. A reflection of civilization. It includes the past, and it extends to the future. Human hands or machines, that's not relevant anymore. Creativity, skills, and care. That's all it takes.

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: "Is craft safeguarding the past or protecting a different type of future?" (Loewe Foundation) This question strikes the heart of today's practice of creative making. It implies that crafts hold the connection between the past and the future. Alan Crawford adds on this idea in his article "Arts and Crafts Movement": "The Arts and Crafts Movement looked both to the past and to the future, and the objects reflect this. The 'old work' that Arts and Crafts people sketched in the countryside and studied in museums provided them with models and meanings for their designs." (3) Artisans and Designers tended to draw inspiration from the past and follow traditions as a way to preserve meanings. He points out that certain decorative techniques are revived and reapplied on houses such as luster painting and Limoges enameling.

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: Arts and Crafts table designed by William Morris, wood, 1865 (London, Victoria and Albert Museum); photo credit of Victoria and Albert Museum, London/ Art Resource, NY Crafts is believed to be a carrier of time and history, not only simply as an extension of the past, but also as a force to unite people and strengthen communities. Paul Greenhalgh ties crafts with the symbolic meaning of "places." He discusses this idea in his book The Persistence of Craft, stating that "A 'space' filled with such objects has the potential to become a "place." He explains that crafts can be seen as "the consumption and"

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: display of ornamental objects, as a reflection and memorial of our traces", and its ultimate goal is to create meaningful "places." (10) With such "places" constituted by physical crafts, objects, and artifacts with the idea of preservation and permanence, an intangible force that represents the local culture and collective memory emerges, which unites the local with their past, present, and future, as well as their environment. Morehshin Allahyari is an Irian artist, activist, writer, and educator who also conducted a series of 3D modeling and printing series called Material Speculations: ISIS (2015-2016.) Allahyari not only reconstructed 12 selected statues from the Roman period that were destroyed by ISIS in 2015 but embedded a hard drive into each statue which includes videos, maps, images, and information about the original artifacts and the sites. Such a multimedia approach comprehensively preserves different aspects of the culture and enables a more secure future for these artifacts to pass on. Another great example is the rebuilding of the Buddha statues in Bamiyan using 3D light projection. The history of the statues goes back to the 6th century, but it ended in 2001 March when the Taliban leader Mullah Mohammed Omar ordered their destruction. In 2015, a Chinese couple Janson Yu and Liyan Hu offered a gift to the local community — a 3D light

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: Morehshin Allahyari: Material Speculations: ISIS (2015-2016)

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: projection of the giant Buddha on the destroyed site. It brought huge relief to the local residents and enabled them to once again embrace their religious treasure and beliefs.

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: A 3D light projection of a destroyed Buddha in Bamiyan, Afghanistan. Jim Huylebroek for The New York Times.

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: Such force can be extremely potent not only in defining common cultural roots and identities but also empowering and thriving the local social and economic development. During the Depression in the 1930s, craft has been a way to alleviate rural poverty, according to Imogen Racz's Contemporary Crafts. As the economy at the time collapsed and people lost faith, craft was a solution for lifting the rural economy by empowering the local communities to learn and produce products on their own. In another article, "Preservation of Cultural Heritage Embodied in Traditional Crafts in the Developing Countries: A Case Study of Pakistani Handicraft Industry," researchers from China also 12 2020 MFA Thesis Hsiao-Wen Hu

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: support a similar idea. They studied the rich history and heritage of crafts in Pakistan. "The handicraft industry is significantly contributing to income and employment generation as well as the economic and social sustainability of the country. The recent statistics indicate that as much as 13.54% of all employees nationally are accounted for in the crafts and related services sector alone," according to their research.

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: As there are museums and libraries for crafts in physical forms, there are also digital archives to store data and digital crafts. Artefacts2 possess a variety of properties and formats, including GIFs, videos, audios, websites, software, and user experience. These artefacts and products as communication mediums possess characteristics similar to the way physical crafts do, offering experience and interaction different from physical crafts. To preserve the data and its circulating nature, the online archive is considered one of the most efficient and accessible ways.

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: Data Diary is a speculative personal archive for keeping track of personal data, resembling the idea of a diary.

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: One of the most popular and widely circulated formats of artefacts is memes. Taking images, news, videos, phrases, and slams from all over the world and compressing

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: Keywords 2. Artefact: This term refers to the data and work of codes in the book Significant Properties of Software (2008). In this thesis, its definition is broadened to also include the overall digital forms of work, including memes, videos, gifs, websites...etc.

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: them into a single GIF or image, the memes became a specific use of sarcastic and jokes about everyday life, as well as critical responses to certain topics in current days. The website Know Your Meme, founded in December of 2008, has documented all these memes, categorizing them based on topics and formats, providing context and history. The Mini Keanu Reeves is a popular meme in which the actor's body is disproportionately short and accompanied by different annotations such as "Me waiting for my mum to stop fighting with dad so I can tell her I ate the lemon-scented soap." The website tracks the image back to Microsoft's E3 2019 Xbox press conference of a video game called Cyberpunk 2077. A Twitter user @KojiMads released this picture on the same day and it started to go viral. In the following days, the image was re-edited and reposted on different platforms. The website listed the history of different versions of the meme and their creators. Know Your Meme is considered a digital archive that preserves, chronicles and documents memes, enabling us to trace back any particular iteration and learn about its background, cultural references and impact.

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: Wayback Machine is a repository of websites' configurations that automatically detects and captures their changes through its own algorithm. It provides a timeline of every version of a website and allows users to view them. Take Pratt Institute's website for example. Analysing the history of its website enables us to see the transformation in branding, positioning, and communication the institute portrays to its audience throughout time. Wayback Machine allows access to every revision of Pratt's website from 1996 to 2019 and

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: organizes them chronically in incredible detail with their dates, time, and content revised. The entire process of making, editing, and iterating is documented and preserved. This is an extraordinary aspect of digital artefacts as usually only the final outcome of an object is ever presented to the audience.

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: Pratt Institute's website on Wayback Machine on 2/8/1997 and 4/8/2020.

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: The Internet has allowed people to access and share open-source projects and enabled users to collect and store data voluntarily. One of the most popular open-source platforms for preserving and sharing is GitHub, a powerful online archive to study, review,

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: and share codes. Even the codes for launching the spaceship of the first Lunar Landing Mission Apollo 11 in 1969 can be found there.

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: Buzz Aldrin on the Moon as photographed by Neil Armstrong (Armstrong seen in the visor reflection) Wikipedia Apollo 11.

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: The study of heritage preservation analyzes the artifact's properties and qualities with certain criteria to examine its value. "Understanding and articulating the values and significance of the heritage are crucial for statutory designation and regulation for the development of protection and management policies," according to The Guidelines on Cultural Heritage which was implemented by the Council of Europe. (51) The team suggests four main categories to identify the heritage significance: Historic significance, Aesthetic Significance, Scientific or Research Significance, and Social and Spiritual Significance, which can be seen as the heritage's contribution to society. To further investigate the value of the heritage and broaden the evaluation of the asset, there are other three comparative criteria on top of the previous four: Intrinsic Significance, Contextual Significance, and Associative Significance. These are more specific in discovering the heritage's own property and quality within the same category. (51-54)

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: One the other hand, the study of data preservation takes on a different approach. Unlike the preservation for the materials, data preservation is free from the imitation of storage spaces and the amount of artifacts. Information could be compressed into microscopic units that take far less space and maintenance than papers or other physical forms of preservation. However, it is still almost impossible to preserve everything in digital. The problem here is not necessarily the quantity of the information, but the efficiency. The composition of digital artefacts is a network interconnected with different sets of data that can only be accessed through their own specific platforms, which complicates the situation from preserving one artefacts to preserving several massive networks and systems. There is growing research about how to sustainably store and manage data, emphasizing the different levels of preservation. The Software Sustainability Institute proposes the "Sustainability and Preservation Framework" to analyze different types of data and apply preservation methods accordingly. There are seven levels of preservation and sustainability: Technical Preservation (techno-centric), Emulation (data-centric), Migration (functionality- centric), Cultivation (process-centric), Hibernation (knowledge-centric), Deprecation, and Procrastination. (2-5) These concepts are more in-depth discussed in Significant Properties of Software (2008.) The book introduces the study of Digital Curation, which mainly focuses on the caring and preservation of the artefacts to ensure its "replayability3" and usefulness in the future. (9) Compared to the study of heritage preservation that selects the most valuable among all the work, data preservation selects the most important function within individual work.

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: Keywords 3. Replayability: A software or a digital platform's ability to fully perform the feature of the data and demonstrate the complete user experience that is incorporated in the data (Significant Properties of Software, 2008.) 19 2020 MFA Thesis Hsiao-Wen Hu

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: Living through the early information age4, we redefined old standards as our expectations for technology soared. Online shopping and delivery become dominant; online banking is considered more accessible; facial or fingerprint recognition is replacing passwords. This transition is obvious as Carruthers quoted Tome Goodwin: "We've taken what we knew before and pulled it through a digital frame." Carruthers explains further with examples of e-commerce replacing catalogues, Instagram adds updating printed ads, and celebrity endorsement transforming into influencer marketing. It is safe to say that a huge part of human civilization is digitized, and data is no doubt becoming the most precious asset to the market as well as each individual. However, the gap between physical activities and virtual interactivity is expected. This gap can be seen as the distance between human eyes and the screen, the control panel and the data, the code in the system and the well- designed interface. As we learn to control information through screens and complex systems, we distance ourselves from the scene. The culture of screens and digital spaces is disparate from traditional mediums. Unlike books, we could not fold, rip, flip, or make marks on the screen. But in order to access and interact with the complex digital world, we developed universal gestures and actions around the use of devices. We scroll all pages up and down and we swipe interfaces left and right. Our engagement with these tools is no longer personal or intimate, but universal, just like how the devices are mass produced. With

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: Keywords 4. Information age: It refers to a period beginning in the late 20th century and characterized by the rapid shift from traditional industry to an economy primarily based upon information technology. (Wikipedia) It is regarded as a modern age in which information has become a commodity that is quickly and widely disseminated and easily available especially through the use of computer technology. (Merriam-Webster Dictionary) 22 2020 MFA Thesis Hsiao-Wen Hu

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: this new dominant medium, how do we preserve identities? How do crafts continue to document modern culture and values?

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: Bringing in the study of crafts and preservation inspires a postdigital5 approach to preserve culture and identity in the information age. Postdigital is a term proposed by Melvin L. Alexenberg, defined as an attitude that emphases being human rather than being digital in artistic practice, concerning our dynamic relationships with digital technology and art forms. (9-10) Instead of looking at data and codes of digital artefacts, this investigation is more concerned with the intimacy between the data and the user, retaining the current digital culture in a more human and individualized way.

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: To explore how data could connect with our surroundings, I tried to visualize these layers of information through photography. The project "Datafication Nancy" compiled my personal data from five different digital platforms: Facebook, Instagram, Youtube, Spotify, and Pinterest. These platforms retain the user's personal information and preference in order to make suggestions through their own algorithms. And since these platforms all have their own characteristics and culture, the data they have on me are also different segments of me. For example, I used to post and share links on Facebook when I was in high school, but then I switched to Instagram when I was in college and started to post only well-taken photos. In these two platforms, the personality of my accounts are entirely different. As a

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: Keywords 5. Postdigital: Of or pertaining art forms that address the humanization of digital technologies through interplay between digital, biological, cultural, and spiritual systems, between cyberspace and real space, between embodied media and mixed reality in social and physical communication, between high tech and high touch experiences, between visual, haptic, auditory, and kinesthetic media experiences… (Alexenberg, 10) 23 2020 MFA Thesis Hsiao-Wen Hu

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: result, their assumptions of me actually look like two strangers. Based on the data that I input in these platforms and the suggestions given by these algorithms, I created five different personas reflecting the different assumptions of me. The personality of the persona is revealed through the settings of the photographs and the appearance of the characters. This project delicately manifests the data and its context.

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: Facebook Nancy / Instagram Nancy / Youtube Nancy / Spotify Nancy / Pinterest Nancy Learn more: https://nancyhuhw.com/Datafication-Nancy In another experiment, I attempted to communicate information through the recreation of texture. Textures are easily associated with objects when a context is given. In this project, the context is the supermarket. This project is to test how people interpret these textures based on their own experience. The participants were asked to interact with a

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: texture panel with their eyes closed and tried to guess what food in the supermarket that these textures represent. I chose five common things that are easy to find in the store: raw meat, vegetable, pasta, rice, and bottled water (from left to right). In this exercise, participants can only rely on the context I provided and the textures they interacted with. Without their vision, the only reference they had was the association between their own experience and the texture, and it varied when each person had a different background, preference, habit, and lifestyle. For example, one of them was not a big fan of meat, therefore she could not connect the texture of the water bag with raw meat. Another participant also could not recognize the water bottle lid and thought it was some kind of candy. The outcome of this exercise was interesting as each participant interpreted the information differently based on their own lifestyles and preferences. Selecting and feeling the texture of food when shopping for groceries is an ordinary thing to do and people might not even realize it, but such experience is unique every time and could not be replaced by online grocery shopping.

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: Craft is a medium that could record processes and replay them. Through the creative making process, stories could be told in a more engaging and personalized way. This project aims to materialize data, transferring digital existences into interactive artifacts that manifest itself as a story instead of flat data. I developed a toolkit that contains nine different materials and a canvas. The project consists of two parts: first, I sent out a survey to nine participants, obtaining baseline information about the preservation of their digital

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: existences. I then asked six of them to use the toolkit that I prepared to materialize their identities, specifically, their Instagram profiles, as if it is a physical artifact that would tell the story of them in the future. The result of this project shows how the process of making and interacting could unfold a narrative and add depth to flat data, communicating visions that could not be revealed solely by looking at Instagram profiles.

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: Looking at human history at large, the gestures developed for the use of digital tools and devices is universal to current generations while unique to this information age. Before the dominance of screen and haptic technology, to "swipe" a page might not make much sense. To bring such a small daily interactivity into an experience unique to the current digital age, these universal gestures are identified as a special culture commonly shared with generations in this information age. By embodying these actions into a physical form, they stop being a by-product of mass production, but interactive artifact that tells a more vivid story of our engagement with the digital world. In response to my research on postdigital preservation, I developed a series of machinery that specifically preserve the interactivity and engagement of digital tools. I designed five wooden devices simulating the actions that we would conduct on our digital devices: draw, navigate, scroll, zoom, and delete, seeing these artifacts as a time capsule of our unique digital culture.

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: Simulator #1: draw. This first piece of machine simulates the experience of drawing on the computer with a mouse. Users hold the pen with the machine the way they hold a mouse. The machine is designed to draw straight lines, mimicking the same feeling of control.

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: Simulator #2: navigate. This piece is mimicking the experience of navigating the cursor with the touch pad on laptops. It is inspired by the feature on Apple's MacBook, in which the cursor would enlarge when users rapidly rubbing the touch pad trying to locate it on the screen.

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: Simulator #3: scroll. This necklace-like piece is commenting on the situation where people are constantly on their phone and fixed to this position where ever they go. This embodiment of scrolling is trying to identify the heavy reliance on mobile devices and bring awareness to it. 28 2020 MFA Thesis Hsiao-Wen Hu

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: Simulator #4: zoom in / out. This is a magnifier referencing the experience of zooming in and out on mobile devices. There is a magnifying lens attaching at the back, which allows users to actually "zoom in" on things with this magnifier when then open the gate.

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: Simulator #5: Delete. Deleting is an action only exists in digital spaces, making things disappear in one press. Unlike throwing, cutting, or any other verbs that we use in real world to "make things disappear," to delete is not to perform a process of removing something, but a simple and instant command. This machine is designed to exaggerate that feeling of being in control, recreating the experience of making something disappear in just one press.

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: Craft is ubiquitous; it is recording the history and fermenting human civilization. Contemporary craft is not to be defined solely by its form or material, but rather includes all kinds of study performed with an attitude of dedicating and mastering. The superior ability of craft to preserve civilizations reveals the strong connection between humans and their surroundings including tools, materials, and social norms. Through craft, these values and culture are naturally manifested in the most authentic way.

author: Hsiao-Wen Hu
thesis_title: Craft as Time Capsule: Postdigital Preservation
paragraph: Looking at the current information age from the lens of craft, it becomes clear that our engagement with tools and technology is taking over a huge part of our interaction with surroundings. Expanding craft as a postdigital preservation methodology, the goal is to bridge the gap between the real world and the digital world, shortening the distance between the physical and the virtual. By translating data and information into a more engaging work of craft, the unique digital culture could be preserved and further examined. Craft as a time capsule not only capture the present, but also reaches back to the past and extends to the future.

author: Erli Wang
paragraph: THE COLLABORATION BETWEEN ARTISTS OR DESIGNERS AND ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE

author: Erli Wang
paragraph: by Erli Wang [Bachelor of Fine Arts, Digital Art Design] [College of Fine Arts of Shanghai University] [Shanghai University] [June 2008]

author: Erli Wang
paragraph: © 2020 Erli Wang ​​ A thesis submitted in partial fulfillment of the requirements for the degree of Master of Fine Arts, Communications Design School of Design Pratt Institute May 2020

author: Erli Wang
paragraph: THE COLLABORATION BETWEEN ARTISTS OR DESIGNERS AND ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE

author: Erli Wang
paragraph: Received and approved: ________________________________________________ Lead Thesis Faculty Gaia Hwang ________________________________________________ Chairperson Santiago Piedrafita, May 2020 ________________________________________________ MFA Thesis Candidate Erli Wang, May 2020

author: Erli Wang
paragraph: What new possibilities in collaborative relationships between designers or artists might arise in the future? How might Machine Learninggenerate a collaborative relationship with artists or designers in the future?

author: Erli Wang
paragraph: Artificial intelligence has recently started to actively participate in the creative production of art and design, in music, architecture, and fine arts. Machine Learning “is a method of data analysis that automates analytical model building,”(Arin, 2019) and the most significant part of Machine Intelligence. Due to the development of Machine Learning, Artificial Intelligence can now help artists and designers generate some concepts and visuals that were unthinkable. Furthermore, Machine Intelligence is gradually transforming its collaborative relationship with artists or designers. This research project discusses how Machine Intelligence can play a prominent role in a collaborative relationship with artists and designers. This research speculates different possibilities for cooperation between innovators and Machine Intelligence according to their distinctive advantages in the creative process.

author: Erli Wang
paragraph: Collaboration, cooperation, help, creative, Machine Intelligence, Artificial Intelligence, Machine Learning, designer, artists, help, support, provide, template, idea, innovation;

author: Erli Wang
paragraph: Collaboration: the action of working with someone to produce or create something ​ 1. Machine learning: Machine Learning(ML) is the scientific study of algorithms and ​ ​ ​ ​ ​ ​ ​ ​ statistical models that computer systems use to perform a specific task without using ​ ​ ​ explicit instructions, relying on patterns and inference instead. It is seen as a subset of ​ ​ artificial intelligence. ​ 2. Artists: An artist is a person engaged in an activity related to creating art, practicing the ​ ​ arts, or performing art. ​ 3. Designer: A designer is a person who makes designs for objects. A fashion designer ​ ​ ​ ​ ​ designs clothing, a web designer designs web pages, and an automobile designer designs ​ ​ ​ ​ automobiles. 4. template: a shaped piece of metal, wood, card, plastic, or other material used as a pattern for processes such as painting, cutting out shapes, or drilling. Creativity: Creativity is the act of turning new and imaginative ideas into reality. 5. ​ ​ Creativity is characterized by the ability to perceive the world in new ways, to find ​

author: Erli Wang
paragraph: 5 hidden patterns, to make connections between seemingly unrelated phenomena, and to generate solutions. 6. Innovation: a new idea, method, or device ​ 7. Collaboration: artists work jointly with Artificial Intelligence or together especially in an intellectual endeavor. 8. Share: to partake of, use, experience, occupy, or enjoy with others; 9. Participation: The process during which Artificial Intelligence are consulted about or ​ have the opportunity to become actively involved in a project or program of activity. 10.Communication: a process by which information is exchanged between Artificial ​ Intelligence through a common system of symbols, signs, or behavior. ​

author: Erli Wang
paragraph: Artificial Intelligence’s influence on the art and design community has developed tremendously: it has gradually carved a space of collaboration with artists and designers. Now, it is the right time to explore what role Machine Intelligence will play in the future of artistic production, especially in light of the development of “a subset of A.I. that provides systems the ability to learn and improve from experience without being explicitly programmed. It deals with algorithms that can look at data to learn from it and make a decision.” (Arin, 2019) Theorizing Machine Intelligence In the 1980s, Machine Learning was still in the fictional imagination of philosophers, filmmakers, mathematicians, and scientists. Interestingly, they also develop significant theories

author: Erli Wang
paragraph: 6 that would influence their future. In 1936, Alan Turing created the Turing Machine, a device consisting of tape, an infinite line of cells, and a head, an active element that moves along it. The Turing Machine is an abstract device that simulates logic and tests theoretical ideas about the potential of a computer. Turing speculated how a machine might decipher and execute a set of instructions. As a result, his theory is regarded as the foundation of Machine Intelligence. These sophisticated machines still remain in the theoretical realm and have not been implemented: they are a representative example in the theoretical stage of Machine Learning research. From Theory to Reality Half a century later, Machine Intelligence developed significantly within the effort to convert theory into practice: many experts tried their best to realize what was once science fiction. According to the “A history of Artificial Intelligence,” John McCarthy launched project MAC, later named the MIT Artificial Intelligence Lab in 1963. Its main research focus included cognition, computer vision, decision theory, distributed systems, machine learning, multi-agent systems, Neural Networks, probabilistic inference, and robotics. Some years later, McCarthy and Marvin Minskey launched SAIL, the Stanford Artificial Intelligence Laboratory. This institute would pave the way for operating systems, artificial intelligence, and the theory of computation (Ather). This research contributed to cognition, computer vision, decision theory, distributed systems, machine learning, multi-agent systems, Neural Networks, probabilistic inference, and robotics.

author: Erli Wang
paragraph: 7 Neural Networks “Neural Networks are algorithms used to detect information and conclusions from large sets of data by recognizing underlying relationships in sets of data the same way a human brain does. They hold a tremendous amount of potential for deep learning, part of a broader family of Machine Learning methods based on learning data representations, as opposed to task-specific algorithms. Neural networks and deep learning are now computationally feasible due to GPUs. It shows unbeatable power on complex prediction problems that have very high dimensionality and millions-billions of samples” (Ather, “A history of Artificial Intelligence”). A Neural Network is designed to solve particular classes of problems. For example, it helps a machine to recognize a specific human face because the machine cannot distinguish among different faces. As a result, a Neural network is able to model many potential faces to recognize them efficiently. Neural Networks are an essential part of Machine Learning and a system modeled on the human nervous system.

author: Erli Wang
paragraph: Modern Machine Intelligence Machine Intelligence has recently moved out of the lab and entered our lives with applications across industries. Even more significantly it began to challenge human intelligence and abilities. In 2011, IBM’s Watson utilized Machine Learning to analyze natural language and defeated Brad Rutter and Ken Jennings, two former champions in a televised match. “Watson used over 100 different methods of reasoning, including analyzing language and investigating hypotheses. During the whole process, Watson utilized over 100 different methods of reasoning, such as analyzing language and investigating hypotheses.” (Ather) Although it is not a perfect player,

author: Erli Wang
paragraph: 8 IBM’s Watson did manage to outwit the stronger human players. In these years, Watson gained the ability to read and interpret information. This case proves that now A.I. can surpass human intelligence in a particular professional field. Therefore, A.I. has the ability to cooperate with humans in complex tasks. This ability is paving the way for a new level of collaboration between artists and A.I. Machine Intelligence has begun to actively participate in a large number of creative activities, such as painting, composing music, and architecture. Even more significant, it also stimulates and inspires artists' imaginations, as well as helps them explore new and distinctive outcomes. Collaboration between Artists and Artificial Intelligence might be an inevitable trend that would shape the future. Machine Intelligence supports the development of innovative virtual platforms for innovators, where they can make full use of their imaginations and insights to create artwork. Machine Intelligence might become an "art translator," which converts artists' or designers' ideas into a different form. For instance, it may translate data into sculptures, installations, or music using different algorithms. From a communication point of view, it is possible for Artificial Intelligence to now directly "read" and visualize artists' and designers' thoughts through an implanted chip. In the future, different modes of collaboration might become available.

author: Erli Wang
paragraph: Voice Recognition Voice assistant or voice agent is one of the most significant branches of A.I. and it presents the most widely available interaction between humans and A.I.

author: Erli Wang
paragraph: 9 In 1971, the Defense a Research Projects organization provided funding for a five-year speech recognition project at Carnegie Mellon University. They created Harpy in 1976. The machine had a 1,011 words vocabulary and could also understand entire phrases. Meanwhile, this device added a new feedback function. When it could not comprehend the speaker, Harpy would return an "I do not know what you said, please repeat." Moreover, in 1986, Tangora made an upgrade of the voice agent, and it connected to a typewriter. “Tangora recognized approximately 20,000 words and the processed speech by predicting the most likely result based on what it had interpreted thus far” (Creative, “A brief ​ history of voice assistants”). However, it could not automatically recognize the distinct accent of ​ the users. In 1997, Dragon's company developed NaturallySpeaking software. “Consumers had broader access to both personal computers and speech recognition tech in the 1990s. Dragon's 1997 NaturallySpeaking software could recognize and transcribe natural human speech — users did not have to pause between each word — into a digital document at a rate of 100 words per minute. The program cost $695 to purchase, which made it "affordable" relative to earlier speech recognition devices. A version of NaturallySpeaking is still available for download” (Creative, “A brief history of voice assistants”). It is important to note that humans started to interact with ​ Machine Intelligence through natural human speech software at first. Voice assistant technology is an excellent example of communication and cooperation between humans and A.I. During the whole stage, the voice assistant has changed the machine's role from passive to proactive.

author: Erli Wang
paragraph: 10 Machine-Human Collaboration “NSynth Super, “an experimental physical interface for the NSynth algorithm” (“What is Nsyth super”), is a representative example of a machine in the role of collaborator. A musician has an opportunity to probe more than 100,000 sounds generated with the NSynth algorithm. Google developed NSynth Super in order to study how Machine Learning can help artists or designers create art and music. NSynth quickly learns and captures the unique qualities of original sounds by utilizing a deep Neural Network to create new sounds mimicking these properties. In other words, it can generate new sounds based on the features of existing sounds. As a result, using the dials, musicians can obtain the source sounds they want to explore. Interestingly, they explore the new sounds that mix the acoustic qualities of the four source sounds by moving their finger across the touchscreen. This device, beyond pure stimulation, is developing a cooperative action between artists and machines. Meanwhile, A.I. changes their relationship with the artist to be more of a creative partner, instead of merely a tool for mimicking their behaviors.

author: Erli Wang
paragraph: Conclusion: Machine Learning has now more opportunities to collaborate with artists and designers. A.I. not only provides artists with more choices and templates to choose from but also stimulate and inspire artists who want to explore new and deep forms, patterns, and visuals.

author: Erli Wang
paragraph: This thesis is an inquiry into what may be new possibilities of collaboration between designers or artists and Machine Intelligence in the future. How might Machine Intelligence generate a collaborative relationship with artists or designers? This thesis attempts to speculate on the possible ways artists and A.I. can collaborate. It focuses on a variety of cooperation methods between humans and machines and how to develop their creativity, respectively. Ethical issues, such as fairness, transparency, trust, accountability, and morality are not addressed. For example, after a piece of artwork is created by artists and Artificial Intelligence, who should hold the copyright of it? This thesis employs different research methods including case studies from some existing A.I. based artworks and classic plots from scientific fictional films like Star Trek and Star Wars, surveys, and interviews with selected artists from Pratt Institute and other art studios. By performing in-person meetings and conducting interviews, I was able to collect detailed information. The thesis prior findings discuss some different rates and ways of cooperation. Some learnings are shared in the table below.

author: Erli Wang
paragraph: - Rules-based Game This rule-based game allows participants to create patterns: the participant must draw one solid line connecting two dots each turn. The primary goal of the game is to explore how artists and machines interpret the rule. I invited several students at Pratt Institute to participate in this game and recorded the results. I also employed P5.js to create a similar Machine Learning tool able to perform the same task. The outcomes generated by humans and A.I. are obviously different. The following differences are listed below. - The time it took to complete the task was shorter when participants were machines. - The patterns created by humans are distinguishable from those generated by machines.

author: Erli Wang
paragraph: 13 Therefore, the combination of the advantages and strengths between humans and machines can create an unimaginable and effective future.

author: Erli Wang
paragraph: Fig. 1 The patterns created by the collaboration among humans (2020). Photograph.

author: Erli Wang
paragraph: 14 Fig. 2 The patterns created by the collaboration between humans and machines (2020). Photograph. - AR and VR The participants could use their smartphone to scan a poster I designed. AR-Tech generated additional information and animations exploring the idea of human machines collaboration through the screen of the phone.

author: Erli Wang
paragraph: 15 Fig. 3 The collaboration between Artists and A.I. (2020), AR Video. 1920x1080. - Transformation design response I designed a cultural probe to collect data on what artists and designers think about collaborating with A.I. The research involved a set of specific activities given to a group of participants followed by a series of interviews aiming to generate insights on the perception of the future of human-machine collaboration. - Create an experimental interactive platform to simulate future collaboration with Artificial Intelligence This interactive platform I created relies on Machine Learning, “an application of A.I. that provides systems the ability to learn and improve from experience without being explicitly programmed automatically.”(Bennett 96) The purpose of the project is to mimic future cooperation between artists and machines to create artwork. During the creation process, they kept communicating with one another and developed their creativity on their own, like empathy by artists and algorithms for A.I.

author: Erli Wang
paragraph: 16 Fig. 4 The new collaboration (2020). Installation. 1024x768.

author: Erli Wang
paragraph: 17 Contribution to the discipline Machine Learning “is a subset of A.I. that provides systems the ability to learn and improve from experience without being explicitly programmed. It deals with algorithms that can look at data to learn from it and make a decision.” Machine Intelligence has been participating in creative art activities and built up more and more connections with artists and designers. The cooperative relationship between them also conceives several changes during the creative process. In the book Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning as Creative Problem-solving, Tomas. R. Kerlin discusses some possible methods of Machine Learning as creative problem-solving. The author claims the definition of creativity is quite a difficult problem. During the research of creativity, they encounter two common issues, including the difficulty of defining creativity and the creative concept of the machine (Kelin; 197). Boden and Colton and Wiggins paid more attention to the final product so that they focus on the result of creativity. On the contrary, researchers turn to the creative process, such as “confirming the value of a product and identifying how it was produced” (Kelin; 198). Wiggin thinks creativity is the process of searching (Kelin, 198). Also, he stated, “Creative systems framework” is a tangible search method to analyze the process of creativity. This searching method involves multi-level and multi-dimensional searching to achieve exploratory creativity. In other words, this method can present the essence of computing creation. When the scientists were studying human creativity, they found insight played an essential impact on “the cognitive processes in human creativity, at the stage of generating new ideas or behaviors,” and also said that “insight is the understanding of original, illuminating solutions to

author: Erli Wang
paragraph: 18 problems.” (Kelin 199). They also define problem-solving as “transforming a given situation into a desired one” and the process of human problem-solving is the process of searching. Experts combine problem-solving with insights. They infer that “the phenomenon of insight in below（Kelin problem-solving” has some characteristics 205). 1. Search in a problem space 2. Consistent failure (impasse) 3. Restructuring, and solution and significant progress 4. Test of perceived solution (Kelin; 202) Moreover, the author mentions that “The restructuring process is the key to understanding insight” because it can obtain some new information, instead of repeating the original information in the process of problem-solving and the author claims “insight depends on the experience, rather than rejecting the previous experience” (Kelin 206). Initially, innovators tend to utilize Machine Learning to understand and replicate existing art. As a tool Machine Intelligence mimics and remixes the styles of a piece of artwork: it collects and analyzes specific characteristics, then recombines and applies them to another artifact. The artists took advantage of the particular algorithm of A.I. to create some unexpected results, through different media, painting, video, and music for example. Although imitative art is easily appreciable, even providing for substantial commercial value, it is generated through a passive assistant relationship, since the completed tasks depend on the artist's requirements.

author: Erli Wang
paragraph: 19 Thanks to the advancement of the functionality of Machine Intelligence, the stage beyond imitation is now possible: we are in the era of collaboration between machine and human. In this case, Machine Intelligence does not straightforwardly complete the tasks artists gave it but is ​ offered more space and choices to achieve a final goal collaboratively. Even more ​ significantly, Machine Intelligence takes a proactive part in the entire creative process and shares information and outcomes with the creators. A.I. can create elements of the artwork artists cannot complete. As a result, the machine is now a liable partner instead of a tool for fulfilling repetitive work.

author: Erli Wang

author: Kelly Waldron
paragraph: ● How does place and daily rituals affect our identity? ● How can Design help us redefine our cultural view of mothering and caregiving?

author: Kelly Waldron
paragraph: Waldron Mothering & Caregiving Spring 2020 1

author: Kelly Waldron
paragraph: This thesis analyzes the relationship between mothering and caregiving through place, identity and ritual. I am interested in how created communities shape the roles of mothers and caregivers. I dissect and deconstruct mothering and caregiving by investigating the topics through the three lenses; place, identity, and ritual. New York City Ballroom culture is an example of a microcosm that has created their own family units and community by challenging our relationship with ritual place and identity. I look toward the ballroom culture to challenge the contemporary dialogue on how Western society defines mothering. The House Mother’s role mixes traditional mothering values with the freedom to care, or mother, as one chooses. The use of ritual, the importance of place, and the construction of identity solidifies the feeling of belonging that has allowed this community to thrive since the eighties to reach a broad audience within popular culture. The societal roles placed on women and girls throughout mainstream society are transformed among this self-designed community. What if we took inspiration from the House Mothers’ strength, independence, and creativity to redesign the meaning of mothering to pass on to our future generations?

author: Kelly Waldron
paragraph: Waldron Mothering & Caregiving Spring 2020 2

author: Kelly Waldron
paragraph: Mothers have been viewed as an entity to be controlled and governed throughout history. The American cultural system has historically set unrealistic cultural standards for mothers to uphold. Forty percent of American families rely primarily on a mother’s paycheck. Seventy percent of women who work outside of the home are mothers with children under 18 years old. One in four mothers is raising their children on their own as a single mom. Currently, in the US, Americans do not place a high value on the act of mothering. We view mothering as free labor, a natural ability of women, and a given responsibility. Accordingly, mothering continues to be stigmatized, stereotyped, and pushed onto the women and girls in our communities. Comparatively, Ballroom House mothers throughout the US have created a self-designed mothering system which they base off of the sense of place, identity, and ritual. They look toward the traditional nuclear family unit for guidance on their roles and responsibilities. The societal roles placed on women and girls throughout mainstream society are transformed among this self-designed community. What if we took inspiration from the House Mothers’ strength, independence, and creativity to design a new system of Mothering to pass on to our future generations?

author: Kelly Waldron
paragraph: Waldron Mothering & Caregiving Spring 2020 3

author: Kelly Waldron
paragraph: Mothering has been a part of our genetic map for millennia. Our ancestors practiced their form of mothering while concentrating on survival and balancing social and loving relationships within their communities. While M​ othering​ is an act of doing and owned primarily by women in a community, M​ otherhood​ is a system created by a patriarchal society in order to control women and children. Contemporary humans started to evolve only 200,000 years ago. For most of the human existence mothering and caregiving were practiced and shared by all community members. Mothers and fathers shared roles in rearing children and mothers defined their own caregiving roles. Strongly defined gender and parental roles manifest in society only recently in our history. Before the 20th century in European and early American colonial societies cultural standards were very different. “Women, and especially mothers, were seen as subservient, which Puritans found justification in the Bible. I​ t was in this context that the Anglo American family was born, an island unto itself beholden to no one and reliant on nothing but its own labor.​ The nuclear family had been a dominant structure in England for several centuries, and the Puritans particularly embraced it in order to ensure that men had total control over their wives and children, whose salvation was their Waldron Mothering & Caregiving Spring 2020 4 responsibility. That idea-persists today, the rotten foundation on which built.”​21 modern American parenting stress is “Under both English and colonial law, men ruled their wives, children, and any servants. Thanks to the Old World law “couverture,” married women had no legal identity of their own nor any economic status; they had no claim to property or money, even if they had brought it into the marriage or earned it while married, and no legal rights to their children. Married women could not sign contracts or make wills, and they were not allowed to inherit their husband’s money should he die, either. The most a widow could inherit was one-third of her husband’s estate, which would be controlled, naturally, by her son if she had one. Colonial American families tended to be very large because there was nothing but an upside to having more children in the New World, and a woman’s value was a function of her fertility​. The average colonial American white woman spent twenty-five years pregnant and nursing babies.”2​ 1 Colonial American white mothering was not the only system present in the American colonies at this time. Native Americans, of course, had been practicing mothering for thousands of years before the arrival of Europeans. The majority of communities promoted gender equality. Because women managed the interior networks of their communities; they worked the land, owned the family homes and household goods, Waldron Mothering & Caregiving Spring 2020 5 and reared children; women held important political, social, and economic power. Another influential mothering culture arrived with the first slave ship from Africa in 1619. Enslaved women created their own form of mothering out of the need for survival. Both of these cultures were deemed as u​ n-American, non-European, or uncivilized;​ in reality, these cultural groups had systems in place that supported working women, working mothers, and their families.

paragraph: Waldron Mothering & Caregiving Spring 2020 8 We have a long history in this country of asking mothers, in particular, to perform a specific role, and then placing them within a legal, economic, and political system that makes that role impossible to adequately perform. “During periods of history when the role of “mother” is given power, as in the case of republican motherhood (the notion that mothers are responsible for the moral fiber of a nation, that they alone have the power to create and nurture great new citizens), it often means that the individual woman is freedoms.”​21 stripped of certain rights and In F​ orget Having It All,​ Amy Westervelt explains that during the time in American history where everyone from Benjamin Franklin to the popular press was telling mothers it was their job, ​nay their duty,​ to raise virtuous citizens, our forefathers were creating a legal system that gave women no rights to protect their children, physically, or financially. African American mothers, meanwhile, had no claim at all to their children and had to bear the unique torture of birthing children into a 2​ 1 world of abject horror.

author: Kelly Waldron
paragraph: Waldron Mothering & Caregiving Spring 2020 9 It is important to recognize the differences in mothering between races through American History. Mothering for white colonial women was a much different experience than enslaved women and Native American women. During this time the idea and system of motherhood we are familiar with was enforced. New patterns in adult lives were altering the architecture of the public and private spheres. The separation of work and home life was spurred by the Industrial Revolution, as factory jobs drew men and women away from industries.​11 farms and other cottage The authors of a new category of women’s literature, the domestic guide, emphasized the need to separate public life, private life, and women’s work within the house. The home, now a place of respite from work, also benefited from new, cheaper industrial goods and an economy in which people had the money to buy ready-made furniture, clothes, and toys. paragraph: 21​st​ By the century, our houses and desire for where to live have morphed significantly. Colonial German and English settlers built houses with two to three rooms. The rooms were referred to commonly as the hall and parlor. By the 19th century homes expanded to attic areas for children and servants and later nurseries, and kitchens and workrooms for the lady of the house. “The baby was the ultimate symbol of the family, the culminating product of the ‘cult of domesticity,’ the center of the familial and cultural expectations,” writes historian Karin Calvett. “Over the course of the 20th century, the American home expanded its footprint and lost formality, placing children, their toys, and their needs at the center of the middle-class-family home” In The Design of Childhood, Alexandra Lange argues that by designing for children we will all benefit. author: Kelly Waldron
paragraph: This section discusses mothering and caregiving through the lense of place, identity, and ritual. Through this lens, I have analyzed the complexity of mothering and how we define mothers in our communities. What is Place? Place is a noun and a verb with multiple definitions. It may be a physical location or a feeling; it may also be a relative position or a designated setting. Depending on the context and persons using the term, it morphs, and ebbs and flows from one meaning to another. In my research, I focus on the similarities and differences of the physical place (grounded, fixed, actual) and place that exists in the hypothetical virtual).1​0 world (ungrounded, fluid, Place in its physical form affects the individual and community in positive and negative ways. Place can shape our identity, it can enforce restrictive cultural norms or liberate us from these norms. In Space and Place: The Perspective of Experience by Yi-Fu Tuan, Space and Place are explored and discussed from a multitude of viewpoints. The tone of the book is both theoretical and emotional. This reference provides insights on how to interrupt acculturated perspectives on place. Tuan explains through literature, poetry, and academic architectural studies that place is us, living transformable beings as well as a Waldron Mothering & Caregiving Spring 2020 19 collection of artifacts. Depending on our experiences in life, we respond to places in different ways. Experience is directed to the external world. Seeing and thinking clearly reach out beyond the self. Feeling is more ambiguous. As Paul Ricoeur put it, “Feeling is . . . without doubt intentional: it is a feeling of ‘something’ - the lovable, the hateful, [for instance]. But it is a very strange intentionality which on the one hand designates qualities felt ​on ​things, o​ n ​persons, ​on ​the world, and on the other hand manifests and reveals the way in which the self is inwardly affected.”1​9 The current Covid-19 pandemic has quickly evolved our intimate experiences of place. Millions of us are confined to our homes in hopes that isolation will take care of one another. Practicing Social-Distancing to stop the rampant spread of COVID-19 has uncovered the importance of caregiving. Our ​survival​ depends on caregiving. Our attention as individuals has turned inward, to the most intimate of our places. Tuan explains that intimate experiences, such as folding laundry, teaching our children, and the overall act of caring for loved ones and neighbors, are difficult acts to share publicly. They are difficult to share because these particular experiences of place are personal and deeply felt. We normally overlook the most intimate of human connection, but the unplanned and intimate human connections hold the richest material. Place exists at different scales, Tuan argues, should destruction occur we may reasonably conclude that the people would be thoroughly

author: Kelly Waldron
paragraph: Waldron Mothering & Caregiving Spring 2020 20 demoralized since the ruin of their settlement implies the ruin of their cosmos. Yet this does not necessarily happen. Human beings have powers.1​9​ strong recuperative Our recuperative powers reside in our ability to care for one another.

author: Kelly Waldron
paragraph: “E​ minent geographer Yi-Fu Tuan considers the ways in which people feel and think about space, how they form attachments to home, neighborhood, and nation, and how feelings about space and place are affected by the sense of time. He suggests that place is security and space is freedom: we are attached to the one and long for the other. Whether he is considering sacred versus “biased” space, mythical space and place, time in experiential space, or cultural insightful.”2​ 0 attachments to space, Tuan’s analysis is thoughtful and

author: Kelly Waldron
paragraph: Waldron Mothering & Caregiving Spring 2020 21 In Situation, by Claire Doherty, there is an attempt to try to understand the origins of “situation” by combining theory on the complex explanation of place, site, locality, and public space. The artists featured throughout the book look at everyday mundane parts of a place to discover the bigger picture. They tell stories through all media and focus on public and private spaces. One question that Claire Doherty asks in the introduction to Situation is “If place-making and the production of new localities are increasingly one of the primary motivations for curators and commissioners, how and where does begin?”5​ artistic engagement with place Situation is written in a scholarly tone with supporting perspectives from artists, theorists, critics, curators, and other cultural influences. Claire Doherty approaches the constellations of place, site, social space, and location in an open cross-disciplinary framework. This book looks at “situation” through four lenses; Action & Public Space, Place & Locality, Fieldwork, and the Limits of Site. Doherty explains that Doreen Massey’s ‘A Global Sense of Place’ is central to the understanding of place as ‘a constellation of social relations, meeting and weaving together at a locus.’5​ ​ particular This idea of place in a ‘constant sense of becoming through practice and practical knowledge’ is held by the creation of place and is the determining counterpoint to the argument between section.5​ the outsider and insider in the fieldwork Waldron Mothering & Caregiving Spring 2020 22 To explore Place, one must explore identity. Identity like place is a multifaceted term. We are continually in search of our identities, and it seems identity is a question without a solid answer. Our self-identity is affected by the numerous spaces we interact with. Our culture, sexuality, family, jobs, friends, religion, government, media, and education; each intersectionality places a part in how we self-identify. This cultural probe was designed as an intimate experience for the extreme users (mothers). Mothers from coast to coast in America participating in this project. Each mother has a different background and a unique take on the probe. Above are details of artifacts returned to me after use.

author: Kelly Waldron
paragraph: Waldron Mothering & Caregiving Spring 2020 23 People assert themselves onto space through placemaking by decorating rooms, planting gardens, hanging flags, erecting monuments, etc. ​In Place, an Introduction ​ by Tim Cresswell, Tim explains the complexity of understanding place and our relationship with it. He suggests using placemaking, place creating and memory making to create spaces where people feel connected. The understanding of place and placemaking within urban and suburban environments is intricately discussed by Jane Jacobs in her essential work ​The Death and Life of Great American Cities.​ Scranton born Jacobs took a spirited look into the commonsense of the urban landscape and brought a refreshing and intimate view of the human relationship to the city or neighborhood in her radar. She discusses placemaking in a soulful way by arguing that our cities and homes should be designed to care for the people that live in them and when they are designed in such a way the inhabitants can better take care of each other. She shares personal stories about her connections to her corner bottega, her streets and sidewalks, her neighbors and parks, and the importance of keeping people on the street to allow them to create their own sense of a personalized microcosm. Reading Jacobs’ work enables you to see how she became a force in urban planning and revival in the ’50s and ’60s.

author: Kelly Waldron
paragraph: Waldron Mothering & Caregiving Spring 2020 24 This film is part of a project, where I used sensory ethnography to dissect the ritual acts of caregiving. I displayed this video alongside a separate audio recording of the laundromat and utilized an essential oil diffuser to construct the laundry mat in a studio space by highlighting the sense of hearing, sight, and smell.

author: Kelly Waldron
paragraph: Waldron Mothering & Caregiving Spring 2020 25 Rituals excite our daily routines and add an orderly set of rules while inspiring imaginative and creative mental play. They can exhibit addictive behaviors in life because of the notion of specialty and magic associated with rituals themselves. Our lives are an ever-changing set of circumstances made more sustainable and organized by our daily routines. Routines help give us structure, but they can over-familiarize us with the existence we create for ourselves. Marlon Bradley’s scholarly epic ​Butch Queens Up in Pumps summarizes the last three decades of the Ballroom culture throughout the country; primarily in NYC, Detroit and Philadelphia. His writing is a tale of his own ethnographic research within Detroit’s Ballroom community, he shares his findings on practices, language, and kinship structures within the community. The communities within the Ballroom culture have an innate ability to design kinship structures through ritual and necessity. The creation of the Ballroom scene was a response to the survival of its members. Most of the members from the Ballroom culture are also from the LGBTQA community and people of color, that have been pushed to the edges of society. The Ballroom community has allowed for an inclusive creative space for its members to express themselves through ritual and design by creating non-biological kinship and family units in stable living environments. B​ utch Queens Up in Pumps ​ presents an in-depth Waldron Mothering & Caregiving Spring 2020 26 perspective that envisions forms of mothering and caregiving that are redefined by ritual, inclusivity, and community design. The New York City ballroom culture has historically used the nuclear family format to create an inclusive living environment centered around caring for marginal groups in our society, specifically the LGBTQA communities. This community is a relevant case to understand how designing family environments can challenge our traditional views of mothers and how our communities can better support mothers and caregivers. Understanding how designed familial space is created to support the power of feminine characteristics is essential in redefining our contemporary view of mothering.

author: Kelly Waldron
paragraph: Waldron Mothering & Caregiving Spring 2020 27 This project is a combination of studying one ritual of caregiving, through ritual, and sensory ethnography. For 100 days I washed dishes, by hand, with a print of Venus in the bottom of the drying rack. The water from the drying dishes bled the printer ink and created abstracted visuals. The more dishes I washed the more abstracted Venus became.

author: Kelly Waldron
paragraph: Waldron Mothering & Caregiving Spring 2020 28 Sensory ethnography, a reflexive and experimental process in which academic and applied understanding, knowing, and knowledge are produced.​15 ​ This practice is used by design researchers to better understand the ways and context in which they should design. User-centered design, social design, experience design, and emotional design all share a relation to sensory studies and are used widely in current design practices. Because sensory ethnography focuses on multi-sensory experiences of the individual, it is an essential tool to use while exploring place and identity. In Sarah Pink’s book Doing Sensory Ethnography, Pink explains how people experience a place by various senses. Sarah Pink is a Professor of Design and Media Ethnography at RMIT University in Australia. She has international recognition for her work in sensory, visual, and digital methodology. In western cultures visual senses are more dominant than smell; vision is organized higher on the hierarchy. But in other cultures smell is the more dominant sense and scent determines a person's identity. Many levels of intricacies and connections can use sensory ethnography for research. Sensory ethnography is here used to explore the intricacies of mothering and caregiving. If we could dissect and deconstruct both themes what do they look like, sound, taste, or smell like? Can we

author: Kelly Waldron
paragraph: Waldron Mothering & Caregiving Spring 2020 29 recognize mothering and caregiving through our deconstructed senses?

author: Kelly Waldron
paragraph: Waldron Mothering & Caregiving Spring 2020 30

author: Kelly Waldron
paragraph: PROJECT My intention of dissecting mothering and caregiving is to redefine the system placed upon women as the main caregivers in society. For my capstone project I used multiple research through design techniques to create an experimental journaling tool kit that helps people explore the construct of mothering and caregiving. This tool kit is designed to be used by highschool students studying sex ed and college level students in gender studies courses.

author: Kelly Waldron
paragraph: The purpose of this tool kit is to give people a hands-on, three dimensional journaling experience. The kit includes wooden shapes, cut and paste words to inspire a journaling direction and blank cut and paste sheets for users to fill in their own words. The shapes signify building blocks, by applying the words to the shapes, the shapes morph into Pillars of Mothering and Caregiving, Pillar Links and Challenging Obstacles. By categorizing the journaling words into three

author: Kelly Waldron
paragraph: Waldron Mothering & Caregiving Spring 2020 31 sections it allows the user to dissect their experience with mothering and caregiving and then reconstruct it into a structure that is inspired by their own experience or is an edited version by adding or redacting words from their structure.

author: Kelly Waldron
paragraph: Because of shelter in place and social distancing I turned the physical tool kit into a digital space. The website I built is a space of its own, created to dissect, inspire and nurture conversations about mothering and caregiving. The shapes and journaling word sheets have been turned into printable templates in which anyone can download and create a mothering and caregiving structure at home. The use of found

author: Kelly Waldron
paragraph: Waldron Mothering & Caregiving Spring 2020 32 objects is also encouraged, and sharing your journaling progress is suggested through Facebook and Instagram platforms.

author: Kelly Waldron
paragraph: Waldron Mothering & Caregiving Spring 2020 33

author: Kelly Waldron
paragraph: Caregiving and mothering are two acts that hold little to no value in American Society. In our current national crisis fighting the COVID-19 virus all of America has turned unexpectedly to lean heavily on the caregivers and mothers in our communities. We now label them as essential workers, people that were mostly not thought of highly in our society are now being asked to sacrifice their health and wellbeing to keep our economy afloat. I hope that by discussing the importance of caregiving and mothering, the future American society will better treat these people and realize the importance of caring for one another. By redefining the meaning of mothering and caregiving in our communities we can create spaces for re-design, enlightenment, and growth. Mothering and Caregiving appear in most levels of our society. The most successful designs make mothering and caregiving a solid pillar on which ideas and communities can grow.
