paragraph: 10 HUMAN vs NATURE APPRECIATION OF... oil production. “By glorifying pristine landscapes, which exist only in the imagination of romantics, Western conservationists divert attention from the places where people live and the choices they make every day that do true damage to the natural world of which they are part.” 3 The nature, placed on a pedestal and fenced off in the city, is a disconnect in which it is easy for humans to regard the city life as separate and inconsequential to the natural system. With nature seeming so distant in the city, an exploration of our relationship with the natural world in the city becomes an inquiry on our disconnect to nature in the urban context. In the investigation of this disparity, nature is redefined and its current perceptions reconstructed. Through design, the deglamorizing of nature from its idealized misconceptions and the introspection of the human’s place among nature may begin to intrinsically reconcile the disparity between the urban-dweller and nature, and, in this way, cultivate environmental stewardship and accountability in the city.

author: Regina Hsin Li
paragraph: 30 APPRECIATION OF... 31 CAPSTONE APPRECIATION OF... The redefining of ‘nature’ does not mean to draw distinctions or to slacken the idea of ‘nature’ into something more palatable to humans or to ease any guilty consciences that we carry for our actions. Reframing boundaries between humans and nature allows nature to become more relatable in the city. Thus, this thesis presents design as a tool to amplify intangible connections, specifically for nature. Design enables appreciation, and, through this process of questioning ethical perspectives, places nature back to the human’s personal proximity. Appreciation itself is an experience that is personal and intimate. Appreciation of nature: the same mindset can be projected to nature this way. Appreciation can be harnessed through design as a catalyst towards a reconciliation and a more harmonious relationship of all of nature. But it is not to suggest that appreciation is sufficient concerning environmental problems—it alone is far from enough, similar to all other singular efforts. Instead, appreciation and attention reveal the interrelations of all on earth: cities to nature, humans, and the earth.

paragraph: This project looks at the impacts of our habits to visualize data and extrapolate information on lifestyle and relationship with nature. Data are cold hard facts, and is also evidence that presented global warming warnings. Visualized data becomes more accessible, but its message does not necessarily reach more people. Poster, 24 x 36 inches

author: Regina Hsin Li
paragraph: The most direct reunion of humans to earth may be in death. Through the system of symbols, this visual language project translated the contemplations of Pure Land Buddhism* with reference to the Painting of the Impure Aspect of the Human Realm. In the process of decomposition, each stage brings the body closer to earth. Posters, 24 x 36 and 14 x 36 inches Booklet, 5 x 8 inches

author: Regina Hsin Li
paragraph: Thinking about our relationship with a physical environment through interactions and the significance of interactions, however brief, in building towards a relationship to a place. The project was located in Albee Square Plaza in the bustling Downtown Brooklyn to serve as context in exploring placemaking in highly urban and transitive settings. Insights gathered from a series of ethnographic research and cultural probes allowed me to collect qualitative data on the interplay between the dynamics of an urban spot and the physicality of space. Through placemaking as a form of relationship, I investigated attention as an instrument to foster connections. Installation on site

author: Regina Hsin Li
paragraph: Graphic translation and exploration of my day in New York City. Visually inscribing experiences, emotions, moments of interests, and different interactions of significance of a Saturday in December. Posters, 16 x 24 inches

author: Regina Hsin Li
paragraph: A series of collages made, one per day, over three months explored built spaces as juxtaposed through natural elements and our human presence. How do they collide in our urban spaces? What defines the interplay between humans and nature? With attention to and inspiration from personal daily moments of the physicality around me throughout three months, the collages reflect and wonder what it means to be humans as part of nature by directly contrasting this interaction. Digital collages, 1080 x 1080 pixels

author: Regina Hsin Li
paragraph: Imagining another method of interchange, this project was a speculation of a way to empathize with a tree as a being through personification. Based on research of the therapeutic effects of nature, the ‘therapist’ is exaggerated to wonder how this may shed some light in the way we view the environment around us. The process of this project harnessed technology and was also an ugly display of the many ways humans impose themselves onto other lifeforms. Arduino, LEDs, chipboard 4 x 4 x 2 inches

author: Regina Hsin Li
paragraph: “Cease!” was a small guerilla-style campaign project that worked with augmented reality technology. It acted as interjections into habits and conventions of everyday life. Using the objects of these behaviors as a window of opportunity, this project brings in loudness and scale to menial and mindless actions that generate and amass a large amount of trash. Targeting the wasteful conveniences of disposables and comfortable and complacent lifestyles, this project wants you to cease and reconsider the easy habits of one’s daily life and the unforeseen consequences. Augmented reality (Aero), manifesto Stickers, 4 x 2.5 inches

author: Lin Chen
paragraph: FROM NO PROSCENIUM TO PROSCENIUM: DECODING IMMERSIVE THEATER METHODOLOGY TO IGNITE SOCIAL CHANGE by Lin Chen

author: Lin Chen
paragraph: A thesis submitted in partial fulfillment of the requirements for the degree of Master of Fine Arts, Communications Design School of Design Pratt Institute

author: Lin Chen
paragraph: May 2020 FROM NO PROCECNIUM TO PROCENIUM: DECODING IMMERSIVE THEATER METHODOLOGY TO IGNITE SOCIAL CHANGE by Lin Chen

author: Lin Chen
paragraph: 2 Abstract A proscenium is the stage of a theater. Today, ‘no proscenium’ refers to the immersive theater—an art form that subverts conventional theater forms into a fusion of installation art, live performance and the audiences’ movement, and actions. Immersive theater highlights the importance of space and design; curates physical, sensual environments; and focuses on the personal, individual, and visceral audience experience. Through immersive theatrical experiences, audiences are empowered to interact with space, performers, other audience members, and themselves to amplify senses of community and conviviality. Immersive storytelling is the visual narrative with the notes of a sensory symphony. This thesis investigates how theater has the potential to change audiences’ perceptions—of reality and contemporary issues, and how designers could create a similar experience to empower the audience in self-reflection and dialogue on social issues, in the tradition initiated by Augusto Boal. As Poppy Crum, a neuroscientist, said, “I believe we can be more human if we accept more than one reality.” “Other” and “otherness” are technical terms used in the social sciences and humanities for the way people tend to consider others, people, or nature, as “not one of us” based on an aspect of their identities. Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie, a Nigerian writer, talks about the danger of a single story in her famous Ted Talk, “The problem with stereotypes is not that they are untrue, but they are incomplete; they make one story become the only story.” Immersive theater, in particular, as a catalyst, has the power to transport the audience members into another person’s reality and present them with a divergent perspective, while simultaneously engaging feelings of aliveness, intimacy, vulnerability, sympathy, and empathy. “Others” are embedded as the characters in the immersive storytelling. This thesis argues that participating in immersive theatrical experiences could trigger feelings of empathy and a more humanistic approach toward reality outside the theater. I argue that designing immersive theater experiences that engage the audience in collaborative and contextual storytelling is a tool to foster social change and inspire a more distributed form of activism to address the issues of “Othering”.

author: Lin Chen
paragraph: 3 Literature Review Theatrical Reality: Space, Embodiment and Empathy in Performance by Campbell Edinborough conceptualizes the methodology of achieving the aesthetics of theatrical representation and meaning. Throughout the book, he addresses two questions, “What do we mean by theatricality?”, and “How might we define the experience of reality within the context of theatrical performance?” “Theatre is a medium where invisible worlds are rooted in invisible objects, where the material becomes a metaphor, and where the intimate and personal are made universal (Edinborough 3).” Edinborough investigates the human experiences of embodiment, space, and place, and studied the complex intersections between reality, fiction, perception, illusion, empathy, and imagination within the performance (20). “One-to-One” is a critical concept in immersive theaters. How performers/characters interact with the audiences will affect the audiences’ engagement level. In Chapter Seven, Edinborough analyzes the approach that the British theatre company Punchdrunk applies in “Sleep No More” and questions that the so-called “unique theatrical experience” has nothing to do with the interactive performance. Audience members are wandering like ghosts. They are still watching or following the performers as in the traditional theatres with silence and a mask, even though they are allowed to move physically and randomly in the place. He shares his uncomfortableness with one-to-one performance and a feeling of self-alienation that reduced how he might usually associate with touch or eye contact. The framework of depending on one’s subjectivity is valued and respected in a way that is not well proved by Punchdrunk’s work. He argues that the importance of spectatorial agency should be used within the context of performance (129). Except for the question of instrumentalizing others within the context of theatrical reality, Edinborough found the reflective distance diminishes between participants and is promoted by a theatrical space. So, what is the process of establishing the spectator/ participant’s corporeal presence with an immersive theatrical reality? And, how to find a dramaturgical balance between these two poles of experience are becoming more and more essential in not limiting the emotional range of the spectator/participant’s relationship, and in an immersive world. Josephine Machon also examines the “One-to-One” performance in her book, Immersive Theatres, and Immediacy in Contemporary Performance, but combines with other

author: Lin Chen
paragraph: 4 theaters professionals’ points of view. The key difference between her argument and Edinborough’s is that she strongly recognizes the unique and positive result of “the aesthetic where the scenographic experience offered ‘not only physically immerses the viewer in three-dimensional space’, it is psychologically absorptive too’ (Machon 34).” Machon acknowledges the audience’s role in “Sleep No More” as a co-producer and a protagonist, and she emphasizes that “Punchdrunk has, accordingly, evolved an audience that has a unique artistic agency within the work (Machon 73).” She addresses that “immersive theaters play with the forms of installation art yet in addition to the audience-actor’s engulfment and interaction with/in the space...” She defines the “immersive theatre” in a chronological way. In the chapter “Defining immersiveness, immersion, immersivity”, she argues that the category of “Immersion as absorption”, “Immersion as transportation”, and “Total immersion” can be applied as definitions of “immersion” within the immersive theater experience. Among them, “Immersive as transportation” can be established within a minimal list of one-to-one moments. “Immersive theatre worlds afford actual, physical cohabitation and contact between human bodies, thereby fusing imagination, interpretation, and interaction (63).” Machon articulates “Immersive theater” is to “describe a particular movement in live performance practice, appears to have some performance practice, appears to have entered common parlance within academic and artistic circles.” Storytelling for virtual reality: Methods and principles for crafting immersive narratives, by John Bucher, studies the interactive and immersive experience via Virtual Reality (VR). The immersive experience means multi-experiential and people’s freedom to respond. The immersion is about the realistic experience crafted through manipulating and mediating the viewer’s point of view. “Communitas” is inspired by artistic events involving a range of more democratic processes within the theatrical experience. In Machon’s notes, she explains that Communitas is a Latin noun; it is both the very spirit of community and/or a community in which there is equality amongst all members. It describes a powerful community spirit, involving a feeling of great solidarity and togetherness (Machon 283).” The word “Communitas” and “Community” resonate with the main ideas discussed in the book Experience Design: Concepts and Case Studies. Experience design is a buzz word frequently used even if its exact meaning is still

author: Lin Chen
paragraph: 5 undefined. The Author, Matthew Turner, argues that “Contributors to Interactions and Performance explore how design can encourage individuals to share the experience by interacting with others, if only momentarily. The public settings for these interactions range from subways to banks, restaurants to theme parks.” In Part Three, festival experience, performance experience, patient experience, mobile user experience, transformative experience, and meal experience are defined as the expression of some “inner condition” of an extroverted group of people, yet managing to create material encounters between designed spaces, performative scripts, and human interaction. Interactions and Performances also take a fresh methodological approach to experience design by addressing the concepts and methods developed in the field of Human-Computer Interaction. Initially, this interdisciplinary subject focused on interactions with static equipment. But as digital technologies have become mobile, ubiquitous, and, in the case of social media, communal or even political, its scope has become increasingly wide-ranging. Experience design reveals different focuses on a different generation. Initially, Pine and Gilmore declared that companies stage an experience whenever they engage customers. To do so, the producers follow a principle to-do-list: create all kinds of scenarios to use the product, not only think about the product itself; The second generation of experience design focused on “customer’s sensory perception, the creation of meaning, and the process of co-creation.” But the customers’ involvement is still mainly defined from the viewpoint of companies when co- creation is usually portrayed as a means for value creation. The author adopted and proposed the idea of “A socio-material approach, turning attention to the performative scripts vaguely encoded in spaces and objects and those who encounter them, and suggesting that these attachments may provide the entrance point to understand more principally how experience is (co-created).” Communal experience and co-creation are the two critical concepts in experience design. “A successful experiential design is thus a platform from which the participants can seek to come under the influence and gain the experience of communitas and togetherness.” Visceral deployment of technology in artistic practice is exploring the way to connect humans with lived experience in unique ways. The more intimately manipulated the technology, the more embodied the experience is perceived to be (Machon, 35). The book, Performing the Digital: Performance Studies and Performances in Digital Culture edited by

author: Lin Chen
paragraph: 6 Martina Leeker, Imanuel Schipper, Timon, tries to map out and investigate the relationship between performance and the ubiquity of digital media. The author emphasizes that digital technologies now are widely and inevitably participate in the ‘making’ of culture. They cannot be taken as separate items. In Chapter “Performing (the) digital,” the position of critique in digital cultures is from the chronological perspective to discuss the implication, possibilities, and limits in digital culture. It starts with the assumption, “Digital cultures are performative cultures. (Leeker 21)” Performance art has conventionally prevailed as a method of gaining the distance necessary for a reflection and critique of techno-cultural. It is interesting for the author to talk more about the ideology of what digital brings to the performance. Communication and action are not arranged rationally; they are highly interdependent on contingent occurrences within a social context and enable technological performances to become more humane. All kinds of sensors and VR/AR/MR/XR are avoidably mentioned when speaking of digital interventions and immersive theater. Regardless of how technology advances, the story is still the most critical essence to shape our feelings and experiences. Chapter 4 “Storytelling principles for Immersive Space” and Chapter 5 “Designing an Immersive Narrative” in the book, Storytelling for virtual reality: Methods and Principles for Crafting Immersive Narratives, John Bucher, talks about how professionals begin to leverage VR as one part of their performance or dance. However, critics have noted that Virtual Reality is more structured like cinematic narratives or even not storytelling. Bucher argues that VR technology can help tell stories in a compelling way. It will invite spectators to step inside the stories. This immersive approach allows the viewers actually to become the camera. Dance functions similarly. Referring to “Theatrical Reality,” Edinborough emphasizes that dance no longer needs to be bound by certain longstanding aesthetic and technical criteria. The artists are using VR to create an immersive environment and helping bridge the gap between actors and the audience. Storytelling in Virtual Reality is less about telling the viewer a story and letting viewers discover their own stories. The emotional journey of the audience through experiences, and perception, how the viewer is experiencing the world, should drive the technical decision making in VR storytelling. Experimenting with VR or other immersive technology means to explore narrative in a way that isn’t limited to a specific place or time.

author: Lin Chen
paragraph: 7 In Chapter Seven, “Creating Narrative Structures,” Buches discusses how “VR requires game mechanics that must be considered when developing an experience” and that “True VR should allow the user agency and the ability to interact with the environment”. These insights are critical when visualizing the relationship between symbolism, metaphor, and bodily empathy within a specific space.

author: Lin Chen
paragraph: 8 How can designers engage today’s audience? In the book, Theater, the lively art, Edwin Wilson denotes theater as a physical place in which theatrical performances happen, like Peter Brook, in the book The Empty Space, said, “A man walks across this empty space, whilst someone else is watching him, and this is all that is needed for an act of theatre to be engaged.” At the heart of the theater experience is the performer-audience relationship, an immediate, personal exchange (Wilson, 6-7). The makeup of audiences has varied from time to time and from place to place. A large percentage of citizens from Athens or Epidaurus would attend the theater in ancient Greece. However, during the late seventeenth century in England, the upper class were the primary audience. In Europe in the nineteenth century, theater once again came to be a popular art form accessible to a wide range of people. A similar situation happened in Asian countries. Theater in some areas was only for an elite audience or for the upper class, but at other times it welcomed audiences from the general population. In today’s theater, audiences are multicultural and diverse. The desire to participate in the dynamics and of a living performance has never changed. Traditionally, they are seen as passive observers or spectators. The blockbuster production and theater phenomenon, Punchdrunk’s Sleep No More, successfully escalates audiences’ experience and provides an elaborately designed space for them to follow characters through locations rather than being led by temporal sequence. In the past two decades, the immersive theater has evolved as a major movement in performance and finds today itself within a mainstream moment. Relational aesthetics helps decode the obsession for audiences to transform from passive spectators to participants. In the collection of essays 1998, Relational Aesthetics, Nicolas Bourriaud explored art within the communication zones for non-scripted social interactions. The term ‘relational’ in Bourriaud’s definition is ‘a formal arrangement that generates relationships between people.’ People are turning their roles as spectators in the society of spectacle to performers in the society of extras (Bourriaud 9). The relational art builds the feeling of community to recover human relations. He also addressed that the work of art as social interstice, a term borrowed from Karl Marx, describes a space that facilitates human social interaction. The falsely aristocratic conception of artwork arrangement is

author: Lin Chen
paragraph: 9 collapsing before our eyes(15). Bourriaud summarized, “It is no longer possible to regard the contemporary work as a space to be walked through. It is henceforth presented as a period of time to be lived through, like an opening to the unlimited discussion.” But not all the audiences want to participate in the immersive environment actively. The engagement level should be varied based on different types of audiences. How to gauge and transport them in a progressive and bespoken way should be considered when studying the aesthetic distance for audience engagement.

author: Lin Chen
paragraph: What can designers learn from theaters to create a lived, shared, resonate experience for audiences to perceive things differently? From Avant-garde theaters to commercial blockbuster to participatory theater In the early stage of modern theater in the nineteenth century, call-and-response was used, especially in musicals. In this form, a performer puts out a call, and an audience calls back a preordained response, adding a participatory element into the theatrical experience. (“What Is Immersive Theatre?”) Epic theater created by Bertolt Brecht wanted to mobilize audiences by having a character in a play break through the invisible “fourth wall”. Directly asking the audience questions but without soliciting answers, Brecht interrogated his audiences, emphasizing that the play was not finished until they acted upon it with their own thoughts. He believed that theater could create an intellectual climate for social change and considered that the audience should not be emotionally hypnotized but should be involved intellectually. He believed that the production should force audiences to remain emotionally detached – or alienated – from the dramatic action. (Wilson, 189) The term Environmental theater was coined by Richard Schechner in the late 1960s. As growing within experimental and avant-garde performance in the post-Second World War era, it attempted to resituate the spectator within the spatial framework of performance. The focus is shifted from the text and its meanings, to the significance of the physical space and the audience within that space. Schechner’s work in the 1960s and 1970s with the Performance Group was an attempt to create not merely a new model of theatre, but through

author: Lin Chen
paragraph: 10 ritual and communality, a new model for society as well. A spectator is allowed to observe performers from a distance, possibly move through the environment or participate performatively to varying degrees (Aronson 20). Schechner was deeply impacted by Russian director Vsevolod Meyerhold, French theatrical theorist Antonia Artaud and the Polish director Jerzy Grotowski. Meyerhold argued for the use of found space (such as streets, factories, and schools) not originally meant for theater. His passion for using auditoriums led him to believe that true intimacy and stage-auditorium unity were achieved only by surrounding the action with the spectators. Artaud wanted to recognize the theater space, make the audience the center of attention, and bombard the viewer’s senses on a subconscious level. Jerzy Grotowski, an innovative Polish theatre director, mentioned that the essence of theater is the interaction between live performances and audiences. Grotowski represents the incorporation of the spectator within a frame, and kinesthetic incorporation – encouraging the spectator to experience the stage space physically, much as the performer does. It forms the basis of much “immersive theater” today. (Wilson, 186) Theater of the oppressed was created by Brazilian theater visionary and Nobel Peace Prize nominee, Augusto Boal, in the 1970s. He drives his audiences to react directly to the action. The audience is not made of passive spectators. Instead, active “spect-actors” are invited on stage to explore solutions on the issues at hand. He applied this discipline to the people of Rio to figure out what they wanted to change about their community and used the audiences’ reaction to change legislation (“Theater of the Oppressed”). Contemporary practices of immersive theater can be seen as an art form that subverts much of the established relationships of conventional theater and a fusion of installation art, video, multimedia, performance, private and public ritual, underground gigs, and open-air festivals. It actively demonstrates the importance of space and design, curating tangible, sensual environments; and focusing on the personal, visceral audience experience. (Machon, 97) Immersive theaters can establish links across sensation, perception, emotion, and moral reasoning in form and content. Tamara(1981) and Tony ‘n’ Tina’s Wedding (1985) can be regarded as significant way stations on the road from environmental to immersive, from experimental to commercial. There are ten intersecting stories centered around the various characters in Tamara (1981). And audiences could exchange information over food and drink. Creator John Krizanc stated, “In Tamara, they’re made to feel that the choice is theirs,

author: Lin Chen
paragraph: 11 that who they follow has a direct bearing on what they experience.” Krizanc and director Richard Rose envisioned this as an attempt at a democratic theatre. But Tamara was not to be taken too seriously at that time. A few years later, an Italian-American wedding in which the audience became family and friends at the ceremony staged in one location. Then the reception held several blocks away from where they ate, drank, and danced with the performers who followed a script but also improvised with the participants. A critic noted that Times Square’s inherent theatricality made it difficult to differentiate the real from the staged (Aronson 17). Matt Trueman, a theatre critic and journalist said, “Immersive theatre...makes a piece of theatre experienced from within rather than as an outside observer…You are part of it, rather than looking on fundamentally distinct”. In Howell’s piece, Foot Washing for the Sole of The Pleasure of Being: Washing, Feeding, Holding or Lifeguard (2012), performers provided intimate moments for participants and audience-participant’s bodies ‘co-author’ the work with the artist, which further blurs the line between audience, performer and creators. Waiting Room (2017) was a series of waiting rooms created for the Museum of Science in Boston in which deeply bureaucratic experiences were designed for museum- goers. They went from room to room waiting in line until finally, some participants decided to topple the authority of this experience, which turned out to be exactly what the game was all about, and that was how you win the game by not playing. Participants had to collaborate to make it through (Pletcher). That democracy in action is a group choosing to work together for the betterment of the herd (Aronson,16). Many political groups and other groups use theater as a means of educating people or furthering a cause. In recent times, we have seen the emergence of theater as representative of various communities: Feminist theater, Gay and Lesbian Theater, Radical Political Theater, Fringe theater, and so on. The people who present such productions feel that the theater’s imaginative and symbolic power will affect their audiences (Wilson, 243). Jean Weinbloom, an Executive Producer at Meow Wolf claimed that immersive could be considered as an “inherently anti-authoritarian” medium. The question she asked at the Immersive Design Summit 2019, “Does the work that we’re creating have to have the same rules as the world that we’re living in?” inspired creators and designers to think critically about crafting the immersive theatrical experience.

author: Lin Chen
paragraph: 12 How does sensory help? From an architectural perspective Juhani Pallasmaa, a leading Finnish architect and architectural theorist, demonstrates how physical space should only be apprehended through all of the human senses, including movement and proprioception. He highlights how physical architecture accentuates an individual’s presence via sensory design (Pallasmaa, 40). Frontal perspectives. Proscenium, end, thrust, alley, and arena stages are all frontal in that a spectator observing a performance rarely has to look more than forty-five degrees to the right or left in order to view the whole production and to take in the entire visual and spatial field. Certain of these stages, such as the arena, will provide somewhat different perspectives for viewers depending on where they are seated, and some will make the spectators more aware of each other, but in all cases, the audience is facing “forward” and is generally focused on the same space and action. The central focus is still used for a non-illusionistic performance, such as a standup comedy or even a lecturer. Most forms of frontal staging provide a fairly clear frame, literal or implied, that separates the spectators from the performance. As Herbert Blau stated, theatre “posits itself in distance” (Blau, 86). Multiple perspective. In the late 12th or 13th century, moveable platforms, called pageant wagons, were developed primarily to stage cycle plays. It is a kind of spatial distribution system. Fixed platforms were the alternative form of staging. The mansions might be scattered out around the place, causing the audience to follow the action and experiences all three (or more) differently. In 1730, Bibienas invented an angular or multipoint perspective. Rather than a single vanishing point located at the rear of a setting, Bibiena used two or more vanishing points at the sides, which brings more perspectives and imagination space to the audience. The rejection of frontal staging had dominated Western theatre since the Renaissance (Laurel, 201).

author: Lin Chen
paragraph: 13 From an anthropological perspective. A culture’s sensory order is one of the first and most essential elements of making ourselves human. Kathryn Linn Geurts defined sensory order (or sensorium) as a pattern of relative importance and differential elaboration of the various senses. Geurts demonstrates how consciousness and ‘knowing’ embrace a panoply of inner states and embodied involvement, incorporating emotion, sensation, perception and cognition, proprioception, and kinaesthesia (Aronson 20). Prioritize the senses, the reawakening of the holistic sentience of the human body, allows for rethinking the ways in which humans ‘think’. All of the five senses are accepted as being interconnected ‘affairs of the whole body’. Embodied sentience is innate in all humans and exists in everyday pursuits when can be activated by carefully crafted immersive events. How does new Immersive Technology support theatrical experiences? In the book, The End of Storytelling, Stepanie Riggs explains that immersive technologies have the potential to replace our sensory input entirely and blur the line between symbolic representations and the known world and defines how they integrate content with reality. Her concept, The Storyplex Spectrum starts from Reality, the real environment. Classical realism is photography and video. Virtual Reality includes 360, Spatial, and interactive. Enhanced Reality is Augmented Reality, Mixed Reality, Augmented Virtuality, Integrated Reality. Integrated Reality aligns the presence of physical “sets” or haptic input devices with sensory effects experienced inside a head-mounted display (Riggs, 18). These haptic affordances facilitate the illusion of being physically present in a virtual environment. She admits that immersive theater performances and escape rooms occur in the real-world and use immersive technology to create an integrated reality that can sometimes be even more powerful than their virtual world counterparts (Riggs, 54). Heidi Boisvert, a new media artist and creative technologist, who now focuses on measuring neurobiological effects when using emerging technology, emphasized in her Ted Talk that “The web, mobile devices, Virtual and Augmented Reality, were rescripting our nerve systems. And they were changing the structure of our brain.” (Boisvert) Lance Weiler, a founding member, and director of the Columbia University Digital Storytelling Lab, a thought leader in promoting tech-driven immersive theater, constantly

author: Lin Chen
paragraph: 14 experiments with new technology that could be applied in real-life immersive theatrical experiences. A research Project Frankenstein AI is designed to provoke exploration around possible shared futures for artificial intelligence. Project The Raven was trying the Internet of Things (IoT) lantern, Bluetooth Beacon, and AR glasses for audio to dramatize the bespoken journey about Edgar Allan Poe’s life.

author: Lin Chen
paragraph: Why and how can designers develop characters by using the space? The contact hypothesis reveals that contact between two groups leads to tolerance and acceptance, but only under certain conditions, such as equal status among groups and common goals. And further studies from the University of California, Santa Cruz research psychologist Thomas Pettigrew, PhD, have shown that the most critical factor in changing attitudes is an emotional connection, starting to like someone once you get to know them (“All You Need Is Contact”). Immersive theater creates a parallel world or can perform as a lab for putting two groups of people together. Dan Archer, the Founder of Empathetic Media, a Graphic Journalist and Creative Technologist, conducted a study to investigate the relationship between the participant’s level of immersion and their desire to take action in 2016. One key finding is that a trusted narrator can increase users’ sense of immersion and facilitate an empathetic connection and enhance engagement. The higher the empathetic response, the higher the likelihood was respondents being able to remember the stories they watched (Archer). A scholar Kim Myung-Goo pointed out in his paper, "People in space can best show their living state and value, so observing people's life and the environment from the perspective of space is the best way to understand people.” Compared with traditional character interiority and development in theater, Punchdrunk adopted its unique methodologies and techniques when designing immersive experiences for non- traditional theater practices and pedagogy. In the guidebook, one exercise is about creating characters through world-building. The relationship between characters and space becomes three-dimensional through considering all the questions such as characters we never hear about but might be connected to named characters, which are

author: Lin Chen
paragraph: 15 their home spaces, which spaces do they pass through, which spaces do they find safe? Which spaces do they fear? This exercise helps designers to visualize and think about the world that they are about to create and can be a springboard for socio-historic contextual research (Punchdrunk).

author: Lin Chen
paragraph: What can designers learn from the principles of psychology, game design and LARP to create an immersive theatrical experience From psychology perspectives, “Willing suspension of disbelief” and “aesthetic distance”. In theaters, even though audiences are aware that the events onstage are not occurring and the people on the stage are just performers, they still feel the immediacy of the experience and may burst into tears. This experience is a phenomenon that the English poet and critic Samuel Taylor Coleridge (1772- 1834) called “willing suspension of disbelief.” Audiences proactively choose to believe in what is happening on the stage and leave all literal and practical considerations behind. This experience could transport audiences to another physical place. Paradoxically, aesthetic distance is another way to help the audience enter into an imaginative world. Aesthetic distance means a physical or psychological separation or detachment of the audience from dramatic action. Once the distance is established, the observer can enter into the experience fully and completely. These two concepts seem conflict, but they both function as means for engaging audience members. Perceptual response cycle. The senses of vision, hearing, taste, and smell are located in the head. The senses of touch and movement are located throughout the body and the senses that people receive information from internal and external environments. The act of perceiving is how people filter, modify, distort, accept, reject, and use that information. Through previous experiences, present circumstances, and future expectations, audiences are interpreting information psychophysically (“Senses The process by which people vary and Perception 1”). our responses to information based on the context of each situation. Perception-

author: Lin Chen
paragraph: 16 Representation-Action (PRA) loop is the concept developed in the book, Computers as Theatre, by Brenda Laurel. She demonstrates an entity can perceive its environment, and then can construct an internal representation of its perception. It will perform actions based upon that representation. Those actions are, in turn, perceived within its environment, forming a loop (Laurel 212). Game design Situational game design is an embodied approach of the interactive experience design, which resonates with a lot of immersive design. And it is an approach that can reflect playfulness in immersive theatre: playing to explore, playing to destroy, playing to construct, playing to experiment, and playing for the story. Kathryn Yu argues that the immersive work can create a space for aimlessness and stillness, but not necessarily leading the participant to a specific “win” state. This methodology breaks through the typical stereotype of game experience and aligns it most closely with other art forms like theatre or role-playing. Consequently, the act of being aimless or still during the experience may even allow the participant to focus on being reflective or present (Yu). Level design can be designing one room in a game or as complex as designing an entire continent or galaxy. The level designer’s job is to create playable content for the core features to be used inside the world and determine where users want to go, where they want to start or end up, and what users will do along the way. This technique is used in the scene and the interaction design of Sleep No More. It focuses on deciding which objects the player should see right away, and which should be hidden, behind blockages or inside drawers; adjusting the placement of light and shadow to draw the eye in the right direction; narrowing space, or lowering the perceived height of the ceiling, to make a room feel cozy or oppressive as needed; changing the affordances of the space to control how the audience was able to interact with it.

author: Lin Chen
paragraph: 17 LARP Interaction Alibi is an excuse to enable a person to perform an act and an action of some kind without fear of the consequences or social punishment. Giving audiences an alibi is equal to giving them permission to be someone else. Interaction alibis are a way of overcoming the embarrassment that keeps adult humans from acting in ways that might draw unwanted attention to them. For instance, screaming is generally not accepted in public, but if the scenario is a music festival or sporting event, people would understand. As opposed to traditional theatre, immersive theater is a performance form that emphasizes the importance of a specially designed space that both the cast and audience inhabit. Sets contain hints and clues, bits of backstory, and additional information that can profoundly influence a given audience member’s understanding of the performance (Rodley). Bespoke design is opposed to either iterating on a local tradition or using the same LARP system. The bespoke term was invented by Participation Design Agency. The bespoke style is focused on creating a powerful compact immersive experience. Characters and groups are pre-written as part of a game’s plot and set-up, then assigned to players. Physical interaction is encouraged in the play space between consenting and enthusiastic players who negotiate them. Bespoke design breaks the rules of classic Mind’s Eye Theatre games because they encourage a different experience (Jackalope).

author: Lin Chen
paragraph: Contribution to the discipline This thesis analyzes the current methods of designing immersive theater, studying perceptual response cycle, casting audience experience, situational game design, level design, interaction alibi, and bespoke design. Learning from the ways in which immersive theater design, by constructing scenarios or situations, designers could bring people together and facilitate them to experiment with the unfamiliar environment, overlay multiple perspectives through multi-sensory and storytelling, trigger conversations, redefine the boundary of self

author: Lin Chen
paragraph: 18 and group identity, and discover new ways of thinking. Designing theatrical immersive experience is designing the possibility for people to have more choices through narratives. Immersive technologies could be leveraged to customize the experience to engage participants. A successful immersive experience should make the audience feel a lived memory, an experience sharing moment within the protected space that also can resonate with each of the participants so as to create a stronger impact, and sense of belonging. This thesis provides non-profit organizers and next-generation designers who are interested in immersive theatrical experiences with a methodology and a conceptual framework. Immersive theatrical design is a collaborative interdisciplinary and open framework that could include a promenade, installation, interactive, site-specific, simultaneous storytelling, environmental, and experiential elements (Garver). Designers could apply this methodology to shape the changes of perspectives among space, performers and spectators, condense collective power via interaction design, translate the knowable underlying mechanisms of empathy, craft the experience by balancing the immersion and agency, and thus create a sanctuary for digital generations to rehumanize social interaction, encourage empowerment, celebrate living and attend to being. Process and Evaluation The conceptual framework, inspired by R. Buckminster Fuller's Geodesic domes and The Octet Truss, consisted of exploring different levels of the relationship among audience members, “others” and immersive storytelling elements, and the flowing space created among different elements. has proven to be one of The spherical structure of a dome the most find. The Octet Truss, called Synergetic Building Construction, efficient human shelters one can is a building component of a tetrahedron and an octahedron with equal edge lengths. The famous Polish-British sociologist and philosopher Zygmunt Bauman pointed out in his book Liquid Modernity, everything is constantly flowing like liquid in modern society, the relation between space and time becomes processual, uncertain and dynamic instead of pre- determined and static. The framework I tried to build for social impact designers is to inspire them to think and create the stories and characters spatially. My research has primarily involved examining a series of best practices and design techniques of immersive theaters to nourish reflection on practice. The best practices are selected from “The Top 35 XR 19 Installations Of 2019”, “The Top 50 XR Experiences Of 2019”, “2019 list of 50+ Immersive Things that mix storytelling, performance, play, design & code”, “NoPro’s Best Shows & Experiences of 2019”, and my personal immersive experiences. The first layer of the framework is the relation established among audience members, immersion, interactivity, and “others”. When audience members are inspired to get closer to characters, “others”, their roles are transformed and empowered as voyeurs, listeners and participants. Voyeurs are redefined as the audiences who are motivated to secretly enter into the characters’, others’ lives, and dig out more hidden stories from the uncanny space storytellers created. Voyeurs take a third-party perspective but observe the others by constantly switching angles, editing their own lens to gain multiple perspectives. Listeners are offered a deep understanding of the others’ experiences and creating a profound human connection with a stranger. They could actively listen to other’s stories and resonate with their own stories in the designed settings. Participants are performing some responsibilities in other’s lives. What they do could alter the plots or affect other’s life experiences. It is essential to use immersive tools for presenting topics to an unfamiliar, casual audience, and giving them a brief exposure to a topic that they are then likely to be curious about. Immersion indicates a way to explore an audience member’s inner world through three dominant sensories, visual-audio, multi-sensory, and whole body. Interactivity shows the agency level audience members are given in the immersive space to interact with the external world. High agency gives audiences the freedom to make any decision that would impact their experience when interacting with the environment, while low agency is more of a “director’s cut” of the films with a fixed view. And I use mouth to represent the characters, “others” in my framework, suggesting the invisibility and lack of voice from the “others”. The second layer is the space to connect the immersive storytelling elements and the examples. The critical elements are summarized as Presence and Liveness, Theatrical Experience, Round Characters, Parallel/interconnected narrative, Chance Encounter, Movement, and Boundaries from those best practices mentioned above and my primary experiences. Presence and liveness emphasize the embodied experience like “to be seen or see others”. The live performance activates the dramaturgy and emotional journey. The theatrical experience focuses on creating suspension to drag the audience's attention through a crescendo, lighting, and new technology. One key finding in a collaborative research

author: Lin Chen
paragraph: 20 project, “Are you what you watch?”, shows if a social issue is covered on Criminal Minds, it will reach a broad range of views no matter what political value you have in the United States. E.M. Forster described Round characters in his book Aspects of the Novel (1927). A round character provides audiences a complex and slightly fresh perspective each time they come into the story, compared with a flat character, who is two dimensional and does not change throughout the work. Parallel/interconnected narrative denotes a story structure in which the writer includes the non-linear stories with multiple perspectives and closely related to situational game design. Chance encounters reflect level design, an element to add spontaneity to the experience and manage serendipity or control surrounding for audience members. The Perceptual response cycle is triggered through human movement and emerging technological tools that expand and refine our motile capabilities. Boundary is the aesthetic distance that sets for audience members to interact with themselves, performers, or space. Some audiences will not get immersed when the roles or agencies are assigned to them improperly. The third level is about the examples I collected to tell the stories when featuring immersive storytelling elements in impressive ways. The framework is designed via aFrame, an open-source WebXR platform for building three-dimensional experiences and virtual space adventure. There is no clear direction or detailed guidelines for players to start. Different people could move differently and randomly to get their own design inspiration at their own pace. To create psychological spatiality for audience members to get into “other’s” world, the following projects I designed are considering those critical immersive storytelling elements. They lay the groundwork for viewers to experience a degree of expectation within the context of the story. Round character, theatrical experience, and chance encounter are most considered in Elio’s Desk. The project brings up the question: Can you know a person from his desk? Can you piece one’s life together and discover his secrets through his desk even if you haven’t seen the movie “Call Me by Your Name.” Based on my study of the character, Elio and his room, I decided to physicalize Elio’s desk as a five-minute immersive theater to interact with my audiences. They can read Eilo’s diary, letter, touch the books or posters he likes, or pick up the phone from “Elio.” Through their exploration, they finally find out this unattainable

author: Lin Chen
paragraph: 21 love between two homosexual lovers within 10 minutes. When they share their findings with each other, the screen projecting Elio’s sad face, the last few minutes of the movie reveals the end of the story. The desk, a corner of his bedroom, engenders its own narrativity, by the mere fact of its existence, and invites voyeurs to get into Eilo’s world with their own lens. Empathy project is designed to discover the boundary, presence and liveness. In memory of a Korean celebrity who suffered from depression and committed suicide after long-time cyberbully, I used a dark room to represent the internet environment and wrote those dehumanized words, such as “bitch, slut” repeatedly on my mask with glow-in-the-dark acrylic paint as those people bullied her on the internet. The audiences who interacted with me were given a small black light. As a performer, I hid myself in a big black gown with a mask covering my face. The question I want to raise is when you can’t see me when you just see other comments on me, what would you do? Will that trigger empathy with the change of the distance, the change of the relationship between the performer and audiences? Instead of explicitly describing a specific event, the project mainly focuses on the experience. When their roles are transited from voyeurs to listeners or participants, their responses or behaviors will change accordingly. One audience patted on my head to comfort me as a friend, but another one tried to stab my eyes to show his resistance since he was scared. It is a process of acceptance of otherness that ensures self-awareness that sustains the human species. Out Inside project is an interdisciplinary intervention reflecting movement and parallel/interconnected narratives. My idea was to use an immersive story box to visualize the synthetic environments and convey a profound and complex sense of sensorial perception to the viewer. Experience designers always talk about how to better center the audience members, the insiders. But what about the outsiders, who could watch over the audiences? Should their experiences be included in immersive storytelling? Is seeing believing? The roles of insider and outsider constantly shift through our perspectives, and often neither the characters nor the audience can fully understand where the real power lies. Like “The Truman Show,” you are being watched when you watch others’ lives. What one did or touched unconsciously could affect others’ perspectives without knowing. And they probably would never know what they missed if they didn’t communicate with each other. In my invented world, when the insider twisted the knob, nothing happened to him, but the outsider would see the roses blooming and illuminating in the dark. When the outsider got closer to

author: Lin Chen
paragraph: 22 the box, the insider would see the light on because of the Passive Infrared Sensor. The study of symbols is also reflected in designing the box, butterflies, unicorn, scorpion, and elephant. In chaos theory, the butterfly effect is to show a small change in one state of a deterministic nonlinear system can result in significant differences in a later state. In the encyclopedias, the horn of a unicorn was said to have the power to render poisoned water potable and to heal sickness. Scorpion is the creature with the burning sting. In Greek mythology, the scorpion was dispatched by the goddess hunter Artemis and her mother, Leto, to kill Orion. So it is hard to define good and evil. And one narrative is about “The Elephant Man (1950)”. John Merrick is the patient who has been called the elephant man. Another parallel story is about a deformed artist’s performance in Union Square Park. Artist Mikel Glass made wooden sculptures to depict cardboard and paper products as a physical manifestation. Viewers would think it is just cardboard if they don’t touch it. I painted cardboard in white to make it look like wooden. If the insider didn’t touch it, he would think it is wooden. I want to use the symbols as a metaphor to deepen the theme and expand the story’s dimensions. The sound collages helped construct the parallel narratives and create the crescendo to hint the participants. The insider and outsider only could know what happened after talking to each other.

author: Lin Chen
paragraph: Conclusion The thesis has aimed to decode how immersive storytelling could address “Othering” issues and assist social impact designers in applying the framework to step into this Immersive Industry as early as possible and seed the humane future. Immersion sparks a lot of creative forms and platforms to tell powerful stories. Immersive theater is no longer a narrative structure, but an invented world within certain boundaries and is organized according to specific rules. New and young audiences are continuously drawn by immersive work more than ever before. With different scales of immersion and agency, audience members are empowered to explore the inner and external world, connect and empathize with the characters and respond to their surroundings automatically. This experience of sensory extension through space to

author: Lin Chen
paragraph: 23 see multiple perspectives could produce lasting and indelible impressions, blur theatricality, extend it to the real world, and ignite attitude change. Immersive theater redefines the relationship among audiences, performers, and space. The potential of immersive storytelling to create meaningful relationships is evitable and tremendous. It is not only to reflect on how present technology shapes our culture value and social behavior, but also to challenge future technology’s trajectory to work through social issues. Immersive experiences can provoke opportunities for creative collaboration and partnerships. My conceptual framework wants to help hone the thinking and creative process by bringing various perspectives from established best practices and depicting a number of immersive environments as settings for action. Social impact designers should enter this industry early to shape its goals and its uses while it is still being formed. They and the marginalized groups are not only the subjects of the stories. They are drivers and real problem definers.

author: Roma Menon
thesis_title: Paradise Abyss: The Exploration Of Intersectional Feminism In Video Games
paragraph: Video game feminism is a fight for gender equality in a patriarchal, misogynistic industry. However, mainstream feminism often forgets about the marginalization and discrimination of black and brown women. Women of colour have continuously strug- gled to have their voices and identities explored in mainstream media. Black Cyberfeminism, coined by Kishonna Gray in 2016, looks at using Black femi- nist critique to reflect on the marginalized in digital realms. By extending Haraway’s concept of the cyborg, which de-essentializes the structure and dualism in identity, Black Cyberfeminism promotes and investigates intersectionality, between race, gender and sexuality, to resist the hegemony of “assumed whiteness” within internet technologies. Mattie Brice, a black, trans game critic, advocates the use of grassroots activism to create change through an interpersonal method as bringing change in this industry can be done through small, non-incremental change. Thus, this thesis aims to use the Black Cyberfeminist framework to under- stand and investigate video games, specifi- cally single-player games as the genre has the largest number of white male protagonists while using Brice’s interpersonal method of activism to fight the hegemony of cis-white males by creating a game with both divergent gamers and normative creators in order to build a bridge between the two. 3

author: Roma Menon

paragraph: grassroots efforts, this thesis worked with divergent gamers and normative creators to create a game that reflected the ‘distinctness of the virtual feminist community’. As someone who is part of both the normative –being Indian– and the divergent –being queer– it was important to consciously collaborate with my participants to create an experience that expanded beyond simply adding diversity into the medium. Utilising the third Black Cyberfeminist framework and Brice’s inter- personal method of change, this thesis used video game design and narrative as a plat- form to highlight the issues found in games and gaming structure to the public, gamers and aspiring game creators. It also hopes to create an initiative that allows minority gamer creators to be included in the conversation and creation of the games. To capture the ‘distinctiveness’, a cultural probe was conducted to garner information on women of colour gamers and their experi- ences with racism, sexism and misogyny both on and offline. The participants started with creating their own ideal video game character with statistics, backstories and visuals. In addition, participants were given a chart and questions to illustrate their experiences. The participants were specifically people of colour. Taking one particpant, the game was built on their experience.

author: Roma Menon
thesis_title: Paradise Abyss: The Exploration Of Intersectional Feminism In Video Games
paragraph: ACTION-ADVENTURE GAMES Action-adventure is a hybrid video game genre that fuses elements from both the action and adventure game genres. Due to the individual genres declining in popularity, the Action- Adventure genre is very broad and very inclu- sive. It combines situational problem solving from adventure games with real-time interac- tions that test the reflexes from action games. BLACK CYBERFEMINISM A social theory created by Dr. Kishonna Gray in 2015. She uses the limitations of Cyberfeminism and Technofeminism and the critical concepts of Black feminism to create Black Cyberfeminism in order to critique the technosphere of white dominance. She explores the intersectionality of race AND gender from physical identities existing in a virtual space and provides the tenets to fight the hegemony of internet technologies. CYBERFEMINISM A genre of feminism that theorizes and critiques internet technologies. The term was coined in 1991 by theorist Sadie Plant in Britain and VNS Matrix an art collective in Australia. Cyberfeminism encouraging women to partake, challenge and reclaim the hyper-male dominated spheres as women and the internet are one and the same. However, a problem in this theory is that not all women have access to computers. It is ignoring the reality of those who do not have access to the same kind of middle-class white privilege.

author: Roma Menon
thesis_title: Paradise Abyss: The Exploration Of Intersectional Feminism In Video Games
paragraph: 17 CYBORG A concept found in Donna Haraway’s A Cyborg Manifesto, it describes a cyborg as a “creature of social reality” [Haraway, 5] Haraway’s cyborg is an important concept that feminists often refer to as she critiques the rigid dualities of social constructs, total theories and the absence of intersectionality in feminism. By using the cyborg as a metaphor she explains how feminists should get away from ‘identity politics’ as its narrative excludes women of colour and queer of colour. “There is no drive in cyborgs to produce total theory, but there is an intimate experience of bound- aries, their construction and deconstruction” [Haraway, 66] #GAMERGATE In August 2014, Eron Gjoni, an aspiring game designer, published an online mani- festo accusing his ex-girlfriend and indie game creator at the time, Zoe Quinn, of sleeping with Nathan Grayson, a writer for the website Kotaku for a great review of her game Depression Quest. It was initially a conver- sation about the ethics of game journalism however it devolved into gatekeeping and harassment. Many men publicly doxed women who supported Zoe Quinn blinded by the false accusation and misogynistic thinking. They also targeted famous female game critics/ designers like Anita Sarkeesian and Brianna Wu by publicly releasing their personal infor- mation and threatening them physically. The scandal exposed the heavy anti-women rhet- oric that many male gamers believe in.

author: Roma Menon
thesis_title: Paradise Abyss: The Exploration Of Intersectional Feminism In Video Games
paragraph: 18 INTERSECTIONALITY Coined by black feminist scholar Kimberlé Crenshaw in 1989 during the third wave of feminism, intersectionality is a framework that deviates from ‘mainstream’ feminism where it investigates the overlap in social identities – such as gender, race, class, sexuality, etc – and discrimination. The goal of intersectionality is to recognise and consider the relationship between the different forms of discrimina- tion and the promotion of social equality. Intersectionality acknowledges that all of the marginalised have different experiences with being marginalised and feminism cannot simplify everyone’s struggle. MEDIA REPRESENTATION Media representation is how media, such as television, film and books, depict certain types of people or communities. Media in the United States typically underrepresent people of colour, LBGTQA+ people, people with a range of body shapes and types, people of non-Christian religions, and different- ly-abled people and the intersectionality of those mentioned in leading roles. While there has been a slight increase in diversity, the numerical gap between white men as leads and non-heteronormative as leads in movies is vast. It is even worse in video games where there are barely any playable lead roles for non-white, non-male characters. PLAY AS ACTIVISM Advocated by Mattie Brice, it is the idea of using gameplay to help understand a social issue through a more engaging and fun process. Using play as a form of activism allows the activist to tackles the seriousness of any social issue and presents it as through an imaginative, explorative but also in a practical purpose.

author: Roma Menon
thesis_title: Paradise Abyss: The Exploration Of Intersectional Feminism In Video Games
paragraph: 19 TECHNOFEMINISM A book that was written by Judy Wajcman, a sociologist in Australia. It is an investigation of the role of gender plays within technology. Another extension of the Cyborg, the book restructures the relationship between woman and machine. Within the book, she argues “analyses of everything from transit systems to pap smears must include a technofemi- nist awareness of men’s and women’s often different positions as designers, manufacturing operatives, salespersons, purchasers, profiteers, and embodied users of such technologies.”( Wajcman, 111) WOMANIST A social theory based on the historical and social experiences of women of colour, specif- ically black women. The term was coined by Alice Walker in her book In Search of Our Mothers’ Garden: Womanist Prose in 1983 due to the lack of mainstream understanding of the black woman’s struggles. She defined womanists as “Womanish, the opposite of girlish…Being grown up…A Black Feminist or Feminist of Color…A woman who loves other women, sexually and/or non-sexually. Appreciates and prefers women’s culture, women’s emotional flexibility (values tears as natural counterbalance of laughter), and women’s strength. Sometimes loves individual men, sexually and/or non-sexually”. (Walker, 1)
