author: Rosemarie Arroliga Noguera
paragraph: 3 Research Questions How and when do we learn about money? How do parents teach personal finance to their children? What role do memories play in our learning?

author: Rosemarie Arroliga Noguera
paragraph: Table of Content Abstract 5 We Talk A Lot About It 6 We Still Struggle 9 We Have No Formal Education About It 11 Our Interaction With Money Has Changed 12 We Learn It From Our Parents 14 We Should Design Tools To Help Parents Do That 16 Story Book and Website 17 References 18

author: Rosemarie Arroliga Noguera
paragraph: 4 Abstract The lack of financial literacy has negatively affected the U.S. population for decades. Currently, most of the country's population is not prepared to make sound financial decisions. As our educational system does not require personal finance as part of the curriculum in most states, the primary source of financial education comes from parents or caregivers in the household. Parents transmit their own financial habits to their children directly or indirectly; however, the ones that are not educated in this matter will struggle to transmit the right financial habits to the next generation, continuing the cycle of financial illiteracy. Personal finance illiteracy rates keep growing every year. This is a response to a problem of great magnitude. Even though it is perceived as a cold and dull topic, the reality is that money is a very emotional subject. Finance is perceived as complicated and intimidating by many and this thesis will use communication design to simplify it to its core in the form of a children’s book and interactive website.

author: Rosemarie Arroliga Noguera
paragraph: 5 We Talk A Lot About It Finance has always been a very popular topic in the media; it is regularly discussed in the news, it is a contentious issue during a presidential campaign and affects many as a daily struggle to overcome debt. We see it in movies, we laugh about it through comedy shows, and we even share transactions through social outlets. Personal finance is often portrayed in popular media. In the pilot of the Cosby Show back from 1989 a dialogue between the main character and his dad is exemplary: The son, after coming home with a bad school report card, lets his dad know that he does not intend to go to college since he does not need to get a high paying job. The dad teaches his son how to budget using monopoly money and estimates his future salary and expenses. Their conversation brings up the harsh reality of real living expenses. The budgeting makes it clear that he will need to reach for higher education so that he can have a better chance of getting a job with a salary that would cover such expenses. This is an example of an ideal scenario of a household communication about personal finance. Shahar Ziv, the founder of Acing Your Finances, recommends this episode as a way to engage his students and make financial lessons more entertaining (2017).

author: Rosemarie Arroliga Noguera
paragraph: Pilot The Cosby Show ​ Another representation of finances in the media can be found in The Simpsons’ episode “The Trouble with Trillions.” This cartoon show has always covered controversial topics for adults with a touch of sarcasm and comedy. In this episode, the show exposes some of the common mistakes we make

author: Rosemarie Arroliga Noguera
paragraph: 6 during tax season. We see the whole town gathering in line at the post office on the last day to submit their taxes. There is a newscast reporter interviewing people on why they waited until the last minute, and the excuses are not even close to valid but also feel really close to reality. We see some trying to cut lines in desperation to submit the forms in time, others filling them up wrong, and then we see Homer watching the madness from the comfort of his living room surrounded by his family. Homer, of course, forgets to file his taxes at all and he remembers to do so after watching the media. As he fills his form in a rush and completely inaccurately, he rushes to the post office to deliver it just right before the doors close. Then his package accidentally falls in the “Audit” pile and he is audited by the IRS. This general concept of people waiting until the last minute is portrayed as something everyone can relate to, and the excuses and mistakes while filing do represent common confusion when it comes to doing one’s own taxes; which is why watching this episode is so funny, but should we be laughing about it? It almost feels like we have accepted defeat.

author: Rosemarie Arroliga Noguera
paragraph: The Trouble with Trillions The Simpsons ​ Similarly, SNL aired a skit in 2006 called “Don’t Buy Stuff” where the concept of debt is mocked by highlighting that avoiding debt is actually quite easy if people simply don't buy things they cannot afford. Given the nature of the show, the episode represents this issue with sarcasm; however, it demonstrates the reality of problems caused by little understanding of finance. The episode portrays Chris Parnell promoting a money management book to a couple, Amy Poehler and Steve Martin, struggling with their finances. Their dialogue is perceived as almost ridiculous which makes it

author: Rosemarie Arroliga Noguera
paragraph: 7 extremely funny. Chris Parnell introduces the book “Don’t Buy Stuff You Cannot Afford,” and after reading some of the book content, Amy Poehler asks Parnell “Okay, but what if I want something but I don’t have any money” to which he responds “You don’t buy it.” Iterations of the same principle keep going throughout the skit. SNL is often representing popular topics happening at the moment by adding a comedy twist. This episode reduces many complex questions and decisions related to money to a simple answer, maybe too simple. The skit highlights the poor understanding of finances and how desire drives our spending habits.

author: Rosemarie Arroliga Noguera
paragraph: Don’t Buy Stuff Saturday Night Life ​ We see the representation of finance all over the media. Whether it is Dwight Schrute from The Office ​ making investment decisions and owning the company building, or Michael Scott having awful spending habits that lead him to even think the only way out is to file for bankruptcy, or the Banks family in Mary Poppins who are trying to repay their loan to keep their home. ​ ​

author: Rosemarie Arroliga Noguera
paragraph: 8 We Still Struggle Even though personal finance has become a popular term, it is still perceived as complicated and intimidating by many. According to the research “Money is Changing so Let’s Talk About It” by Visa, “Millenial women are more likely to talk to their friends about weight, relationship issues, or their sex lives than they are to discuss money.” This is not only an uncomfortable topic and taboo within friends but also in the workplace. Research shows that the number one challenge for women when asking for a raise is that they do not feel comfortable doing so.

author: Rosemarie Arroliga Noguera
paragraph: Money is Changing so Let’s Talk About It. Visa ​ The perception of finance as a confusing and complex field can also be identified in college students. According to the policy paper “Decoding the Cost of College,” students have been facing financial aid award letters that use confusing jargon and terminology (Burd, 2018). In this report, out of 455 colleges offering an unsubsidized student loan, they found 136 unique terms used for that same loan, including some that even omitted the word “loan.” Another of their key findings also includes that seventy percent of the letters failed to disclose the difference between scholarships, grants, work-study, and loans; instead, they grouped all aid together. The lack of consistency and clarity is not allowing students to make financially informed decisions.

author: Rosemarie Arroliga Noguera
paragraph: 9 The project “Let’s Talk Student Loans” studies the financial jargon used in student loans. Such vocabulary was applied in a deck of definition cards. The dynamic was executed with college undergraduates that had previously taken on a loan. Students would blindly choose three cards from the deck and the interviewer would ask if they knew the definition of the words in each of them. In this experiment, thirty-five percent of the students were able to define their three corresponding words correctly. The rest had some trouble identifying terms such as “award letter”, “APR (Annual Percentage Rate)”, “Accredited”, among others.

author: Rosemarie Arroliga Noguera
paragraph: Let’s Talk Student Loans, Rosemarie Arroliga

author: Rosemarie Arroliga Noguera
paragraph: Despite the misunderstanding of terms and misleading jargon, making a loan is not difficult at all, and neither is it to get a credit line in addition to the student loans. According to an article in the New York Times from 1991, “companies that offer cards will do everything in their power to see that the students acquire them”. The article explains how credit card companies come to campuses and offer incentive gifts for students to sign up such as t-shirts, food, etc, and waive the requirements that normally applicants would have to meet to apply (Witt, 1991). This is a practice that continues to happen today. With technology, it's even easier to apply for credit through a website, app, or any digital tool.

author: Rosemarie Arroliga Noguera
paragraph: 10 We Have No Formal Education About It Education plays a very important role in this issue. According to a personal finance index, 2018 data shows that most Americans lack financial literacy, hence they are not prepared to make appropriate financial decisions (O’Neill, 2018). Personal finance is not required to be taught in high school in most of the states. Current data from Next Gen Personal Finance shows that only five states require a separate one-semester course on personal finance in high school and only twenty-one states overall require taking a course that integrates this content into an existing course. The content offered during these courses touches upon budgeting, investing, and savings by applying these concepts to a day to day life. However, students from lower-income families are affected. Across the U.S. “in schools where 75% of students are eligible for free lunch/reduced lunch, only 3.9% are required to take a one-semester personal finance course” (2020 Survey of The States).

author: Rosemarie Arroliga Noguera
paragraph: Personal Finance Class Material 2019 Connecticut

author: Rosemarie Arroliga Noguera
paragraph: 11 Many Americans have been struggling with personal finance for years now. Some of this struggle may come from the lack of financial education. When looking at the timeline of our life stages, the first encounter where we come across major financial decisions it's when they transition from high school to college. The use of student loans can be helpful for a start and is a great opportunity for us to create a professional career even when we do not have the necessary funding; however, most students do not get a high paying job right after college, and this debt takes years to pay back (qtd. in Agnew et al, 2015). Attending college can easily put a student into debt. In the future, this tends to set them back as they graduate and try to start an independent life. It is unrealistic to expect them to make good student loan decisions if they are not prepared.

author: Rosemarie Arroliga Noguera
paragraph: Our Interaction With Money Has Changed The concept of personal finance was initially used by Hazel Kyrk in the 1920s. Kyrk was one of the first women to obtain a doctorate degree in economics. At the time Kyrk would refer to this concept as family consumption or consumer economics (Beller and Kiss, 1999). Today, personal finance is defined as “the activity of managing your own money” by the Cambridge dictionary. In the finance world, it is defined as the management of one's money and personal financial decisions that include budgeting, savings, investments, and retirement planning. However, the concept of money has been around long before personal finance was established. Ancient civilizations would trade goods and services as a payment system. In 1580 BC Egyptians started trading with silver, copper, and gold and used a weighting system for such metals as currency (Mark, 2017). The concept of money started as an exchange of goods and was later translated into assigned value to specific objects. The first invention of a coin is recorded to be in China in 1000 BC, and other iterations of the concept in Turkey in 600 BC. The invention of the first paper currency wasn't until 1368 in China and later spread to Europe. Later on in history, we start seeing new iterations of currency like the invention of the credit card. A concept that facilitated the initial notion of credit practice by many years already. Credit first started in the 1800s when merchants would

author: Rosemarie Arroliga Noguera
paragraph: 12 extend credit to farmers in order to allow them to pay their bills until they had enough time to complete their harvest and to sell their cattle (Grossman and Grossman, 2010). A more recent iteration of currency is money as we know it today. In the year 2000, PayPal was introduced as a money transfer service. It was one of the first tools from online banking. Sequentially, many online banking tools started to grow rapidly, changing significantly the way we interact with money on personal, social, and familiar levels. Most banks launched their own online tools and many private ones also arose. Services like Venmo in 2009 and Zelle in 2017 broke down the barriers between online banking and facilitated online transactions for people. As technological advancements progress, the interactions around finance and money also change. Instead of going to a bank teller to obtain cash or make a deposit, technology allows us to do everything from any digital device such as our computer or phone.

author: Rosemarie Arroliga Noguera
paragraph: My Own Savings Book Home Federal Savings and Loan Association of Chicago ​ Educational tools also evolve. In 1941, the Home Federal Savings and Loans Association of Chicago created a savings book for kids. They gamified the experience of learning about money by explaining to kids how coins worked. Kids would put coins of different sizes and values into this book and the bank would invite them to come in and open a savings account to start their savings with the coins collected in the book. Another very popular mainstream example is

author: Rosemarie Arroliga Noguera
paragraph: 13 Monopoly — gamified experience of buying and selling properties that also uses the concept of pretend money. Monopoly does not represent the reality of the majority of Americans because buying and selling properties is not part of their daily financial decisions; however, interacting with pretend money is helpful for kids to count and familiarize themselves with currency. We now have some digital experiences where we can learn about the concept of money or even personal finance management. Next Gen Personal Finance provides a series of academic tools and digital games that teach this subject. They provide this material targeting students in high school. Nowadays there are also extensive financial apps available to keep track of our spending, budgeting, or savings such as Mint, Albert, Robinhood, etc. with the vision of providing guidance in personal finance but not necessarily teaching it. These may be used as a convenient resource for young adults as they do provide financial assistance in budgeting and savings. Most financial apps can guide users as a starting point but a deeper understanding is needed to make decisions in other areas such as loans and retirement.

author: Rosemarie Arroliga Noguera
paragraph: We Learn It From Our Parents Our perception of money often develops during childhood. The beginnings of our idea development start in the early stages of our lives. As kids, we are exposed to education from different sources such as our parents, household, school, professors, etc. Our perception of this matter reflects what we see in our surroundings. Childhood is when we start creating our own habits about personal finance depending on what we observe. The project “Teachings of Money” explores the origins of our learning about money. Participants share stories from childhood, some very short, and others quite detailed. Within them, traditions and special moments are revealed. Memories such as piggy banks, money from grandparents for Christmas, or trips with parents to the bank, all formed special moments that are remembered to this day.

author: Rosemarie Arroliga Noguera
paragraph: 14 Teachings of Money, Rosemarie Arroliga Technology has changed many behaviors and the logistics of different systems. With more accessibility to information and connectivity, new learning opportunities are available. When we try to remember how we learned about money, many experiences or tools might come to mind. Robert Kiyosaki, the owner of a financial education company and the author of the popular book Rich Dad, Poor Dad, explains how the teachings from our parents have such a big impact on our financial lives. In his book, he uses parables to explain how his biological father and another paternal figure taught him about money from two different perspectives. “One of the reasons the rich get richer, the poor get poorer, and the middle-class struggles in debt is that the subject of money is taught at home, not in school. Most of us learn about money from our parents. So what can poor parents tell their child about money? They simply say, ‘Stay in school and

author: Rosemarie Arroliga Noguera
paragraph: 15 study hard.’ The child may graduate with excellent grades, but with a poor person’s financial programming and mindset.” (Kiyosaki, 1997) Kiyosaki argues that in the current education system, parents are the main source of education regarding personal finance. If parents do not have good financial habits themselves, how are they supposed to transmit good and effective habits to their children? As mentioned before, experiences have a significant impact on learning. We save childhood memories that have special meaning to us in one way or another. How can memorable experiences during childhood aid the understanding of personal finance?

author: Rosemarie Arroliga Noguera
paragraph: We Should Design Tools To Help Parents Do That Learning tools have evolved since Hazel Kyrk first introduced personal finance in the 1920s. Whether digital technology-driven or physically colorful board games, there are some factors that remain constant. What all of these tools have in common is narrative. Lange quotes Jean Piaget, a Swiss psychologist who was recognized for his studies in a child’s mind and whose work significantly influenced twentieth-century education. “Knowledge has to be learned from experiences, like by putting a block under a blanket and then finding out that it still exists, or, in a later stage, by dropping blocks from different heights and seeing what happens” (qtd. in Lange, 2018). The Growing Avocado story serves as a guideline for young soon-to-become parents providing them with a more comprehensive way of teaching personal finance skills to their children. Since the home is the first source of financial education in a person's life, parents and caregivers become the first instructors on this topic to children, and in some cases the only one. This capstone is aimed at new parents that are struggling to transmit good financial habits to the next generation of their family. Financial illiteracy can be present in multiple generations until someone decides to act on it. Parents can use this story to bond with their children in the process of learning.

author: Rosemarie Arroliga Noguera
paragraph: 16 Story Book and Website With this research, I designed a children’s book that through storytelling and narrative explains the core principles of personal finance in a simplified manner. The book’s main purpose is to inspire kids to familiarize themselves with the concept of money in a seamless way. The story was originally designed as a printed book to be read to children by their parents in order to create a special parent-child bond and create a stronger memory of this content through bedtime stories. In times of COVID-19, the book has been transformed into an interactive website in order to adapt to the special circumstances. This allows parents and kids to have access to this tool from their digital devices at home while practicing shelter-in-place. This tool provides parents with an activity to share with their children while having extra time at home during the quarantine. Experiences have a powerful connection to learning. Elizabeth Kensinger, a Psychologist known for her research in emotion and memory, explains how memory depends on engagement, and pleasure is not always perfect. “It has long been known that experiences that elicit arousal are more likely to be remembered than experiences that do not evoke an emotional response”. With this body of work, the main goal is for The Growing Avocado to help parents create new positive memories with their children that can, in the future, impact their understanding and engagement of personal finance.

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: The journey to completing this study hasn’t been easy one. But it’ll be a past I’ll look back at, rejoice and think, “You were able to do it then, you’ll be able to do anything.” (Reference: Covid-19, 2020) I’m thankful to the special people who’ve supported, challenged, and inspired me throughout my time at Pratt Institute, empowering me to question, learn and think critically. I’m tremendously thankful to faculty members–Santiago Piedrafita for guiding me at the very start of this journey. Jean Brennan, for the invaluable suggestions towards the trajectory of this thesis. Jarret fuller, for his refreshing views on the subject. Gaia Hwang, for leading me towards bolder perspectives, sharing her in-depth knowledge, and for never allowing a dull moment in any discussion. I’m grateful to my colleagues for the countless critiques, conversations, and jokes about our work. The endless rants and sometimes encouraging discussions on group chats–We were a fun thesis class! Special thanks to my buds–Regina, Nancy, Neha, for all the encouraging texts and video calls. For enduring this together. Thank you Mom, Dad, and Sheethal for always thinking of me. I’m lucky to have a friend, critic, emotional support, the best roommate, and husband, all in one. Thanks, Sai, for the innumerable hours of listening to me talk about my work.

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: 5 Keywords Digital Interface A digital interface is a medium through which humans interact with computers. Interfaces represent an amalgamation of visual, auditory, and functional components that people see, hear, touch, or talk to as they interact with computers (digital devices). Human-Computer Interaction Human-computer interaction (HCI) is a multidisciplinary field of study focusing on the design of computer technology and, in particular, the interaction between humans (the users) and computers. Microcopy Microcopy refers to small bits of copy usually found in digital communication channels like mobile apps, websites, and emails that help clarify, explain, and instruct users on what to do and how to do it. Some examples of Microcopy are Clear descriptive and instructional text explaining how to use, subscribe, or order. Copy that guides users as to how to fill out a form – headings, error messages, form labels, and drop- down menus. Onboarding tutorials that walk users through their first user experience. Validation, confirmation, password reset and Thank You emails. Call-to-action button copy. Error (404) pages. User Interface The user interface (UI) is the point of human-computer interaction and communication in a device. This can include display screens, keyboards, a mouse and the appearance of a desktop. It is also the way through which a user interacts with an application or a website. Technical communication Technical Communication is a broad field and includes forms of communication that provide instructions about how to do something regardless of how technical the task is or even if the technology is used to create or distribute that communication. (society for technical communication). They make information more useful and accessible to

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: 6 those who need that information. Technical writing Technical writing is the practice of documenting processes, such as software manuals or instructional materials. Empathy Empathy is the visceral experience of another person’s thoughts and feelings from his or her point of view, rather than from one’s own. Empathy facilitates prosocial or helping behaviors that come from within, rather than being forced, so that people behave in a more compassionate manner. Genre Genre is any form or type of communication in any mode (written, spoken, digital, artistic, etc.) with socially-agreed-upon conventions developed over time. Generative design Generative Design is the capability of Mechanical Computer-aided Design (MCAD) applications to autonomously generate one or more geometric designs to satisfy specific objectives and constraints. Generative editorial design tools Hemmingway app, Grammarly.com, Autocomplete feature on google, Predictive text feature on IOS. Authorship The concept of authorship is basically a variable, which is not defined by spontaneous attributions but simply by our way of handling texts, or by the place of discourse within society. (Barthes and Foucault) Electronic literature Electronic Literature is created digitally and is meant to be viewed digitally. Electronic Literature is something that is created digitally and is meant to

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: 7 be viewed digitally. As explained by Katherine Hayles, “Electronic Literature is generally considered to exclude print literature that has been digitized.” A few other terms for electronic literature are cyber literature such as literary machine (Nelson, 1993), cybertext (Eskelinen, 2012), digital literary (Hoover, et al. 2014), digital media (Ryan, 2013), digital literature (Sanz, 2007) Human-Centric Design Human-centered design is a creative approach to problem-solving and the backbone of our work at IDEO.org. It’s a process that starts with the people you’re designing for and ends with new solutions that are tailor-made to suit their needs. Human-centered design is all about building a deep empathy with the people you’re designing for; generating tons of ideas; building a bunch of prototypes; sharing what you’ve made with the people you’re designing for; and eventually putting your innovative new solution out in the world (Ideo). Humanlike Human, humane may refer to that which is or should be, characteristic of human beings. In thus describing characteristics, a human may refer to good and bad traits of a person alike ( human kindness; human weakness ). The word once had also connotations of courtesy and refinement (hence, the application of humane to those branches of learning intended to refine the mind). User Experience design User experience (UX) design is the process design teams use to create products that provide meaningful and relevant experiences to users. This involves the design of the entire process of acquiring and integrating the product, including aspects of branding, design, usability, and function. Prompting - The action of saying something to persuade, encourage, or remind someone to do or say something.

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: 8 Codify arrange (laws or rules) into a systematic code. arrange according to a plan or system. Authoritative writing The definition of authoritative is someone or something having power, influence or the right to control and make decisions. The purpose of authoritative writing is to provide specific instructions and guidelines to direct readers to a certain direction. Repurposing content in writing Repurposing a piece of content, however, you’re doing one of two things (or both): changing the format of the content, and/or changing the target audience for the content. Examples of changing the format include turning a series of blog posts into an ebook, or taking key content from a webinar and placing it in an easy-to-consume infographic. Human language Human language is also the only kind that is modality-independent; that is, it can be used across multiple channels. Verbal language is auditory, but other forms of language—writing and sign language (visual), Braille (tactile)—are possible in more complex human language systems. Wayfinding Wayfinding refers to information systems that guide people through a physical environment and enhance their understanding and experience of the space. Natural Language a language that has developed naturally in use (as contrasted with an artificial language or computer code). Plain language writing Plain English is a style of writing that enables the reader to understand

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: 9 the message the first time they read it. It uses short, clear sentences and everyday words without unnecessary jargon. Cognitive structures Cognitive structures are mental processes that individuals use to process and understand information. They organize information for learning and recall. Cognitive structures are integral for comprehension and memory. Generative Relating to or capable of production or reproduction. Denoting an approach to any field of linguistics that involves applying a finite set of rules to linguistic input in order to produce all and only the well-formed items of a language. Ethical Relating to moral principles or the branch of knowledge dealing with these. Ethics is a set of moral principles that govern the behavior of a group or individual. Language The method of human communication, either spoken or written, consisting of the use of words in a structured and conventional way. a system of communication used by a particular country or community. Electronic Literature Electronic literature or digital literature is a genre of literature encompassing works created exclusively on and for digital devices, such as computers, tablets, and mobile phones. Algorithm A process or set of rules to be followed in calculations or other problem- solving operations, especially by a computer.

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: 10 Speculative Engaged in, expressing, or based on conjecture rather than knowledge. Literary sense of, relating to or having the characteristics of humane learning or literature, text Digital interface A digital interface is a medium through which humans interact with computers. Interfaces represent an amalgamation of visual, auditory, and functional components that people see, hear, touch, or talk to as they interact with computers (digital devices). Content The things that are held or included in something, the material dealt with in a speech, literary work, etc., as distinct from its form or style.information made available by a website or other electronic medium. Medium An agency or means of doing something. the intervening substance through which impressions are conveyed to the senses or a force acts on objects at a distance. Linguistics The scientific study of language and its structure, including the study of morphology, syntax, phonetics, and semantics.

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: Microcopy is a collection of words and phrases found on Digital Interfaces that guides users to interact with devices effortlessly. Although many experts have defined their own sets of rules and best practices, there are limited academic and speculative discussions on this growing writing form [1]. In this article, Microcopy or Interface Writing [28] is distinguished into an evolving genre in the digital literature that embodies empathy to disguise authoritativeness, uses generative tools, multiple authors, and a syndicated or ready-to-print copy style and thus becoming consequential in the discussion of authorship in the age of electronic literature.

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: This contemporary form of communication is emerging between people, technology, and communities. Deconstructing and critically examining this language to consider what forms it will take in the future is paramount.

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: This study proposes concepts that require critical thinking with which future research can be performed. It hopes to bring more democratic systems to embody, deconstruct, analyze, and critique this language with views on influencing human language and behavior, transparency to its readers, moral, and ethical values in interacting with digital experiences.

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: Anytime I download a new app or unpack a new digital device, I have the urge to rush through the onboarding screens, anchoring my eyes on the word ‘Skip.’ While browsing through websites, I’ve realized my unconscious capacity to automatically clear the screen from Pop-up messages containing Ads, Newsletters, Register or Sign up, Privacy, Notifications. With practice, it feels embedded in my muscle memory to locate the words ‘allow’ or ‘block’ or ‘Ok’ or ‘No thanks’ to move on quickly. In another instance, While I was scanning through articles on the ‘medium’ site, I was interrupted by a pop-up message that read ‘Pardon the Interruption, We’ve seen you here before. Let’s Make it Official.’ Even before I read the rest of the information, I knew that the message was asking me to ‘Register’ or ‘Sign-in’ to an account to continue using the website any further.

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: What are these words that appear to have differing literal meanings, almost like speaking in code and making me respond in a nearly natural way?

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: 15 Medium Website: Sign in/Register Pop up Dialog box

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: As Johanna Drucker states it “Language is a symbolic system, full of implications” (1998)[2]. To understand the digital language structures and small sets of words on the screens – their symbolic, metaphorical, and associated meanings, it is essential to introduce Microcopy. Microcopy is a small group of words in a user interface that instructs and motivates the user to act and provides feedback on the actions taken. These words and phrases exist in various categories across screens and have distinct characteristics of purpose, tone, voice, visual appearance, meanings, and connotations. Some of the types of Microcopy are Information copy, Headlines, Subheaders, Body copy, Interaction copy, Captions, CTA buttons, Notifications, User boarding, Menu copy, Error messages, Tooltips, Product-generated emails [3]. Since its identification as an integral part of designing User Experiences around 2009 by a designer Joshua Porter, [4] there have been multiple articles and books on its usefulness, feel-good approaches, and best practices. Although many experts have defined their own sets of rules and best practices, there are very few to no academic and speculative discussions on this growing writing form. While it is common knowledge in the Human-Computer Interaction community that before a digital platform becomes public, it is prototyped and well tested. However, unlike Literature, Art, and Design works, the User Experience field does not have the stage for design critique. 17 Books on Best practices of Microcopy, UX Writing.

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: “We are all the offspring of the modern movement, and we want to know more about our intellectual forebears. We need our roots. We need to know who the protagonists were, what prompted them to operate as they did, who their clients were, and how their rapport generated a climate of creativity which affected others.” Massimo Vignelli

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: It is essential to allow more democratic systems to embody, Analyse, reform the current trends of Microcopy, language structures, tone, patterns in the hopes of creating responsible and sustainable future forms. It is rather apparent that Microcopy is the language that makes our devices relatable. Microcopy’s mirage qualities of manifesting empathy and associations to the actions make it invisible as a language. Although easily ignored, it proves to have a strong influence on human behavior and actions.

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: 18 IN THIS DISCUSSION, I EXPLORE MICROCOPY WITH RESEARCH THROUGH DESIGN METHODS AND IN THE LENSES OF LITERATURE, PSYCHOLOGY, AND SYSTEMS IN BOTH HISTORICAL AND CONTEMPORARY CONTEXTS. MICROCOPY, AS A LITERATURE GENRE, A CODIFIED LITERARY SYSTEM USING GENERATIVE WRITING AND DESIGN TOOLS [29] AND ITS RELEVANCE TO AUTHORSHIP. MICROCOPY’S SIGNIFICANCE OF INTUITION AND INFLUENCING BEHAVIOR. FINALLY, MICROCOPY EMBODIES EMPATHY AND APPEARS HUMAN-LIKE.

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: This research argues that Microcopy, in its invisible character, is a contemporary and evolving genre in the digital literature that wears on empathy to disguise authoritativeness; it uses generative tools, multiple authors, syndicated or ready-to-print copy: it is consequential in the discussion of authorship in the age of electronic literature. Deconstructing and critically examining this language to consider what forms it will take in the future is paramount.

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: What is a Genre? A genre is a recognizable pattern for providing specific kinds of information for an identifiable audience demanded by circumstances [6]. For analyzing Microcopy as a literary form, it is essential to determine what defines a genre in a slightly progressive nature. A genre is a typical response to a rhetorical situation; it becomes a recognizable pattern for providing specific kinds of information for an identifiable audience demanded by circumstances. Genres have a purpose and a pattern. For writers, using the patterns of a genre accepted by readers for accomplishing their goals allows them to establish a working relationship with readers. For readers, genres help organize information so that they can easily make sense of what they are about to read [6]. In Design, the genre also responds to the place it appears, the layout, artwork, and appearance [7].

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: 20 Microcopy, as a genre, would realize the purpose of enabling interaction, motivate immediate action, appeal to users’ feelings, engage with emotions, and is written to have a personal tone. A guideline in the Writing section of Google’s Material Design states, “Skip unnecessary punctuation. To help readers scan text at a glance, avoid using punctuation in places where it isn’t necessary. Avoid using periods in solitary sentences.”[8]. English has unfolded continuously; it is a living language. Now, these internet brands play a pivotal role in driving this evolution in the digital world, further influencing our connection with the language. At a lecture in 2013, author of Internet Linguistics, David Crystal, said: “The vast majority of English is exactly the same today as it was 20 years ago.”[9]. However, the way we communicate – the punctuation (or lack thereof), the grammar, the abbreviations we use – is reliant on context and the means with which we are interacting. We don’t need to accommodate the way we put together sentences in a piece of journalism to the casual style of texting on a phone or social media as they’re different. Language structures change and propagate due to uncertainty and external agents, such as the influence of visual limitations, tone, and voice of the brands on the internet and the use of slang and commonplace abbreviations [9].

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: Apple’s Human Interface Guidelines Website; Terminology

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: “Keep interface text clear and concise. People absorb short, direct text quickly and easily and don’t appreciate being forced to read long passages to accomplish a task.” This is one of Apple Terminologies’ guidelines on how the copy on any Apple digital product should follow [10]. “Reveal information progressively and as it’s needed.” is written on Google’s Material Design for Writing [8]. The Pace of our interactions with these platforms, which is part of the broader topic of Internet reading, hyperlinks can have a significant influence on the way we read, observe, and articulate information. It is obvious to imagine short stories and poems on the internet that use one word to less than three sentences as an outcome of these codified systems of writing.

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: 22 Applying the attributes of Genre in Literature and Design mentioned earlier, to the evolving system of writing on the interface, we can argue that Microcopy follows a definitive purpose, visual pattern, distinctive grammar and language structures, and layout according to its place (brand and product). It is unique in comparison to the News or Academic literary forms, where the primary purpose goes beyond information and communicating. Its didactic nature makes it a subset of Technical Communication that deals with instructional, technical, and scientific writing. Technical illustrations define steps and identify the parts of a product, letting users focus on interacting with a product more accurately [11]. Microcopy covers communication that presents instructions about how to do something, making it more usable and accessible.

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: Historical information, introspection, and interpretation is important to understand the motivations of earlier writings of the same nature and to start a dialog around it. In order to understand the evolution of Microcopy within the Technical communication realm, it is critical to analyze it in a historical and contextual framework. The beginning of this evolution dates back to the early designs of minimal and understandable user manuals, followed by building machines that did not require manuals.

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: “We are all the offspring of the modern movement, and we want to know more about our intellectual forebears. We need our roots. We need to know who the protagonists were, what prompted them to operate as they did, who their clients were, and how their rapport generated a climate of creativity which affected others.” Massimo Vignelli

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: Marcus Vitruvius’s De architecture, in 15BC, was among the first systematic writing or a Manifesto on ‘how to build.’ It offered clear and concise instructions on how and where to construct a house, which was later translated in 1914 into a more understandable User Guide by Morris Hicky Morgan. In 1684, the first comprehensive manual in any language was published on printing. Subtly different from any instruction manuals before, this had a DIY tone and suggested that its readers could learn a new trade at home in their spare time. It provided ingenious methods offering something new and relatively democratic: an agency, skill, command for anyone who could read. 24 25 Plain language writing was designed to ensure the reader understands the text quickly, easily, and thoroughly. It avoids verbose, convoluted language, and jargon, similar to the current trends of microcopy. The Plain English Campaign (PEC) was founded in 1979 by Chrissie Maher as advocacy in promoting Plain-language persuading organizations in the UK and USA to communicate with the public in a language they could easily comprehend [12]. Similar to PEC, the term Natural Language became popular when the evolution of computational technology demanded other languages. Natural language then became defined as anything in contrast to artificial or constructed language to other communication systems in nature. In 1955, Henry Dreyfuss, an American industrial designer and author of the book, ‘Designing for people,’ endorsed that it was necessary to ‘fit the machine to the man rather than the man to the machine.’ He promoted a new way of looking at a human’s relationship with technology – familiarizing the machine and complex city structures with the understanding of common people [13]. 1980 was the time of the PC Revolution and the web revolution when computers became popular in households. A significant problem arose when the owners of these computers did not know how to use the programs after installing software [15]. This led to the Design and writing of instruction manuals for computers and software programs. The manual Design took a complete minimal turn when John Carroll published his book ‘The Nurnberg Funnel’ in 1976. His research steered at making computers more usable for ordinary people. He outlined the philosophy that instead of focusing on the needs and values of the system designers, it has to shift onto the end-user [14].

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: 26 Vitruvius Ten Books on Architecture, Plain English Campaign logo, The Ninberg Funnel book, Early Computer mouse, Microsoft’s Start Menu with early Microcopy, Apple’s first Graphical user interface.

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: The invention of the mouse enabled the users to interact with the interface and opened up new avenues of possible interactions [16]. The touchscreen was another addition of how language was going to change on the interface. These inventions enabled the use of natural language and words like Menu, search, click, cancel, etc., for the user to understand and navigate through the interface instead of using a Computer language (code) to engage with every step. Human-Computer interaction was a term that became popular in the 1980s, where researchers studied the Design and use of computers and focused on making interfaces easily usable. The need arose to write better manuals for people to understand machines and later came the concept of using these products intuitively and without an instruction manual. Apple Macintosh, in 1987 allowed hyperlinking between various pages within a document and was probably the first use of the word “hyperlink.” In 1990, Windows Help, introduced with Microsoft Windows 3.0, had widespread use of hyperlinks to link different pages in a single help file together, in addition to a visually different kind of hyperlink that caused a pop-up help message to appear when clicked [17]. 28 In 1993, Don Norman, an American researcher, professor, and author, joined Apple inc. and introduced the term User Experience Architect and Human- centered products. He wrote the book, ‘The Design of Everyday Things,’ where he states that Design must optimize the conduit of communication to make the experience better. At the beginning of the web revolution, the Internet experiences were designed by engineers, and later dedicated designers used language to help with action, movements around the interface [18]. One such medium and tool that has evolved during this process of using technology intuitively is language in the form of Microcopy. Presently, the User Experience Design field is made up of more than just an architect; it comprises of designers, researchers, and writers.

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: Authorship in Technical Writing and Microcopy With the numerous emerging digital products and brands, the copy associated with the product or interface does not belong to an individual but instead attached to an organization. For Instance, Mailchimp has a predefined style and tone of language used throughout its applications. Writers use generative writing tools like Hemingway App, Grammarly App, and codified processes like card sorting, A/B testing, Heuristics. New programs like speakhuman.today [19] have algorithms to toss out microcopy for various scenarios. Microcopy uses generative writing and editorial tools, evolving a codified system of vocabulary, with multiple authors, syndicated or ready-to-print copy to produce its content for the products.

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: 29 Technical writers and editors are responsible for composing innovative, practical, and compelling texts for products and organizations. It involves collaborating with groups of writers, editors, or subject matter experts as opposed to working autonomously. Reworking, building on, or reusing existing text rather than producing something entirely original (Jessica Reyman, p 347) With Microcopy and Interface writing, Authorship can go beyond the actual written words and phrases in comparison to other Literature genres. Microcopy would involve the context of a device, the Tone, Visual language, Purpose, feedback, and performance effectiveness. Multiple authors contribute to creating these instruction words – Researchers, User Experience designers, Content Writers who support in creating the product. These words create experiences that involve taking into account the Design of interface, navigation, the pace of interaction, and more. Words belonging to one product may not be attributed to one author, especially with technology companies owning their style of Microcopy. Jennifer Slack, David James Miller, and Jeffrey Doak (1993) argued that researchers and practitioners have much to gain by conceiving of the Technical Communicator as an author. They debated that Technical communication researchers and writers must accept the articulation model, which allows Technical writers to take the role of an author who actively contributes to the creation of meaning: “the articulation view allows us to move beyond a conception of communication as the polar contributions of sender and receiver to a conception of an ongoing process of articulation constituted in (and constituting) the relations of meaning and power operating in the entire context within which messages move” (p. 169). They make a compelling argument for the value of Technical Writers as contributors to the “articulation of meaning,” in explaining that by assuming the status of Authorship, they would be free to share in ways that allow more than merely conveying facts. This model could be a method to guide Digital experience writers collectively to elevate microcopy to a genre of writing.

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: 30 This would further help to drive more democratic arrangements in the control of writing – drawing consciousness to the blinded topic of dark patterns in instruction writing, tone, the importance of understanding that words guiding actions can influence behavior and lifestyle [20].

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: The Autocompletionist is a generative writing project inspired by rules and limitations. The writing tools included the Predictive text feature on the Apple iOS device (Notes app), a physical dictionary to locate words in alphabetical order concerning parts of speech (noun, verb, adjective, etc.), Grammarly App and Google search engine. The random short sentences are results of self-crafted rules (sentence structure codes) following the use of the generative writing tools. It imitates the syndicated copy style of Interface Writing, showing multiple authors through various mechanisms, limitations of size, structure, and grammar.

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: In this form and materiality design exploration, I use the default design tools within Illustrator as a metaphor to explain the characteristics of ‘Microcopy.’ Simple iterations of form are used to represent Transition, Repetition, Progression with an interplay of type to draw parallels to the features of Microcopy and demonstrate that use of generative tools. Using the Risograph emphasizes on the generative concepts of the language structures used on our devices.

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: The human mind, having evolved over a long period with advanced structures, is considered highly complex. Most of these complex operations in the mind are subconscious and hidden. “Conscious attention is necessary to learn most things, but after the initial learning, continued practice and study, sometimes for thousands of hours over a period of years, produces what psychologists call ‘overlearning.’ Once skills have been overlearned, performance appears to be effortless, done automatically, with little or no awareness.” [18]. Designers take an enormous effort in building consistent micro-interactions so that interfaces can appear invisible to the user.

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: 44 Microcopy acts as an integral training point for users to learn what actions to take within an interface. Most essential interactions, like exiting an app, uploading information, removing information permanently, are consistent in almost all digital platforms. They use the same words, such as ‘Go back,’ ‘Submit,’ ‘Send,’ ‘Delete,’ etc. Uniform patterns allow users to recognize words and create an illusion of having an intuitive command over the machine, making these instrumental words invisible. These words that are embedded in our subconscious minds affect natural language and linguistics.

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: Microcopy or words on screens are commonly borrowed from the ordinary communication language and actions in the natural world to make them recognizable. For instance, ‘Add friend’ on Facebook would mean to include a person into your group of existing members so they can view your activity. Once Facebook became popular, It was common to hear someone say ‘Add me on Facebook,’ or ‘I’ve never spoken to her, but she’s my friend on Facebook.’ Another example on Facebook is the option to ‘Poke’ a ‘Friend’ as a way of greeting them or letting them know you are thinking of them. The ‘Poke’ feature evolved from being cute to creepy to non-existent over the years. One would imagine the actual meaning of the word itself–What does it mean to poke someone? Are you sharing a secret? Are you being creepy or hurting them? And what does it mean to ‘poke’ someone virtually?

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: 45 ‘Liking’ System on Facebook. These connotations to the word seem to have mixed representations of the natural and digital world. The Microcopy ‘Like’ or similar expressions and icons common in many platforms can sometimes be confusing to use. The word ‘Like’ means to have an inclination or preference for something/someone. But seeing it being used on social media has a nuance to the word. How does one ‘Like’ a post of someone announcing a person’s death? When someone shares a photo of police brutality, would liking the post mean support to the brutality or unstated disapproval?

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: According to the hypothesis of Linguistic Relativity, also known as the Sapir-Whorf Hypothesis, the structure of a language affects its speakers’ perspective and cognition. While several standpoints are supporting and criticizing this hypothesis, for this discussion – words and phrases used on the interface are considered to influence human thoughts and behavior [21]. Instagram surfaced the system of ‘following’ and ‘unfollowing’ people and topics. Similar to ‘adding’ friends, groups, and topics, Instagram uses a straightforward didactic language. To follow someone on social media is quite similar to the word used outside of it – being aware of their everyday activities, important life events, personal relationships, emotions, opinions. Their thoughts and ideas could inspire their ‘followers’ and so began the rise of the notorious ‘influencers.’

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: 46 Cancel Culture The ‘Cancel Culture’ is another example where the word Cancel is associated with discarding people, particularly celebrities, politicians, or anyone who takes up space in the public consciousness. In other words, culturally ‘blocking’ someone from having a prominent open platform or career because of their offensive actions. There are many references and sentiments to the emergence of the word ‘Cancel’ within this context [22]. Still, it is hard to ignore the similarity to hitting the ‘Cancel’ CTA button to withdraw a subscription or a software installation. In many cases, canceling someone happens overnight with strong opposition through personal messages, comments, memes, calling out the person for their offensive behavior expecting them to disappear much like the urge to ‘exit’ an Advertisement pop-up box in a web page instantly. The theory of immediate reaction upon clicking on a Microcopy in an interface to this ‘Cancel Culture’ situation seems palpable.

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: 47 Qualitative Research - Cultural Probe

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: How does Writing on the Interface and Microcopy influence the way we tell stories and behave? How does a user perceive ‘Microcopy’ during and after their interaction with their everyday digital devices?

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: Goals of this project To Understand Microcopy in the literal sense. Removing Microcopy from the digital space and placing it in a conversation/ sentence/ verbal function. Examining connotations behind the words - reader’s understanding of it and hoping to find new insights through these exercises.

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: 48 Five experiments with ‘Microcopy.’ The participants are briefed on what ‘Microcopy’ means [the words/phrases that help users navigate through any digital interface] and are given the five exercises that allow them to observe Microcopy intentionally and apply it in specific scenarios.

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: 1. Random page Prompt The participants were given a page of text and asked to mark all the words that evoke emotions/ thoughts/feelings in them and find an obvious way to mark those words. Insights Each participant found a way to represent their chosen words– erasing the other words/sentences, tearing, or scribbling over the words. All of the participants had picked mostly the same words that resonated with them while reading the entire text. They chose a word or group of words based on the clarity it provided to the sentence and the meaning of the passages.

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: 49 2. Click Tap Touch Prompt The participants were given two cards with words taken from different app/ interfaces pasted on them. The group was then told to create a sentence/ scenario using these words. Insights This exercise helped me understand the nuances behind the words from the digital space and their representations in verbal communication. Some words that are commonly attached to specific online platforms were used in the same digital appearance. For Instance, the word Premium would mean several things from a commodity that has a superior quality or its application in finance. Here the word was used with a Music application Spotify, “I do not own Spotify Premium.” 50 3. Less is more Prompt The participants were first asked to read through a passage and rewrite it to a maximum of 10 words without changing the intent of the sentence. In the next step, they were asked to show a friend/colleague their rewritten sentence and ask their comment on the nature, structure, and understandability of the shorter sentence. Insights The participants found it hard to reduce it to 10 words and still keep the paragraph’s actual intent. Because of the limitation of words - the new sentence seemed rigid, limited, and grammatically incorrect. The participants felt that with more words, the sentences could sound polite and still be able to convey the exact meaning. 51 4. Reel real words Prompt The participants were first asked to pick a digital interface and write down a set of interactions in linear order. They had to note every word they clicked on to go to a new page or a result or any other interaction. Based on each word they clicked - they had to consider the literal meaning of the word/ what action it prompted them to do or a feeling they had while interacting. Insights The participants wrote down literal meanings/actions/feelings of the word/ Microcopy. A few pointed out unnecessary steps to an action or words that did not make sense in that particular interaction. They associated brand names with a few words. Some results showed that the words did not provoke any feeling or seemed invisible because they were used to the interface (muscle memory).

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: 52 5. Quick talk Prompt The participants were asked to document their interaction with a stranger in the forms of words/sentences or drawings. The communications to be observed could be in any form – digital, verbal, non-verbal. Insight The drawings of the conversations felt brief and direct, and words were only added for better understanding. Short phrases from the interactions were noted for clarity. Virtual conversations with chatbots looked similar to scripted dialogues of cashiers/retail shops.

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: The words on the interface instruct, guide, and motivate the user to perform actions. Instant reactions from the machine follow these prompts making Microcopy appear practically invisible. The micro-interactions, guided by the Interface writing in time, influence human language structures and behavior. This design project is an exploration built by repurposing Microcopy through a series of protest posters, as they take on a role advocating social justice and equality. Its attribute of authoritativeness and inciting immediate results are made visible through this experiment.

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: It is common knowledge in the HCI and User experience field that Microcopy is written in Plain language to make the interface friendly, understandable, and easy to interact with. The words intend to hide the didactic and instructional quirk of the copy while imitating Human conversations and materialize empathy towards its readers. They consider Tone, Humor, Clarity, Visual style, Anatomy of the screen, and Audience to its place (brand) [23]. Ideally, Brands strive to make certain aspects of Microcopy reflect their values and leave most of the repeated interactions consistent with other digital interfaces. While these words are made to seem understanding and empathic to its users, its generic quality and irrelevance to the user’s real-time situation could cause frustration [24]. Here are some examples of commonly used Microcopy that attempt to embrace empathy encouraging the user to perform actions contrary to their feelings.

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: We don’t need research to tell us, saying ‘No’ can be hard. It can be an absolute joy to refuse something without the fear of judgment. All that pent- up guilt and fear of conflict for wanting to say ‘no’ can be unleashed on the internet, by clicking or tapping on ‘No thanks.’ But lately, this interaction is becoming exhausting. Understandably, businesses need subscribers and monetary supplies to last, and one way to do that is to ‘ask’ visitors on their websites for it. With existing privacy pop-ups, notifications pop-ups; additionally, these marketing and ad pop-up windows create a cluttered and stressful experience to navigate. The one ‘relief’ option you look for and expect to hit (like it’s in your muscle memory) is the ‘no thanks’ button. Confirmshaming is a tactic used by corporations to shame users into buying. This has been discussed as a part of Dark patterns, and designers have called them out in the past [25]. We can be relieved to be interacting with fewer – ‘No thanks, I don’t want to know about gardening. No thanks, I’d rather pay full price. No thanks, I’m not into savings. No thanks, I’d rather not keep up-to-date. No thanks, I don’t like delicious food. No thanks, I’ll miss out. No thanks, I don’t care enough.’ In another light, Deconstructing this copy linguistically and grammatically can discuss its influence on behavior and language. ‘No thanks’ without a comma simply means you are ungrateful or not giving thanks. It might have intended to mean ‘No, thanks’ like when someone offers you tea, and you politely refuse it with a ‘thanks.’ Relating microcopy and it’s characteristic of imitating empathy, it does not reflect human conversations. Would a simple ‘No’ sound more functional and polite (tone-wise) than an arrogant ‘no thanks?’

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: ‘Learn more’ would express the same as ‘Learn about,’ which means to become knowledgeable or to discover some particular information about someone/something. This copy usually leads the reader to largely stacked information of secondary or tertiary importance that did not fit in the aesthetics of the primary page [26]. It’s not rare to feel restless or avoid the ‘learn more’ page solely because of the loading time, even if it’s only microseconds unless it is significant. If Microcopy was all about embracing empathy and made it sound human-like, then imagine being told to Learn ‘more.’ Just plain mannerless straightforwardness.

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: Hearing someone say ‘Oops’ would either mean they are sorry, or they are sorry for you that something went wrong – in a subtle, ‘I don’t care much, but I also want to show that I feel for you’ way. The dictionary states that a person would say it during an apology, a clumsy act or blunder, dismay, or even a surprise. But what does it mean to read it as a message on a computer screen? I’m confident that I may not be the only one who feels frustrated when coming across ‘error: invalid code’ but changing that to ‘Oops, something went wrong’ doesn’t solve the problem [27]. It is displayed in plain language and mimics a human conversation; however, in many parts of the world where English is the second language, Oops is still an unrelatable word for dismay. An example of how Microcopy embodying a human-like communication fails to adopt universal principles of design. In another aspect, It also depends on the context. For instance, when a user adds a long list of items to their cart after spending significant time, while simultaneously doing other tedious activities only to be greeted by a message saying – “Oops, Seems like your cart is empty.” This would be an entirely inappropriate way of displaying dismay even for English speakers who recognize the use of ‘Oops.’ The more appropriate way for the empathy-embodying Microcopy to respond to this situation would be to say – “Holyyyy.. Ohhhmyygodddd, I messed up. I’m sorry!!! You have every reason to be pissed at me right now!!!” Just saying – at least that sounds real.

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: The images show a juxtapose of two kinds of instructional copy - Microcopy, that is current and evolving, and another, wayfinding, with a historical and a physical presence. This exploration, portrayed as a dialogue between the two kinds of copies, shows the differences in the tone, structure, clarity, and associations.

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: Understanding the pain points and the User’s journey while reading words that falsely imitate a friendly or understanding conversation can be problematic. With the surfacing of Conversational User Interface, the role of Microcopy in CTA buttons and as Information labels can take an all- encompassing approach. A conversational user interface (CUI) is a digital interface that allows users to interact with a product based on real-life human communication principles. In other words, Users can tell the device what to do verbally or in writing instead of looking for information in the graphical interface. There are two types of CUIs – Chatbots and Voice assistants that have begun advancing into everyday life [30].

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: Since ‘Talking’ is a fundamental part of being human, it is easy to assume that Voice assistants and Chatbots should be designed to talk and behave like humans. Social conversations are complex and can be unpredictable. People converse to build relationships, and to get things done; these two purposes are connected [31]. Research suggests that pursuing natural conversations with machines that sound like humans can become an unnecessary and burdensome objective. However, the future of CUIs will inevitably be inspired by Human conversations similar to the current properties of Microcopy [31].

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: 69 Some of these Voice assistants and chatbots already mimic the tone, humor, didactic quality, clarity like the words on the screens. This would further advance itself to being conversational and dynamic. While the copy used for these systems would be longer and be devoid of the current limitations of space and anatomy of the screens, the role of empathy becomes obvious. Generic style of copy or Dark Patterns like Confirmshaming could cause instant frustrations because of the personal aspect of the CUI. Users then will be compelled to use the same language structure to give orders to the machine changing their typical speech structure to a short and rigid manner. This alteration to Natural Human language without Pauses, ‘Errs,’ and ‘Uhmms’ can presently be observed while giving commands to Chatbots or Voice assistants [32]. “Alexa, Play Illayraja.” or “Siri, set the alarm for 6 am.”

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: These sentences mimic what is currently the structure and grammar of Microcopy. How would this routine in alteration of tone and language structure influence social conversations and among different age groups?

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: As a response to the topic, The Invisible copy project is an archive of words and phrases used in digital platforms. These words are elaborated with the literal, figurative, and associated meanings they carry along with examples to support the deconstruction. This study is a contribution to the larger community of designers, copywriters, content and brand strategists or simply users who can identify with these words, allowing discussions on evolving vocabulary of Microcopy, tone, connotations, visual components, new approaches – sharing resources and creating a codified body of work while deconstructing the existing system.

author: Siona Balaji
paragraph: Microcopy or Interface Writing is discussed beyond its practicality, guidelines, and effectiveness to the product and audience. By proving its distinct characteristics such as visual style, tone, and grammar associated with technology organizations, codified systems using generative tools, [33] and multiple authors, the state of Microcopy is discussed as a form of Writing Genre [6]. The study talks about the evolution of Microcopy from Technical communication in a historical and contextual framework. The relationship between Microcopy and Intuition is discussed in small but significant details, along with elements of the writing that influences Human behavior. Another contributing factor to the uniqueness of Interface Writing and Microcopy is its ability to adapt empathy and mimic human conversations, which are discussed with examples and design explorations. Since this form of written language is a relatively new concept, this study hopes to develop a critical thinking framework with which future research can be performed.

paragraph: The research in vernacular architecture has a long history and detailed records, including object-oriented, socially-oriented and culturally-oriented studies. Instead of focusing on the history of vernacular architecture, the analysis of how did scholars research this topic is more important when applying this concept to the design area. paragraph: The focus in vernacular architecture can be traced back to the eighteenth century. Under the influence of the Arts and Crafts movement, European and American antiquarians and architects conducted many studies concerned with local architectures. As for the non-western culture, explorers and colonial officials contributed abundant resources in a time of rapid colonization. When encountering buildings that were fundamentally different from what they were familiar with in their hometown, they wrote down detailed information including patterns, materials and skills in dairies and reports that become 12 significant studies of several cultures. 12Oliver, Paul. Under this condition, the vernacular design by common people that reflects personalities, culture and traditions is an effective tool to provide a new vision towards the current design process, subvert the design principles, and help reconsider the relationship between designers and audiences — in other words, using vernacular as a tool to decolonize design.

author: Siyu Tian
paragraph: Decolonization originally means “remove (a place) status.”24 from colonial Under the contemporary context, decolonization represents a new recognition and attitude: In the western world, society has been built upon the colonization of other nations. “To decolonize and not just diversify curriculums is to recognize that knowledge is inevitably marked by power relations......Yet decolonization is not just about bringing in minority texts but also about texts.”25 how we read ‘traditional’ Minority cultures are ignored in history or classified into extraordinariness, especially in the design area. 1. History The work designers make is inspired by taste, and the taste is often derived from what we’re exposed to during our upbringing. But design values and history is taught through a canon; that accepted pantheon of work by predominantly European and American male designers that set the basis for what is deemed “good” or “bad.” The authority of the canon has undermined the work produced by non-Western cultures and those from poorer backgrounds so that Ghanaian textiles, for example, get Figure 19 Ghanaian textiles Photography by Jacek Sopotnicki 2019

author: Siyu Tian
paragraph: After the historical and contextual analysis of vernacular design and decolonizing design, I raise my question: How might we use vernacular design as a tool to decolonize design? To be more specific, how to bring audiences' personal or cultural (especially non-western culture and underrepresented cultures) identities into design work by employing a vernacular design process? How might vernacular design impact the relationship between designers and their audiences in the future? Whether design work should have principles or not? Why? If the answer is yes, what are design principles and by whose standards? If the answer is no, how to subvert existing principles? To answer those questions, I deconstruct the vernacular design into several parts: tradition and culture, materials and tools, and authority and control. Then what does it mean to

author: Siyu Tian
paragraph: Traditions can be seen as creative processes of transmission through which people interpret past knowledge and experiences to face the challenges and demands of the present. It is continuously re-negotiated from generation to generation. Vernacular designers learn from the past and apply new techniques and thinking only where previous failures exist. When the tradition meets a new environment, “these common people making common designs are unafraid to create what they need for today and then update or change it when needs and desires tomorrow.”35 change Successful experiments today will become a tradition in the future. Culture, a topic that is often brought up alongside with tradition, continues shaping the constitution of relationships and identities, to influence the communities, and to frame the experience of a large world.

Vernacular design is resistance to designers by common people. Instead of accepting the basic canon of authority, vernacular identifies design works as social representations and links them to personal and coherent cultural systems of values and beliefs. Designers have the power to influence people’s behavior. And since the design process is almost completely in the hands of designers, it's significant for them to realize it. “Control is undermined by ambiguity, choice, and complexity because subjective factors in the user become more effective, and the user is invited to control.”36 participate. author: Siyu Tian
paragraph: Though we have co-design, audiences' right to have choice and opportunity of participation is still given by designers. The role of the designer is still the essential component of a co-design project. Designers provide ways for people to engage in the project as well as providing ways of communicating, testing and sharing insight. In vernacular design, the absence of designers inspires me what might be the relationship between audiences and designers in the future where designers only serve the role of assistant. In my opinion, it is necessary to “weaken” the “dominance” of designers. In addition to the reason above, designers are also influenced by the surrounding unconsciously. And the design work which changes people's behavior will be the most direct reflection of the impact. When design students accept their education in school, that is where all issue starts. Because of the structure in the education system and the authority of professors, students seldom question those design thinking, rule and canon taught by their teacher or applied in the commercial world. The project Reverse is a project exploring the meaning of decolonizing authority and canon. Students were asked to pick up a piece of artwork in Whitney Museum, use its visual language, but express their argument. I chose Bondage Baggage Prototype 4 by Maia Ruth Lee. She re-creates packages that she grew up seeing in Nepal. It models a condition of the self, as well as concepts of self-preservation and the economic oppression of developing countries. Since decolonization is about reimagining something beyond the current system that we exist, and this art piece is in the museum, which represents some aesthetic principles, I make a counter piece that subverts all of those rules. For example, the original one is symmetric. Mine is asymmetric. As for the process, I asked people on the street whether I can pick Siyu Tian 25 up something in their cart and gave them some food for exchange. That's the way I got those ropes and waterproof covers. And the boxes are from the refuse room in my apartment. In this process, I question what is the canon and by whose standards? What can be put in a museum? And why?

author: Siyu Tian
paragraph: Through the thesis practices, I found my way of decolonizing design by learning from vernacular design. And I realize that to decolonize design is not just about breaking old and existing rules. It’s about realizing the principles, criticizing, and forming designers’ own beliefs.
