author: Siyu Tian
paragraph: The capstone project is an online class for designers and design students. Instead of teaching my audiences what I find, I show them my process and guide them to find their ways of decolonizing. Siyu Tian 27 Figure 29 Decolonizing Design Online Class Screenshot Siyu Tian 2020 The frame is a metaphor. The whole process is about how to destroy it and build a new frame. And every design decision I made for this website is based on my understanding of decolonizing design. In this class, audiences will explore pre-existing theories and concepts for decoloniality and decolonizing design. Class activities consist of readings, exercises, and experiments in the making. In the first section, after reading articles and listening to some interviews, they need to visualize their thoughts about decolonization under certain requirements. However, the self-critique will focus on the requirements rather than the final work. It is much more important to discuss the reason for setting up requirements and the messages hidden behind them, which leads to the second section. Audiences are asked to question all the design rules they have met, whether in school or commercial world, even the tools. Why is everyone using Adobe Software? In the third lesson, audiences will start to decolonize design. Since there are so many elements connected with design, which makes it hard to decolonize, the first step is to deconstruct it. At the same time, they keep on asking who defines design? Who makes those rules? Who follows? And why? Finally, with a one-hundred-day project, they will observe, research, document, explore, and finally decolonize an element in the design.

author: Siyu Tian
paragraph: Decolonization is a new and vital topic. It is necessary to widen all design-related concepts and methods so that they can be attached to all people with distinctive cultural backgrounds. At the same time, the vernacular design reflects personal perspectives, local culture, and traditions. Therefore, I will discuss the possibility of using vernacular design as a tool to decolonize design through my thesis. This thesis talks to designers and design students who desire a critical approach to design and culture, try to find a better way to participate in the community, engage audiences, and reposition their practice in a more critical framework. Practitioners who are interested in reshaping current creative practices and deconstructing the hegemonic knowledge system are also my target audience. Design can change people's behavior. Designers have to be responsible for their works and need to be aware of that influence. The power system in the design process, the role of the designer and design theory are always controversial. In response to the existing practices in design history, I want to create a new vernacular design process that shaped new thinking. Instead of making people into designers, designers might regard themselves as common people. They're not an objective problem solver. The design-thinking framework follows an overall flow of understanding, exploring, and materializing. Within these large buckets, it has 6 phases: empathize, define, ideate, prototype, test, and implement. By using different methods in each phase, designers can complete a project. For example, conducting research, collecting data, observing, and doing interviews are common ways to develop knowledge about audiences. However, instead Siyu Tian 29 of being an observer and outsider of the community, designers regard themselves as a resident or even living in the neighborhood for several days is considered a better way of “researching.” There is the saying that “nobody knows you better than yourself.” It's definitely true that audiences know themselves, the problems, and their living environment better than a designer. Therefore, having an audience-leading design process and putting the designer in a lower position might be the future of design. This thesis doesn't deny the necessity and importance of having designers or the existing design theory. But I want to show a new perspective towards the objects, materials, tools, constraints, traditions, and cultures by presenting a set of strategies. When using those strategies in design practices, designers will explore a new relationship with their audiences and the responsibility of being a designer.

author: Wan-Sian Lai
paragraph: Recognizing the limitations of form factors and gestures in hardware interface design and interaction design, the thesis attempts to use suppleness as a methodology to innovate the design of interaction in human-computer interaction (HCI). The research incorporates the adoption of soft concepts and materials in the art and design field. Through exploring contextual literature and soft characteristics within materials and philosophy, softness is framed as an ideology of fluidity, responsiveness, and flexibility. Tactilely, we sense the quality and characteristics of softness intimately and instantly. The intersection between our bodies and materials happens at the moment when our skin makes contact with other surfaces. Therefore, the body becomes an entity for us to perceive softness. The theory of embodied interaction (Dourish 2004) and affective computing (Picard 2000) serves as the fundamental research to speculate the possibilities of form in the realm of interaction design in a form of tangible interaction. The capstone project employs speculative and critical design approaches to identify future scenarios and experimenting with home surroundings, in turn, it stimulates the users to rethink their relationships with digital interfaces. Grounded in Metamodernism, the investigations hope to achieve the balance between various viewpoints, intersecting of tactility, whole body parts, and affection within the human. Based on the reviews of existing literature and various experiments on pliable materials, artifacts, and visualizations, my dissertation reframes the concept of softness, and extends to supple interaction and methodology. 10 11

author: Wan-Sian Lai
paragraph: Digitalization comes with the design of simplicity and effortlessness, but, at the same time, with the simplification of gestures and a significant loss of complex tactile experiences. As Heylighen argues “social and technological evolution is characterized by ephemeralization, an accelerating increase in the efficiency of all material, energetic and informational processes. (...) However, the accompanying "lubrication" or reduction of friction in all processes creates

author: Wan-Sian Lai
paragraph: a number of non-physical problems, characterized by the increasing instability, complexity and reach of causal networks, and therefore decreasing controllability and predictability.” (Heylighen, 2002) Interaction designers utilize programming to generate tactile feedback for digital interfaces in order to bring back the richness of tactility (Levesque, Vincent, et al.). How to design embodied body gestures and interactions, beyond augmented tactility? How to design interactions that allow us to express and receive affection? From the conceptual and material explorations of softness, this thesis questions the rigid form factors in hardware interface and interaction design. Petting a furry dog, holding a fluffy pillow, playing with slime, a breeze caressing the skin are all examples of soft interactions and experiences. Not only tactility but other senses can trigger the perception of softness. Softness, as an ideology, reminds humans to be sensitive about the capacity and embodiment of our bodies. The muscles are capable of restoring repetitive patterns as memory and responding to the environment. Based on the concept of “embodied interaction” coined by HCI scholar Paul Dourish, the embodiment is regarding the social presence and participatory status, surpassing a physical property, “having an (inter) active role in changing and becoming” (Gajendar, 2010). Consequently, interaction designers should not merely focus on the graphic user interface (GUI), but the values and meanings an interaction can deliver. An interface should be where the integration of sensors of the body and the technological devices. This research aims to reframe softness as a new perspective for future interaction designers, in addition, employs soft behaviors to design interactions with technology. 12 13

author: Wan-Sian Lai
paragraph: Brief In Brenda Laurel’s book, The Art of Human-Computer Interface Design, she states “An interface is a contact surface. It reflects the physical properties of the interactors, the functions to be performed, and the balance of power and control.” Broadly, an

author: Wan-Sian Lai
paragraph: interface can be a space/surface of the intersection between any existence. What exists in that space/surface are power dynamics between the agents, environments, human minds interacting with each other. This thesis aims to pursue the balance and oscillation occurring in that space/surface as soft interactions. The notion of human passing through an interface between ideals and physicality has been long discussed. Narrowing down to digital devices, an interface is where our mind, body, the hardware come across. It could be one second, one action, but gradually accumulated to a continuous behavior, experience, and habit. Keramida Kimon further explained in his book, The Interface Experience, “Laurel's definition reminds us that the interface is a tangible foundation of the computer experience. (...) The user's experience of this place is not just in how one engages with or intends the surface, but in the whole of the machine computer will recognize the input and respond with feedback.” The line between whether humans adapt technologies or technologies adapt to humans is gradually blurring, therefore the distinguishment is unnecessary. The experiences should be natural as we grab a cup of tea, pet our cat, or chat with a friend. Thus the interactions are affectionate, responsive, and casual. Rosalind Picard’s Affective Computing concept has deeply influenced the HCI fields. “Her idea, in short, was that it should be possible to create machines that relate to, arise from, or deliberately influence emotion or other affective phenomena.”(Höök) What can softness as an affective core inspire the adoption of technology and philosophy in the HCI fields?

paragraph: Malleable material case study - Slime Temperature, moisture content, and density of material are factors to influence the degree of softness to stillness. Materials can exist in numerous status, yet more importantly, it is the capability of changing, adapting, and

author: Wan-Sian Lai
paragraph: transforming that define “softness” in this study. The first exploration of soft materials has examined paper, fabrics, cotton, clay, and glue, to eventually settle on the slime. (Figure 1) An inconspicuous and inexpensive material with a surprisingly large group of a devotee on social media, slimes render different perspectives of softness. Slime, as an alienated form, is a superorganic, multi-antennae existence that reaches the world with a solid move.

author: Wan-Sian Lai
paragraph: Figure 1. The New York Times article. Yael Malka. 20 21 For decades, slime has been the preferential material for science projects, teaching kids about viscosity, polymers, and chemical reactions. It leverages its physical properties as a simple affordable tool, a thicker slime that works like a lint brush to clean a computer keyboard and other small electronics for example. Fashion designers Adam Selman, Christian Dada, Jeremy Scott exploited the traits and form of slime and created a series of slime-inspired fashion artifacts. In Landlord creative director Ryohei Kawanishi’s 2019 show, sparked by Window 95 and the 90’s “Internet surfing” and referenced skate and surf silhouettes, models were directly poured over kaleidoscopic and multicolor slime. (Figure 2) It crosses the boundaries of science and art, and entertainment. In the first cable channel for children around the 80’s Nickelodeon show (Figure 3), You Can't Do That On Television, slime was used as the material for a prank: if someone said "I don't know" slime was poured all over them from above. It was typically dumped on a person's head in an act of either humiliation or celebration. The excitement and the relief were revealed on the face of the people who “got slimed.” While covered in slime, our senses are obstructed, the loss of control relieves people. In the article Why Slime Is Everywhere: A Cultural Compendium written by Alexander Iadarola, she explains, “Perhaps slime is appealing because it reconfigures flexibility as something pleasurable, even delightful, whereas for most people in the labor market, the day-to-day experience of bending to fit capitalism’s needs is nothing short of excruciating.” Slime is a neutral and mellow existence, linking people from different classes, and backgrounds. author: Wan-Sian Lai
paragraph: Figure 4. Fidget Cube. 24 Fiber Art Soft materials, with their lower density, can compass various components and be able to connect with diverse elements. In Silka P’s article Fiber Art and Its Scope, she defines “Fiber art refers to fine art whose material consists of natural or synthetic fiber, and other components, such as fabric or yarn. Placing focus on the materials and the manual labor on the part of the artist, aesthetic value over utility is prioritized.” Normally, fiber artists employ the method of weaving to reconstruct pieces of threads and materials. The technique of weaving is a gesture of connecting one piece to the other, gathering one end to the other, and congregating the thoughts and materials, symbolizing a soft action and communication. Soft thinking, therefore, can be defined as the acceptance of the opposite opinions and the power, the space to include differences, and the courage to listen and take actions. In Real Life magazine’s article Fiber Optics composed by Leslie L. Bowman, she quotes from fiber artist Louise Bourgeois, “I want to re-experience the past, I try to reconstruct it. Sudden recollections that are awakened by the senses tell you more than emotions.” Due to the softness within the materials, repetitive reconstruction, refinement, and transformation can be conceptually achieved. Weaving is a soft and tactile interaction with the materials, and the fabric is a soft interface where the thoughts weave in and out through a series of repetitive gestures. The flexible, elastic, and mellow shape and form is transformed into a visual language to express its ideology. “The 60s and the 70s brought an international revolution in fiber art. With the rise of the women’s movement and the consequences of feminist art, along with the birth of postmodernism theory, fiber art was reinforced.” As needling and weaving were usually reckoned as women’s casual and leisure activities, the textile and fiber were reframed and reborn in high art by the feminist art movements. Fabrics, yarn, threads can simply be categorized or even stereotyped as a feminine image. Nevertheless, the language does not constrain to quietness, but provocativeness. “Since the 1980’s, various fiber works, influenced by postmodernist theory have become more and more conceptual. The theory inspired pieces which confronted cultural issues such as gender, issues of politics and socially predetermined roles, identity, and so on.” British artist Sarah Lucas adopts soft elements, such as body parts, stocking, mattress in a provoking way to subvert the traditional notions of feminine, sexuality, and identity. (Figure 5) Soft material and fabrics convey gentleness and fragileness for her to conduct a soft-look yet unyielding weaving protest. It is subtle activism. Figure 5. While Modernism celebrates rational thinking, logic, and scientific process, Postmodernism advocates the belief that there is no universal truth and inquires and challenges the rationality of Modernism. Within the art and design fields, various approaches such as cubism, pop art, comic art, and main dadaism emerged. Wolfgang Weingart, a German graphic designer credited as the pioneer of New Wave typography, adopted a rebellious and chaotic approach which Figure 6. Herzog & de Meuron, Elbe Philharmonie. Copyright Herzog & de Meuron. was known as New Wave. “The results of Weingart’s experiments with Modernist principles were, in part, to expose the relationship of the person involved, and to push the design process towards something more akin to art, rather than a calculated process of expression of the structural components” (Outhouse, 2013). The term metamodernism appeared in 1975 when Mas'ud Zavarzadeh isolatedly used it to describe a cluster of aesthetics or attitudes which had been emerging in American literary narratives since the mid-1950s. Metamodernism is the philosophy or viewpoint that corresponds to the digitized, postindustrial, global age. In the realm of literature, “It is about the oscillation between the modern and the postmodern, history and historicity, optimism and pessimism, sincerity and irony, the concept and the material, the figurative and the formless, narrative and the plotless, discursive originality and individual intertextuality, meaning and meaninglessness” (Vermeulen, 2010). The writer later clarifies that rather than the swinging between binaries, Metamodernism supports more on dynamics and intensities between multidimensions. author: Wan-Sian Lai
paragraph: Figure 8. Emerging haptic technologies. 2000 2000

author: Wan-Sian Lai
paragraph: 1 Haptic Design Interface - Ming Kong 2 Google Glasses 2010 3 New Tangible Interfaces 4 Ontenna 2019 5 inFORM Present 2007 6 Ultrahaptics Figure 7. The timeline of physical interfaces evolution. 30 31 The evolution of the physical and personal interfaces (Figure 7) unfolds the microcosm of the impact through Modernism to Metamodernism. It indicates technology compresses time and distance as humans pursue convenience and efficiency. The buttons of electronics, digital devices, machines, and computers represent convenience, power, and control. Nevertheless, physical buttons became flattened and merely a symbol for the mobile phones after the touch screen was invented in the 1970s. By observing the timeline, the design of the mobile phone, mouse, and screen becomes thinner and more flat. Therefore, the action and motion of interactions between humans and devices are simpler and more abstract, while they simultaneously serve more complex functions. Nonetheless, emerging haptic technologies thrive during times when minimalism design is prominent, Without the physical tactile feedback of the button, we perceive less confirmation of making an action. Therefore, adding haptics or vibration alerting improves the user experience and improve the performance of the user. A new elastic conductive material (Pic 1) designed by Ming Kong can be molded into different shapes to create a tactile interface for digital modeling. Except for utilizing a malleable material, wearable devices allow other parts of the body to interact with the interfaces. For instance, Ontenna (Pic 4) is a tiny hair clip, designed by Japanese company Fujitsu, allows deaf people to sense sounds by slightly pulling their hair. The attached microphone of this device converts sound into haptic feedback. Both inForm (Pic 5) and Ultrahaptics (Pic 6) are interfaces that could be controlled via the air in real-time. Ultrahaptics adopts invisible ultrasound to generate tactile feedback so that users receive tactile feedback by gestures. As a result, the future interfaces might be more intangible and the interactions tend to be gesture-based or voice-controlled. With technology as an extension of our physical body, it’s vital to investigate our bodies and minds internally and externally. By identifying these innovative haptic technologies applied on interfaces, the future digital interfaces could be softer and manipulative. 32 33

author: Wan-Sian Lai
paragraph: Under the field of tangible interaction in HCI discipline, the study investigates the “tangibility and materiality of the interface, whole-body interaction and the embedding of the interface and the users' interaction in real spaces and contexts.” (Hornecker) Additionally, from the perspective of affective computing, the explorations and research emphasize emotion as one of the critical factors in the

author: Wan-Sian Lai
paragraph: realm of interaction design. In Moggridge’s book Designing Interactions, Crampton Smith introduces the four dimensions of interaction design languages: words, visual representations, physical objects/space, and time. Kevin Silver, an interaction designer added a fifth dimension: behavior. This thesis draws heavily on the third and fifth dimensions: physical objects/space and behavior, without addressing the usage of languages, visual representations, for instance, microcopy or graphic user interface (GUI) design. Examining from philosophy to contemporary art, softness and suppleness are reframed as an ideology broadly by means of explorations on various pliable materials and the flexibility and embodiment within the human body. Apart from the design of the interaction as the primary capstone project, the process of the experiments incorporate the documents of visual communications and artifacts. The concept of softness is grounded in the philosophy of Metamodernism, correlating the phenomenon of digitalization and the traits extracted from the malleable, stretchy, and elastic materials. 34 35

author: Wan-Sian Lai
paragraph: Speculative design The term Speculative design was coined by Anthony Dunne, professor and head of the design interactions program at the Royal College of Art, and Fiona Raby, professor of industrial design at the University of Applied Arts in Vienna.

author: Wan-Sian Lai
paragraph: Before speculative design, in the mid-’90s, they also formed the concept of critical design. In 2013, Dunne and Raby popularized the concept in their book, Speculative Everything: Design, Dreaming, and Social Dreaming. They explain “[Speculative design] thrives on imagination and aims to open up new perspectives on what is sometimes called wicked problems, to create spaces for discussion and debate about alternative ways of being, and to inspire and encourage people’s imaginations

author: Wan-Sian Lai
paragraph: to flow freely. Design speculations can act as a catalyst for collectively redefining our relationship to reality.” I believe design plays a vital role in social responsibility and using imagination to create the possibilities and break the boundary is one of the methods. As the mainstream design follows the rigid rules "determined by [capitalist] government and industry”, speculative design can be defined as the

author: Wan-Sian Lai
paragraph: process of addressing big societal issues with design processes and systems. (Dunne, Raby, 2013) The aim of speculative design is not to predict the future, but to give broader perspectives and discussions in order to inspire the creator. To use speculative design as an inspiration, and create the undiscovered and unfamiliar, it could be answering broader questions: What’s the impact of design for the whole world? How could we use design to influence future cultures and technologies? How to design interactions between technology and humans? “Where typical design takes a look at small issues, speculative design broadens the scope and tries to tackle the biggest issues in society.” (Dunne, Raby, 2013) Every design is for the future, but once it is created, it becomes history. Critical design matters, but it’s vital to acknowledge its position in design and the criticism of it. One of the most common criticisms of critical speculative design is that, as a practice, it comes from a position of white, northern European, culturally colonizing, patriarchal privilege. (Prado de O. Martins & Oliveira, 2014) She claims “SCD (speculative critical design) needs to be held accountable for its political and social positions; it urgently needs to escape its narrow northern European middle-class confines; it needs to talk about social change; it needs more diversity, both in its visual representations and in the practitioners in the field.” Unreality could be naive but inspiring, yet the execution of ideas should be delicate and sensitive. 36 37 01 Soft Series - Soft Flag By adopting the speculative design methodology, Soft Flag conceives of a way to transform a country’s flag into a soft shape (Figure 10) through the use of a VR or AR device (Figure 9), as an attempt to soften boundaries and borders between territories. The intention behind the inquiry: what is a country? In addition, how does your nationality define you as an individual? Users can screenshot the picture, and share it on social media to express their activism in a soft way. As a Taiwanese, Soft Flag is a project dedicated to those who have multiple nationalities and whose nationalities are not fully recognized. Manifesto National identity is not subjective like a nickname. It is a recognized fact over which individuals have little control. When other countries do not officially recognize your country, such a status shapes your identity as the (non)citizen of such an entity. This is especially true when traveling. Airports, borders. Where are you from? Perhaps you have a sense of belonging somewhere. Maybe it has as much to do with where you live, as your genes or ancestry. What if we break the lines on the map that divide countries? What if we offer tenderness to fight, and vulnerability to be brave? What if we utilized instability to create diversity?

author: Wan-Sian Lai
paragraph: Figure 9. The Flags on an AR device. Figure 10. National flags and Soft flags. 38 39 02 Soft Series - Soft Computer When observing and mapping the evolution of the personal digital device, it manifests that the form and shape tend to be predictable and rigid. Our bodies are designed to be flexible and stretchable, yet the digital devices/electronics are mostly solid and rectangular. This project aims to use soft material as a way to discuss these findings. By intervening in the scale and material of an object and proposing it as a personal device, this project explores new forms of interaction. By modifying the “body” of the voice-controlled devices, such as Google Home or Amazon Echo Dot, the interactive installation hopes to shift the visual ideology of what a digital device should and can be. The project attempts to utilize the replacement of the visual and material representations to reinforce a new approach of interaction. In the paper, An Embodied Approach to Designing Meaningful Experiences with Ambient Media, Tan and Chow mention “the notion of embodied cognition provides designers and researchers with valuable insight into how the body-mind interplay can influence meaning-making during embodied experiences.” Soft computer attempts to build an embodied experience and interaction by enlarging the physical scale of the “body” of the voice-controlled device and transforming the solid shell into soft fabrics. In Sarah Pink’s Doing Sensory Ethnography, she mentions “... the body not simply as a source of experience and activity that would be rationalized and controlled by the mind, but itself as a source of knowledge and subsequently of the agency.” [14] Based on the previous discussion on the concept of embodiment, I support this theory and believe in the wisdom of the body. Furthermore, the inclusion of the whole body parts to participate in challenges the one dimension interaction of the voice-controlled devices.

author: Wan-Sian Lai
paragraph: Figure 11. Labled with "My name is computer." Figure 12. Wansian Lai, Soft Computer, 2020. 40 41 Tracing Figure 13. Wansian Lai, Handy Repetition, 2020. “Inherent in tracing is the activity of revealing, of exposing the underlying structures, arguments, and assumptions of an issue. Second, tracing is an activity of ‘mark-making.’ To trace is to follow and record the presence and movement of an artifact, event, or idea. Within the context of the construction of publics the tactic of tracing can be defined as the use of designerly forms to detail and communicate, and to make known, the network(s) of materials, actions, concepts, and values that shape and frame an issue over time.” (Disalvo, 2009) The manifestation and trace of a phenomenon shape and frame the topic by revealing the insightful facts and stories. These projects hope to visualize and physicalize the ethereal sense of tactility and gestures.

author: Wan-Sian Lai
paragraph: 01 Handy Repetition In Henri Bergson’s book Matter and Memory, he declares, “The two forms of memory are the past that survives as a bodily habit, or as an independent recollection.” The first one contains the trait of habit and it is stored in the body through automatic and repeated actions. The other type of memory exists in the form of an image, and can’t be repeated again. Inspired by Bergson’s ideas, I documented the most common five hand gestures used to interact with the touch screen by tracing down the moves on different materials and tools. It allowed me to analyze phone usage in public and private space. The five gestures are tapping, swiping up, swiping right using a thumb, swiping right using a forefinger, and long-press/ finger authorization. (The last one is a gesture only for iPhone users.) Although the actions are constant, by using different tools and tracing on numerous materials and surfaces, a myriad of possible patterns can be created. 42 43 01 Visual Brush This project aims to visualize the tactility and textures of a brush. By printing out the various textures and patterns generated by the brushes on the tracing paper, and inserting the collection in the general brush, the combination demonstrates the movement and textures in an extremely literal and honest way.

author: Wan-Sian Lai
paragraph: Figure 14. Wansian Lai, Modern-day Diary of Gest ures, 2020.

paragraph: Mimicking the poem format, this poster attempts to create a “Modern-day Diary of Gestures.” By using the traces of gestures on different surfaces as words, the poster documents the interaction of gestures with my phone within two minutes. The interactions with touch screen phones are written by the handy repetition of Figure 15. Textures created from the brush. touching, tapping, and swiping, blending into this visible literature piece. Figure 16. Wansian Lai, Visual Brush, 2020. 44 45 Figure 17. Wansian Lai, Melting Screens, 2020.

author: Wan-Sian Lai
paragraph: 01 Melting Screens This work is inspired by Dali's The Persistence of Memory or known as more descriptive titles “Melting Clocks” and “Soft Watches.” The process is aimed at stirring the discourse around interaction design by inquiring the status quo of the form and usage; it places design in the role of a critic of itself. Inspired by the physical properties of melting glass, I created phone size shapes to experiment with the concept of stillness and softness, reality and unreality within a shapeable material. 46 47 Capstone Project In the journal Grounded Cognition, Barsalou mentions “Extensive linguistic evidence across languages shows that people talk ubiquitously about abstract concepts using concrete metaphors.” He suggests psychology researcher Raymond W. Gibbs Jr. and cognitive linguist and philosopher George Lakoff and Mark Johnson believe that “People possess extensive knowledge about their bodies (e.g., eating) and situations (e.g., verticality), and that abstract concepts [such as love] draw on this knowledge metaphorically.” Therefore, rather than creating new gestures, the selected postures are concrete actions, for instance, to touch, to eat, and to hug. During the research process, I used a culture probe to understand the ideology of “softness”. I collected from the participants a list of things and objects (Figure 18) that would relate to softness. The list informed the design of these home interactions. The capstone project intends to adopt three familiar and intuitive gestures and to communicate via casualties. As mentioned before, interaction contains messages. Through interacting with a plant, a pillow, or food in a natural and casual way, the user can trigger an action on a specific electronic device. There are three embodiment of this idea:

author: Wan-Sian Lai
paragraph: Figure 18. Things related to soft ness collect ed from culture probe. 48 49 01 Plant-light emphasizes the gesture of touching and the softness of a live plant. By touching the plant, the lighting device can be turned on and off. Technically, it cooperates with the live plant and lighting device by utilizing a smart plug (IFTTT), Arduino board, and capacitive sensor. After plugging in the smart plug in the socket with the lighting device, touching the plant triggers the sensor to send data to Adafruit IO linking with the IFTTT. An IFTTT connection allows users to pull data from Adafruit IO feeds into the rest of the Internet or store data from the Internet into your Adafruit IO account. 02 Scroll-eat uses the action of eating and the sense of taste as a supple interaction as a metaphor of how we absorb the information on digital devices. It connects a scale to a personal device allowing the article on the website or feeds on social media to scroll while the weight of the food decreases. 03 Musi-cuddle functions as a supple interaction with a soft pillow. It turns the music player or speaker on and off. Listening to music is an auditory approach to relieve affections and emotions, and the combination with the interaction with a pillow delivers the sense of soft and tender tactility physically within an interaction. Using speculative design approaches, my work identifies future scenarios and experiments with surroundings at home, in turn, stimulates the users to rethink their relationships with interfaces of technological devices. As a result, by examining the outcome, this work wishes to incorporate the affections between humans into the explorations of supple interaction in the future. 50 51 52 53 54 55 56 57

author: Wan-Sian Lai
paragraph: 1. Responsiveness - Responsiveness means giving, constituting a response, answering, or quick to respond or react appropriately or sympathetically. As a human, paying attention to the environment and people is the key to be responsive, sometimes could be sensitive. Yet as soft materials, in essence, they naturally contain the quality of responsiveness. 2. Softness - 01. pleasing or agreeable to the senses: bringing ease, comfort, or quiet 02. having a bland or mellow rather than a sharp or acid taste 03. (1) not bright or glaring (2) having or producing little contrast or a relatively short range of tones 04. quiet in pitch or volume 05. smooth or delicate in texture, grain, or fiber Softness reveals the peaceful qualities, such as quiet, ease, smooth, but doesn’t mean weakness, surrender, and quitting, rather it carries strength. I refer to softness as a form that can include diversity, and be able to be remixed. Softness exists not only in cotton and fabrics but in metal and glass as well. 3. Haptic - Haptic is relating to or based on the sense of touch. (Merriam Webster Dictionary) Haptic could be reflected in the texture, material, and vibration. Haptics could not only be tactility and texture. Recently, the feedback design is the vibration and confirmation through the interfaces after users command an action. The difference in temperature could also be sensed through haptics. 4. Body memory - Body memory is the ability of the body to store memories unconsciously. It has a sensory recollection of traumatic experiences related to pain, discomfort, tension, and arousal. (Psychology Dictionary) 5. A matter that has qualities which give it individuality and this oscillation as a balance however; rather it is a pendulum swinging between by which it may be categorized. (Merriam Webster Dictionary) numerous, innumerable poles.” Softness includes responsiveness, which is a status of reacting and answering. I interpret Metamodernism as a constant swinging In this thesis, materials express different emotions, individuals and characteristics. dialogue and the communication of the extremes. Through getting familiar with different soft materials, the skills of mixing certain materials together are applied to making artifacts. Exploring soft materials and 10. Oscillation - Oscillation is the variation, typically in time, of some measure textures is a method to frame the definitions of softness in this paper. as seen, for example, in a swinging pendulum. “The term vibration is sometimes used more narrowly to mean a mechanical oscillation but sometimes is used to be 7. Gesture - The use of motions of the limbs or body as a means of expression. synonymous with oscillation.” (Science Daily) Regular vibrations can generate soft (Merriam Webster Dictionary) tactility, but rather than an aggressive and rapid speed, oscillation tends to move at a regular rhythm. The fact that our bodies are malleable and stretchable enables the countless possibilities of gestures. The softness of the body allows the movement for the human to express their emotions, identities, and characteristics. Gestures could be intentional and unintentional and every part of the body can create gestures. Even though they are unintentional, they contain certain messages for us to uncover. 8. Speculate - Speculate means to form opinions about something without having the necessary information or facts; to make guesses. (Cambridge Dictionary) “[Speculative design] thrives on imagination and aims to open up new perspectives on what is sometimes called wicked problems, to create spaces for discussion and debate about alternative ways of being, and to inspire and encourage people’s imaginations to flow freely. Design speculations can act as a catalyst for collectively redefining our relationship to reality.” (Dunne and Raby, 2013) I agree with Dunne and Raby that designers not only should address the issues of today but by taking a look into the future and ask, “How can we address future challenges with design?”. By speculating future scenarios and utilizing the imagination, the broader perspectives, and more possibilities could be created. 9. Metamodernism - Vermeulen and Van den Akker frame Metamodernism as follows in the article, “Notes on Metamodernism.”: “We [argue] that this modernism is characterized by an oscillation between a typically modern commitment and a markedly postmodern detachment. We call this structure of feeling “metamodernism”. According to the Greek-English lexicon, 60 61

author: Wan-Sian Lai
paragraph: In the field of hardware interface design (HID), the form factors of a mobile phone, a tablet, or a laptop tend to follow a rigid and predictable design standard. Asides from the physical specifications of components, the evolution of personal digital devices (Figure 7) illustrates the simplification of gestures and a significant loss of complex tactile experiences. They evolve through a physical bouncy button

author: Wan-Sian Lai
paragraph: to the prevailing touch screen nowadays, and furthermore the intangible interfaces. Grounded on Metamodernism, this thesis uses softness as an interdisciplinary methodology to experiment and speculative the affectional and communicative interactions. Softness and suppleness serve as an ideology, able to be materialized as emotional tactile and material, visualized as comforting

author: Wan-Sian Lai
paragraph: and complex patterns, conceptualized as affections, perspectives, and even mindsets. In addition, by using the proposals of speculative design, my investigation aims to provoke questions and discussions on the interaction between humans and haptic interfaces. As a recognition that “the shift in phrasing from Tangible Interface to Tangible Interaction was intentional, similar to the distinction between Interface and Interaction Design.”, the research addresses the design of the interaction, oppositely the visible interface. (Hornecker) Compared with visual and auditory recollections, tactility is less understood as a familiar factor in human cognition. Through research on the evolution of digital interfaces, this thesis focuses on supple interactions, as an understanding of the importance of responsive and haptic feedback is essential in the field of HCI, now and in the future. Building on the theory of affective computing originated by Rosalind Picard's paper Affective Computing in 2000, the research explores the emotions within the malleable, fluid, pliable materials visually and tactilely. With adequate experiments and knowledge, and mixing different disciplines of study, such as psychology and interactive media, this current investigation wishes to provide UI, UX, or interactive designers with new frameworks in the process of designing interfaces/interactions. Additionally, by speculating on human interactions with devices, designers could also have a broader understanding of the significance of tactile and reevaluate the relationship between humans and technologies. Giving the fact that the embodiment of the human body, the affections within the human consciousness, and the insights that soft materials and characteristics convey, this thesis hopes the implementation of supple and tender interactions can be produced in the future. 62 63

author: Wan-Sian Lai
paragraph: Metamodernism describes the hybrid and highly-saturated phenomena that define this digital and globalized epoch. Developing technologies and overload of information uncontrollability convey a state of ethereality. The evolution of physical interfaces (Figure7) is a microcosm in the evolution from Modernism to Metamodernism.

author: Wan-Sian Lai
paragraph: In the realm of Human-Computer Interaction, “Tangible Interaction has come to be the ‘umbrella term’ used to describe a set of related research and design approaches which have emerged in several disciplines.” (Hornecker) The applications of tangible interaction exist in various and cross-disciplines, such Conclusion as design, arts, and architecture. MIT Tangible Media Group commits to design human interfaces that employ physical objects, surfaces, and spaces as tangible embodiments of digital information and processes. Therefore, this thesis aims to discuss embodied interaction and tactility through the lens of suppleness. As an understanding of the emerging haptic technologies in the applications of physical interfaces, from vibration/haptic feedback of mobile phones to the application of ultrasound beams to “provide invisible tactile feedback in mid-air” (Ackerman, 2016), the physical interface could transform to be invisible, yet undoubtedly the fact that humans perceive the world through the tactile sense cannot be replaced. Hornecker mentions, “The shift in phrasing from Tangible Interface to Tangible Interaction was intentional, similar to the distinction between Interface and Interaction Design. It places the focus on the design of the interaction instead of the visible interface.” Considering the perspective of interaction leads to a broader viewpoint for designers and includes the discussion of the agents broadening the understanding of the environments involved in the whole experience. Interactions should never be entirely virtual since physicality is not replaceable. The virtual world is merely the augment of reality. It can be real, sensory, and effective. This thesis defines a soft interaction from the perception of tactility to the pliable material and broadly expanded to the comprehension of a design notion. The soft texture is a visual and tactile language delivering multi-sensory communication. Visually, the fibrous and silky images have a comforting power. The case study of slime suggests that its widespread adoption across science, education, art, design, and entertainment have formed a textual culture. The vision of squishiness, flexibility, and fluffiness somehow trigger comparatively intuitive and intimate sensors, such as taste or tactility. By weaving the soft fabrics and materials with the feminist art movements, Fiber art demonstrates the potent dimension of softness. Softness has been referred to as fragileness, obedience, and weakness, yet its quality of strength is more often neglected. The capabilities of 64 65 being rewoven and reconstructed illustrate the ideology of inclusiveness, diversity, and reimagination. How can interaction design embody these concepts? The expression of affection and emotion has been addressed as early as Darwin’s evolution theory and both pros and cons are recognized in history. Within the discourse of affective computing, Höök claims, “Instead of placing emotion as the central topic in a design process, it is now seen as one component contributing to the overall design goal. In particular, it becomes a crucial consideration as we approach design for various experiences and interactions.” Affection can be employed as one of the critical indicators in design, yet cautiously in order not to overwhelm the users. Metamodernism sets a framework to consider multiple poles around the subject and context, and moreover to balance all of them. “Newer conceptualizations of Tangible Interaction focus on human action, control, creativity, and social action instead of the representation and transmission of information” (Hornecker). To design beyond utility is to include humans’ nature, sentiment, and intuition as deliberations in the design process. These are factors that can not be quantified precisely, revealing the mass and chaotics of emotions. However, the Metamodern standpoint manifests the freedom to express as a human. A soft thread, a piece of fabric, a mixable slime enables the expression of emotions and relief of control. Softness can be materialized as textures, visualized as patterns, conceptualized as affections, perspectives, and even mindsets. The squishy, bouncy, and malleable materials can be disordered, complex, and intractable. Nevertheless, cotton sheets, silk pillows, marshmallows, clay rarely relate to intimidation, stillness, or segregation. The fluidity and softness allow soft materials to be squeezed in a dark corner, to be held tightly, to fit in various situations. Using the concept of softness to innovate the interaction design does not imply creating a brand new, instead, investigating negligence. 66 67

author: Wan-Sian Lai
paragraph: Softness could be developed to a broad concept and utilized to generate more detailed and structural methodology in order to provide a more refined ideology of softness for designers to put into practice. Specifically, it will be more comprehensive if investigating the factor of temperature under the concept of softness. Moreover, participation with people should be included to correlate and

author: Wan-Sian Lai
paragraph: develop the affections beyond one person. As a result, interaction design can be expanded through a further dimension. With the speeding of globalization, digitalization, and the advance of technologies, designers should be resilient and limber to the complexity of problems and issues to achieve the goal of sustainability and inclusiveness. 68 69

paragraph: _________________________________ MFA Thesis Candidate Urva Joshi, May 2020 3. Urva Joshi Andy finds her confusion utterly funny when shown two shades of blue. Andy calls it stuff with her naive ignorance. Miranda being the devil for perfection but also sincerity chuckles and what followed next was one of the mainstream instances that portrays the efforts that fashion industry births. She starts, “This “stuff”? Oh… okay. I see. You think this has nothing to do with you. You go to your closet and you select out, oh I don’t know, that lumpy blue sweater, for instance, because you’re trying to tell the world that you take yourself too seriously to care about what you put on your back. But what you don’t know is that that sweater is not just blue, it’s not turquoise, it’s not lapis, it’s actually cerulean. You’re also blindly unaware of the fact that in 2002, Oscar de la Renta did a collection of cerulean gowns. And then I think it was Yves St Laurent, wasn’t it, who showed cerulean military jackets? And then cerulean quickly showed up in the collections of eight different designers. Then it filtered down through the department stores and then trickled on down into some tragic “casual corner” where you, no doubt, fished it out of some clearance bin. However, that blue represents millions of dollars and countless jobs and so it’s sort of comical how you think that you’ve made a choice that exempts you from the fashion industry when, in fact, you’re wearing the sweater that was selected for you by the people in this room. From a pile of “stuff.”

author: Urva Joshi
thesis_title: The Paradoxes of Fast Fashion: Can We Overcome a Wicked Problem?
paragraph: The Fast Fashion industry occupies a major chunk in the fashion industry and the world economy, working on the principle of providing customers with the commodity as fast as possible without estimating the demand. It was only until the last 5-8 years that activists found out the severe loopholes in this industry from no third party regulation for trade leading to unfair labour exploitation, igniting a resistance towards these circumstances that were under the cover until now. This activism and resistance against fast fashion is fairly new as recently as 2013 vigorously. Despite these efforts, it somehow has not been successful to the point that it affects the functioning of fast fashion businesses. Thus, this design research intervenes on the resistance and highlights why the apparel industry fails to be ethical, what aspects make it unethical and what can shape the fashion system of the near future. How paradoxes in this system are so wicked and personal at the same time. It highlights the chase of consumers for something latest and the trickle down behaviour from social media and hence what will make them shift to the fashion sector that is ethical. The thesis looks into why resistance against fast fashion fails to be effective. But with a design intervention how can another form of resistance be more powerful as well as accessible. A methodology that reinforces ethical consumer behaviour questioning the vices and virtues of a commodity even if for a personal thought in the mainstream market and not as an alternate option born of activism.

This demand is based on many constraints. As this research looks into a very important sector of the economy, consumers and the buyer behaviour are inevitable. B=MAP is a model of behaviour established by B.J. Fogg. According to Fogg, three elements must converge at the same moment for a behavior to occur: Motivation(M), Ability(A), and a Prompt(P). When a behavior(B) does not occur, at least one of those three elements is missing.This behavior model highlights three Core Motivators: Sensation, Anticipation, and Belonging. Each of them having two sides: pleasure/pain, hope/ fear, acceptance/rejection. Ability and Motivation having trade-off relationships when it comes to performing behaviors according to Fogg. In order to perform a target behavior, a person must have the ability to do so and without a Prompt, the target behavior will not happen. Sometimes a Prompt can be external, like an alarm sounding. paragraph: Fast fashion’s seeds were sown way before this term came into existence. The mass has enjoyed it and so have the companies that made the fashion industry a $2.8 Trillion industry as of 2018. All good things come to an end, not necessarily, but they need to be disciplined if there are long term adverse effects from it. Outsourced labour has become an integral part of how a business works in the present capitalist society. But this outsourcing is imbalanced, mainly because of lack of standardisation due to different economies and different governments. Only after major backlashes in the current apparel industry due to underpaid outsourced labour and hostile working conditions, the resistance sparked off. A resistance as recent as 2014. Why is the resistance effective or otherwise. The resistance takes its effect because it is heard and understood on a global level. But it fails as fast fashion brands thrive at the same time. Moguls like H&M puts their wrongdoings under the carpet by introducing a “conscious” brand alongside their key operations of fast fashion. The Origins of Resistance Against Fast Fashion As a majority of this compendia talks about the idea of adversities due to the apparel industry getting thrown under the bus, one could not help but wonder, why does it happen ? There are many activists out there, many more organisations, and many brands quietly trying to make their voices heard in order to make the apparel industry better. Quiet because the flow of the market is too strong and too powerful for a breakthrough even for a cause like this. 10. thesis_title: The Paradoxes of Fast Fashion: Can We Overcome a Wicked Problem?
paragraph: Consumption has become a crisis in recent years. From the consulting firm McKinsey shows that the majority of consumers keep their garments for only half as long as they did a mere fifteen years ago, often throwing out their cheapest pieces after as few as seven wears. The problem being, even though Fashion Revolution has achieved a heavy chunk of following, cheap-prices of fast fashion clothes still remain a major motive for consumers based on B. Fogg’s behavior model. Not only that, the influencer culture on social media, which is ever increasing acts as an incentive in this process. As Hasan Minhaj quotes, “People go for fast fashion because they want to look expensive-ish”. The other thing that becomes ignorant of consumer’s part as well is how they have been treating clothes or a lack of treatment. Fast fashion clothes are made for 10-15 washes and they lose their quality. Cheap prices and sales drive consumers buy more, “I just got it for $5, idk what I’m gonna do with it” culture.Besides that, But once the consumer decides to dispose of the garment, they typically choose to throw it out rather than donating or selling it in a majority of cases, with close to 60% of all discarded clothing ending up incinerated or in a landfill before its first birthday). This behaviour of being aware of the wrong doings but still participating in the same activity brings in the question whether this resistance is futile considering the fact that consumers do not go into the vice and the virtues of the commodity.

Many brands claiming to be sustainable just because it started gaining worldwide attention, are actually causing great harm to the environment, often because their efforts don’t extend beyond one area of their business. It’s ironic, in a sense, because they claim to be protecting the environment in order to sell more clothes, which is actually harmful to the environment. author: Yi Luo
