paragraph: To understand why some human constructs (e.g., Yugoslav Spomenik Monuments) strike viewers as Sci-Fi-ish objects, and what commonalities they share with Big Dumb Objects (hereafter referred to as BDO), I examined some of the most iconic BDO from Sci-Fi movies, and placed them alongside other concepts from Sci-Fi genre. By listing out the attributes generally possessed by BDO, and breaking down the images of BDO and said constructs in terms of form, function and (human) reaction, much common ground was found, upon which a new set of guidelines can be established to answer the question of 1)What the essential attributes of BDO are; and 2)How to build and reproduce our own BDO. The Extraterrestrial

author: Yi Luo
thesis_title: BIG DUMB OBJECTS: Deconstructed and Reconstructed
paragraph: For most consumers of science fiction movies, it is no mystery that unidentified alien artifacts of immense size and unknown origin inexorably evoke a “sense of wonder”, with each appearance and contact having us unconsciously holding our breaths and stopping the busy popcorn chewing. From the Black Monolith of 2001: A Space Odyssey, Death Star of Star Wars Franchise, to the most recent ​ ​ ​ saucer-shaped “Heptapod” Spacecraft that hovers over the earth in Arrival, Sci-Fi ​ ​ filmmakers have used these entities to seize audiences in recent decades with success, and sometimes great controversy. Consumers are undoubtedly attracted to the gigantic and unknown as the substantial inclusion of these objects in the genre proves. How the very presence of such concepts in a narrative can have a profound effect on a viewer is both a source of inspiration and perplexity. The term “Big Dumb Objects” (hereafter referred to as “BDO”) was first brought up by British writer and Sci-Fi critic Roz Kaveney in her essay Science Fiction in ​ the 1970s: Some Dominant Themes and Personalities. Kaveney jokingly used it ​ to refer to Larry Niven's Ringworld construct, a ring “with a radius of 93 million miles—the sun-Earth distance—with the sun placed at the center.” (Hadhazy) ​ ​ However, it is not until the second edition of The Encyclopedia of Science Fiction ​ (hereafter referred to as “SFE”) came out in 1993 that the term starts to draw public attention. According to Peter Nicholls, the creator and co-editor of SFE, the decision to include BDO as a critical term in the reference book was made on a whim: … A possible theme that had been suggested was Alien Artefacts, but it was April Fool’s Day and I decided purely for my own entertainment, and aware that John Clute might well be cross if he found out, that it would be appropriate to write a joke entry as a prank. I would pretend that a phrase of Roz Kaveney’s that I’d always liked, but which was not in general use, was actually a known critical term. I decided that I would write Alien Artefacts but call it Big Dumb Objects, and write in a poker-faced style, suggesting an even more absurd critical term to be used in its place, “megalotropic sf”. (Nicholls) Since then, attempts have been made to define what a Big Dumb Object is, and what determining attributes made some of the Sci-Fi creations BDO and others not. To quote Christopher Palmer’s saying in Big Dumb Objects in Science ​ Fiction: Sublimity, Banality, and Modernity, ​ A Big Dumb Object (Clute and Nicholls in The Encyclopedia of Science Fiction are right to appeal for another term, but this is the one that prevails) has the following qualities: it is artificial; it wasn’t made by humans; its makers are absent so that it is or seems deserted; it is large enough to explore; indeed, it is usually very large, so that the human explorers are dwarfed; and very often the human explorers are swallowed up — they are enclosed, they are exploring an interior. (Palmer) Fred Scharmen also elucidated BDO in his essay What Is A Big Dumb Object: “It ​ ​ is large, of unknown origin, its meaning and function equally unclear. On closer study, they (protagonists) realize that it is not natural, it is constructed, and more than that, it seems to offer itself for interaction and even inhabitation.” While Palmer and Scharmen tried to set the parameters by describing the specific settings in precedents, others see BDO as a type of plot device functionally equivalent to Hitchcock’s MacGuffin, “an object, device, or event that is necessary to the plot and the motivation of the characters, but insignificant, unimportant, or irrelevant in itself.” (Wikipedia) According to Lizzie Muller, “Generic science fictional MacGuffins include the ‘BDO’ or Big Dumb Object, a thing that inspires awe and wonder through its presence, whilst remaining mysterious (the obelisk in Stanley Kubrick’s 2001: A Space Odyssey is a classic example).” In an article written for the Guardian, Damian Walter explained how these two concepts are connected: “BDOs either look extreme or unusual, and can often do extreme or unusual things: everything from lurking on a horizon to creating worlds. Usually, BDOs are plonked into plots to awe us with their majesty and mystery — really, they’re science fiction’s equivalent of a MacGuffin.” It is crucial to point out that, though the term BDO was coined in a purely text-based context, it has now evolved into a concept encompassing all mediums Sci-Fi could take including prints, film, television, and even games. Meanwhile MacGuffin took a reversed route from BDO, starting from Classic Hollywood cinema and later adopted by fictions. As the person responsible for bringing BDO to the fore and making it commonplace in Sci-Fi criticism, Nicholls dissected this matter in his 1997 speech by touching upon the ever-present tension between “the orderly scientific thought (the classical)” and “the phenomena which do not submit to this order (the romantic)” in science fictions, crystallizing BDO as the embodiments of the fundamental human need to experience “sense of wonder” and “conceptual breakthrough”: There is in science fiction, even or especially in so-called Hard science fiction, something which in other context we tend to think of as unscientific, be it called sense of wonder, or the sublime, or the transcendent as the Panshins have it, or the romantic. And one rather mechanical way of creating this effect is for the storyteller to imagine something very very big and mysterious, like the spaceship Rama, or like Larry Niven’s Ringworld. That is, the mysterious something in science fiction often has its locus classicus in the Big Dumb Object. (Nicholls) Interestingly, if one looks up for the BDO entry in the latest SFE (which has been made available for free online), they will find the following sentences: “In Sunstorm (2005) by Arthur C Clarke and Stephen Baxter, a huge artificial shield built in space to protect Earth from the titular Disaster is explicitly referred to as a big dumb object. It is however a big smart object inhabited by a highly capable AI, and the frequent need to qualify ‘Big Dumb Object’ in this way has led to this edition's adoption of Macrostructure as the main relevant headword.” (Langford, and Nicholls) The decision to ditch the term and replace it with Macrostructure hardly comes in surprise, however, if one has gone through all the aforementioned materials. The literal formation of the term Big Dumb Objects has determined that it’s too confining a word for science fiction, a genre propelled forward by novel settings and concepts (Nicholls himself also admitted that “it is not of itself an especially sophisticated concept”). It presets that for an Sci-Fi being to trigger “sense of wonder” and bring “conceptual breakthrough”, it must be big, dumb, and of course, an object, while in reality said effects are not always activated by a single cause, and an object doesn’t need to possess all the properties to have such effects on readers or viewers. After all, it’s the works that shape the boundaries of a genre, rather than vice versa. According to Eugene R. Espinueva, even the validated BDO could have conflictions with the term’s original settings: … These properties are ubiquitous throughout BDO but are not linked by them, as there are some notable exceptions. In terms of scale, BDOs can take the form of enigmatic objects such as the Monolith of 2001 Space Odyssey (1968). Another example of a smaller scale BDO would be the spherical object of the 1998 movie Sphere. In both examples, the objects in question have all the parameters of a BDO, but not the scale parameter. Even Niven's Ringworld can be challenged as not being a BDO because of the fact that the creators are finally revealed in sequels. The distinction between “BDO” and macrostructures is more a hard science fiction than a narrative object. BDOs are often a designation for macrostructures, as long as these structures retain much of their mystery. Macrostructures are a reference to the objects themselves. (Espinueva) ​ ​ The only way to be sure that something is qualified to go under the umbrella of BDO, it seems, is to enumerate all the verified examples, having people of significance nodding unanimously and go, that seems fit, and then having the Wikipedia page updated so non-hardcore Sci-Fi readers could look up and recite the newest list of Big Dumb Objects. Looking back at the etymology of Big Dumb Objects, “bigness”, “dumbness” and “objecthood” are all words that are not friendly with open interpretations (texts), but fully ready to be translated into and performed with visual languages (images). Ted Chiang’s award-winning Story of Your Life, for instance, is a novel ​ ​ with major themes on language, memories and determinism, but adapted to a movie with heavy BDO connotations. Chiang put great efforts in the descriptions of the alien Heptapods’ movements and communications with humans, but didn’t say much about their ships other than that they “move in orbit”. The storyline does fit some of the criteria, like it explores something much bigger and more advanced than humans. Narrated in the linguist Dr. Louise Banks’ nostalgic voice and written as a message from a mother to a daughter, however, nothing that happened in the story is supposed to feel new or eye-opening (or as Nicholls put it, resemble the effects of “finding a diamond in a dung heap”), at least not to the protagonist. Although Story of Your Life on many aspects goes against the classic settings of ​ ​ BDO novels, like the Heptapods descend from extraterrestrial space and are the ones who make the first contact, and these alien “forerunners” who built the civilization are utterly present and self-evident, in the adapted movie Arrival and ​ ​ its marketing campaign, the Heptapod’s spacecrafts are heavily featured and almost became the embodiment of the movie’s visual language. Released alongside the first trailer, twelve posters featuring twelve different locations show the dark saucer-shaped alien crafts hovering ominously above varying landscapes. These posters each present a singular craft from a unique angle, and if put in the right order, one can see how the ships spin in the sequence. When asked about how these decisions were made, production designer Patrice Vermette explained that, the spacecraft was described as a sphere with no further supporting descriptions in the original story, but the director Denis Villeneuve wanted it to be vertical: I explored many shapes, and we went with a sort of elongated oval… We took some creative liberties, but it's all about the same idea on which the book and script are based — the strangeness… Then we thought, “What if it were black?” We studied different charcoal textures. And what if the ship never lands? It hovers over the earth. What if humans have to take the last step (on an elevator) — the last 30 feet? (Giardina) ​ ​ Shape, texture, size, color, posture, position, and atmosphere, alongside many other visual elements that go along with the mise en scène of the movie, helped achieve this “strangeness” that the crew pursued, which inevitably brought up a question: if the mentality of BDO effects can be verbally elucidated, as many scholars have successfully done, could the aesthetics be, too? The Terrestrial

author: Yi Luo
thesis_title: BIG DUMB OBJECTS: Deconstructed and Reconstructed
paragraph: On Chinese social networking service website Douban exists a forum titled “BDO Fetishism Syndrome”. Founded merely a year and a half ago, it now has more than 43,000 members actively posting and sharing contents they consider related to BDO. Wuling, founder and moderator of the forum, set the following etiquettes for her members: BDO refers to a group of artifacts in science fiction that are gigantic, mysterious, and of immense power. But here, we’re also fascinated by stylistically alienated Brutalist buildings, functioning or nonfunctioning industrial spectacles, infrequent meteorological phenomena, infinite spaces, and imaginative monsters of mysterious civilizations… (the range of BDO) can be extended to myths and legends, prehistoric civilizations, extraterrestrial civilizations, cyberpunk, and other subgenres of science fiction. Things you can post here include but are not limited to celestial bodies, deep sea, earth core, dams, spillway, windmills, water towers, cooling towers, cable towers, air defense towers, nuclear bunkers, nuclear power plants, radars, missile silos, radio telescope matrices, lighthouses, Buddha statues, Torii gates, cargo containers, offshore drilling platforms, giant machinery, mines, craters, monuments, huge billboards, airships, aircraft carriers, UFO, mirages... But we don’t recognize city buildings, bridges, or mountains as BDO. These contents will be deleted. BDO are essentially different from things that are just big. To further empathize the stand of this group, only buildings from sci-fi movies or speculative arts are recognized as BDO. Your everyday high-rise buildings don’t count, unless they’re infused with counterculture aesthetics or non-mainstream ideologies, e.g. Soviet Totalitarian architecture, Parisian Utopian housing project, or Hongkong Kowloon Walled City. To wrap up, the determining factor of BDO is they bring us “Sense of Wonder” and are “Awe-inspiring”. BDO Fetishism Syndrome can be seen as the first expression of the so-called BDO aesthetics, as well as the first major initiative to expand its range and make the concept mainstream. The results are astonishing: the accompanying hashtag “Those Big Dumb Objects That Either Fascinate or Intimidate You” now has more than 26,000,000 views and 26,800 followers, making it one of the hottest topics on Douban, the latter being one of the most influential web 2.0 websites in China (Wikipedia). One will be amazed at both how deeply engaged in this topic netizens are, and how so many geologically and culturally dispersed objects, artworks and even smartphone pictures, share at least some visual commonalities with classic BDO. The descriptors netizens use to caption their nine-image grids, not for lack of vocabularies or metaphors, are also strikingly similar, serving as constant reminders of Sci-Fi as an ultimately humane creation. From Anish Kapoor’s gigantic “Dismemberment, Site 1” sculpture on New Zealand meadows (“feels like you’re being sucked into a blackhole and squeezed by the distorted time-sapce”), to Instagram pictures overlooking Tokyo Bay (“Tokyo Bay in the night looks like a busy docking port for spaceships, with Tokyo Tower as their signal station, lighthouse, or even future weapon of some sort”); from Sunset at the Great Pyramids (“Standing in front of these constructs you’re left with nothing but shock and awe for they’re so immense and built so long ago. Compared with the surrounding housing you’ll start wondering if they are actually human-made — they simply look too advanced!”), to the molten salt solar power farm located in Dunhuang, Gansu Province (“Just saw this on Weibo, each mirror in the picture is 115 square meters big. Was shocked speechless. The heat absorption tower in the middle looks too Sci-Fi-ish”), the range of contents would surpass Roz Kaveney’s wildest imagination, were her informed of the existence of such a subcultural phenomenon in China. Among all the 2,878 posts of ideological relics, tourist landscapes, landmark constructions, religious symbols, and industrial facilities, one set of pictures of Yugoslav “Spomenik” Monuments got particularly high impressions, with 1,660 “Likes”, 571 “Reposts”, 292 “Collections”, and 72 “Replies”. This strikes as an interesting case, partly because these constructs built in the post-war era only started gaining cult status less than ten years ago, thanks to Antwerp-based photographer Jan Kempenaers’ “laborious trek through the Balkans to photograph a series of these mysterious objects”, partly because in MoMA’s recent exhibition “Toward a Concrete Utopia: Architecture in Yugoslavia, 1948–1980”, these objects were unveiled to the Anglo-Saxon public for the first time in a very long period. According to the curators, this exhibition was held to “fill one of the gaps that have resulted from a myopic perspective,” (Kulić et al.) one that treats Balkans region as “Europe’s ‘Orient’” — an exotic, “other” territory between East and West. By juxtaposing the public’s impressions - Sci-Fi-ish, futuristic, alien and the ​ ​ ​ ​ ​ ​ historians’ reflections - Eurocentric, myopic, exotic- side by side, a new cause ​ ​ ​ ​ ​ ​ and effect connection can be made, resonating with Fred Scharmen’ succinct articulations of the causes of the dumbness of BDO: ​ ​ The use of the word “dumb” in the Big Dumb Object is not necessarily meant to imply that the artifact lacks embodied intelligence. It is more to say that the purpose, origin, and significance of the piece as a coherent thing are not clearly communicated. While they are things, either these objects are entirely mute, or they present an array of incomplete, overlapping, and contradictory associations that cloud their precise meaning, even while they still seem to mean something. The Big Objects in question are capital-D Dumb because they do not speak to us in ways that we can comprehend. (Scharmen) To summarize, the necessary condition for an interaction to be seen as BDO-ish is not human interaction to extraterrestrial, but protagonists to “the other”. To put it another way, it’s half on the beholder, and half on the beheld. A tourist’s first encounter with a Yugoslav monument, therefore, can be fully seen as a BDO experience, considering how little the world knows about the historical and political contexts of these constructs. Same could go with Sendai Daikannon of Japan (“The way this statue is situated on top of a hill overlooking a neighborhood and an Aeon mall sends me chills,” said one of the reviews left on tripadvisor.com), Princess Mackerel Statue of China, Golden Bridge of Vietnam, and many other big objects of varied forms and functions scattered across the surface of this globe. A new taxonomy of BDO hence can be developed to enclose such a group of human-made objects, be it for the purpose of recognition enhancement, or identity reconstruction, or tourism boost. Looking back, it’s almost uncanny how Scharmen’s comments can be used seamlessly on Yugoslav Monuments to explain where that Sci-Fi-ishness ​ originates from, though historians have better explanations for why they are of such miscellaneous appearances in spite of the universalizing strides of socialist modernity. In Building Brotherhood and Unity: Architecture and Federalism in ​ Socialist Yugoslavia, Vladimir Kulić elucidated how Yugoslav architects balanced ​ between its federalist slogan “brotherhood and unity” and its mode of “kaleidoscope of cultures” as a self-representation towards a global audience, giving rise to a vibrant architectural culture irreducible to simple ideological terms: Indeed, the history of fluctuating geopolitical divisions that cut through the region stretched back to antiquity… The result was a dizzying mosaic of dialects, ethnicities, and religions, as well as of varied architectural and artistic traditions brought together into close physical proximity, if not always in direct contact… Nevertheless, diversity persisted, compressing a great deal of architectural phenomena — varied regionalisms, the particular articulations of certain programs, or idiosyncratic personal poetics — into a small geographical space the size of the state of Oregon. (Kulić) The dumbness of BDO, or the strangeness of the Heptapod spacecrafts, or the alienness of Yugoslav Monuments, therefore, can all be seen as symptoms of a series of intentional or unintentional human efforts. And by analyzing and codifying said symptoms of both fictional BDO and physically existing BDO, a new tool can be invented to: 1) reproduce classic BDO effects, such as the instinct to worship, the ​ ​ impulse to explore, and “sense of wonder” as defined by the Encyclopedia of Science Fiction; 2) democratize the right to build BDO and emancipate it from the ​ ​ monopoly of those with resources (e.g. production companies and political regimes), making the fun of Big Dumb Objects accessible to everybody. Contribution Statement

author: Yi Luo
thesis_title: BIG DUMB OBJECTS: Deconstructed and Reconstructed
paragraph: BDO as a Sci-Fi concept has been replaced by the more inclusive Macrostructure, for the literal formation of this term determined that it requires to be qualified all the time: what if somethings big and smart, or small and dumb? Or what if it’s indeed big and dumb, but at the same time sentient or organic? Many would deem BDO too confining a word for Sci-Fi, a narrative genre propelled forward by new ideas and possibilities, leaving it in an awkward situation in the realm of Sci-Fi criticism. Some human-made objects, in the meantime, undergo another type of awkwardness due to loss of context, identity confusion, and lack of recognition. Such awkwardness can be seen on Spomenik Monuments in former Yugoslavia (ideological relics), the hand-shaped bridge in Vietnam (kitsch tourist landscapes), the haunting mackerel mermaid statue in Liaoning Province, China (landmark constructions), as well as the seemingly omnipresent big Kannon in Japan (religious symbols). While they’re certainly big objects, there are two leading causes that contributed to their “dumbness”. Firstly, the effort put in, the ambition demonstrated, and the authority implied, all together created an impression similar to the one you get from looking at a miserable try-hard. Secondly, viewing through a Eurocentric lens, these big objects would inevitably be dumbed down to bathos and silliness, for the world is simply too unfamiliar with these “other” cultures. And yet, to educate on such huge beings of varying historical origins and geographic locations almost equals Mission Impossible. What they require is a label, a taxonomy that shouts out to the world: “I’m worth as much attention as Stonehenge or Christ the Redeemer.” And this is where the BDO could come in and be assigned the mission, which is to give these objects a name. The transition from mundane objects to BDO, on the other hand, can be seen as the spiritual heritage of both Japanese Tokusatsu craftsmanship and Hollywood B Movies in the age of DIY and self-media, signifying the democratization of the creation of “Sense of Wonder.” While the appearance of bigness and dumbness can be achieved through means including lighting, size contrast, and background set-up, effects such as the instinct to worship, the impulse to explore, and “Sense of Wonder” as defined by the Encyclopedia of Science Fiction, can also be triggered by particular combination of visual formulae, thus making the transition from everyday objects to Big Dumb Objects a replicable and standardizable process. BDO Manifesto

author: Yi Luo
thesis_title: BIG DUMB OBJECTS: Deconstructed and Reconstructed
paragraph: LIFE IS A BORE, and I need my MFA degree. ​ Therefore, I reinvented the territory of BIG DUMB OBJECTS, and here’s how the game is played:

author: Yi Luo
thesis_title: BIG DUMB OBJECTS: Deconstructed and Reconstructed
paragraph: 1. A BDO should be of vast scale. Therefore, A diamond mine is a BDO, and a dog hole in your backyard is not.

author: Yi Luo
thesis_title: BIG DUMB OBJECTS: Deconstructed and Reconstructed
paragraph: 2. A BDO should be of unrecognized origins. Therefore, My safety pin is a BDO, and your safety pin is not.

author: Yi Luo
thesis_title: BIG DUMB OBJECTS: Deconstructed and Reconstructed
paragraph: 3. A BDO should be erected for unknown purposes. Therefore, The statue of princess mackerel is a BDO, and the Statue of Liberty is not.

author: Yi Luo
thesis_title: BIG DUMB OBJECTS: Deconstructed and Reconstructed
paragraph: 4. A BDO should not fit in where it presents itself. Therefore, This guy here is a BDO, and this guy is not.

author: Yi Luo
thesis_title: BIG DUMB OBJECTS: Deconstructed and Reconstructed
paragraph: 5. A BDO should not communicate in conceivable ways. Therefore, A Yugoslav monument is a BDO, and an ordinary monument is not.

author: Yi Luo
thesis_title: BIG DUMB OBJECTS: Deconstructed and Reconstructed
paragraph: 6. A BDO should not be wholly graspable by existing aesthetics or discourse systems. Therefore, The Golden Bridge is a BDO, and the Golden Gate Bridge is not.

author: Yi Luo
thesis_title: BIG DUMB OBJECTS: Deconstructed and Reconstructed
paragraph: 7. A BDO should approach or be approached in a manner that symptomizes transcendence and epiphany rather than haunt and paranoia. Therefore, Tadao Ando’s Buddha is a BDO, and Louise Bourgeois’ Spider is not.

author: Yi Luo
thesis_title: BIG DUMB OBJECTS: Deconstructed and Reconstructed
paragraph: 8. A BDO should and should only be perceived as a sensual object, its sensual qualities extracted from human confrontations the only criteria for its categorization.

author: Yi Luo
thesis_title: BIG DUMB OBJECTS: Deconstructed and Reconstructed
paragraph: Therefore, 9. A BDO should and should only remain a human concept, its restrictions the premise to understand where this manifesto ends.

author: Yi Luo
thesis_title: BIG DUMB OBJECTS: Deconstructed and Reconstructed
paragraph: Therefore, 10. A BDO should and should always differ individually and contextually, which undoes all previous points. List of Works

author: Yi Luo
thesis_title: BIG DUMB OBJECTS: Deconstructed and Reconstructed
paragraph: BDO Manifesto: ​ A video essay on where to redraw the territory of BIG DUMB OBJECTS and a crystallization of my thesis studies. I added a pseudo VHS touch for a more coherent visual experience, and set the aspect ratio at 4:3 to give a “monumental” effect.

author: Yi Luo
thesis_title: BIG DUMB OBJECTS: Deconstructed and Reconstructed
paragraph: Giant God Safety Pin Appears in Tokyo: ​ This video is based on Shinji Higuchi's Giant God Warrior Appears in Tokyo. By replacing the Warrior with a safety pin in Cinema 4D while keeping the original composition, I tried to validate that the effects caused by confronting a BDO can be triggered by particular combinations of visual formulae.

author: Yi Luo
thesis_title: BIG DUMB OBJECTS: Deconstructed and Reconstructed
paragraph: BIG DUMB OBJECTS: Deconstructed & Reconstructed: ​ A series of attempts made to build BDO with mundane materials, culminating in the animated video and the video essay. Glossary of Terms

author: Yi Luo
thesis_title: BIG DUMB OBJECTS: Deconstructed and Reconstructed
paragraph: Sci-Fi Science fiction is usually thought of as a narrative genre, primarily experienced through film, television or print.

author: Yi Luo
thesis_title: BIG DUMB OBJECTS: Deconstructed and Reconstructed
paragraph: BDO (Big Dumb Objects) In the discussion of science fiction, a Big Dumb Object is a vast, mysterious artifact (usually of extraterrestrial or unknown origin and immense power) in a story that generates an intense sense of wonder by its mere existence. Peter Nicholls, in recalling the writing of The Encyclopedia of Science Fiction, identifies the concept of BDO along with two other themes running through science fiction as a literary genre, the Conceptual Breakthrough and the Sense of Wonder.

author: Yi Luo
thesis_title: BIG DUMB OBJECTS: Deconstructed and Reconstructed
paragraph: Macrostructure In the newer online edition of The Encyclopedia of Science Fiction, the term “Big Dumb Object” has been deprecated in favor of the word “Macrostructure”. According to The Encyclopedia, “the frequent need to qualify ‘Big Dumb Object’ in this way has led to this edition’s adoption of Macrostructure as the main relevant headword.” Though one can see “Macrostructure” as an upgrade from the architecturally scaled “megastructure” and the ill-defined “Big Dumb Object”, the use of these three terms are still heavily overlapping in today’s Sci-Fi criticism.

author: Yi Luo
thesis_title: BIG DUMB OBJECTS: Deconstructed and Reconstructed
paragraph: Attribute An attribute is a quality or a characteristic ascribed to someone or something, as opposed to property, which demonstrates itself through physical phenomena without the need for attribution. Bigness The Bigness of BDO is a relative attribute. While some articles claim that an object should be of planetary size to be qualified as a Big Dumb Object, this contradicts the very fact that the black monolith in 2001: Space Odyssey is often seen as one of the most iconic BDO. According to the author of Big Dumb Objects in Science Fiction: Sublimity, Banality, and Modernity, a BDO is large enough 1) for the humans to explore; and 2) to dwarf the human explorers.

author: Yi Luo
thesis_title: BIG DUMB OBJECTS: Deconstructed and Reconstructed
paragraph: Dumbness The Capital D “Dumb” in BDO has two major connotations. Firstly, the purpose, origin, and significance of the object are not clearly communicated, at least not in the first appearance. While they are things, either these objects are entirely mute, or they present an array of incomplete, overlapping, and contradictory associations that cloud their precise meaning. Secondly, the manifestation of sublimity and transcendence occurs in parallel with a kind of overlay of bathos and silliness, as can be found in Larry Niven’s Ringworld. When one character remarks, “It looks like a star with a ring around it. What is it?” another replies, “It is a star with a ring around it, … a ring of solid matter. An artifact,” while another giggles.

author: Yi Luo
thesis_title: BIG DUMB OBJECTS: Deconstructed and Reconstructed
paragraph: MacGuffin Originated by Angus MacPhail for film, adopted by Alfred Hitchcock, and later extended to fiction, the word “MacGuffin” represents a plot device/story engine that often exists in the form of an object to propel forward the action of a narrative.

author: Yi Luo
thesis_title: BIG DUMB OBJECTS: Deconstructed and Reconstructed
paragraph: Sense of Wonder The “sense of wonder” defines science fiction not by its content but by its effect. To put it another way, it comes not from brilliant writing nor brilliant conceptualizing; it comes from a sudden opening of a closed door in the reader/viewer's mind, or a new perspective gained through either a sudden dislocation of scale, or a shift to a new position along the enormous span between cosmos and microcosm.

author: Yi Luo
thesis_title: BIG DUMB OBJECTS: Deconstructed and Reconstructed
paragraph: Conceptual Breakthrough Conceptual breakthrough can best be explained in terms of "paradigms". A paradigm is a generally held way of looking at and interpreting the world. All the most exciting scientific revolutions have taken the form of breaking down a paradigm and substituting another. Such an altered perception of the world, sometimes in terms of science and sometimes in terms of society, is what Sci-Fi is most commonly about. The breakthrough in recent Sci-Fi is often catalysed by confrontation with alien artefacts (such as BDO and Macrostructures). Bibliography

paragraph: Thesis Advisor: Gaia Hwang CONTENTS ABSTRACT 5 FOREWORD 6 OBSCURITY 8 INDEXICALITY 12 EVOCATIVE 18 EMPATHETIC AND INTUITIVE 22 DESIGN IN FUZZY LANGUAGE 24 BIBLIOGRAPHY 38 ABSTRACT Fuzzy Language is a visual language that we hardly notice. Fuzzy Language communicates at a slow-pace, implicitly, ambiguously, and open-endedly. It uses physical traces as a medium to engage, provoke, and inspire audiences. Instead of directly presenting meaning, it pro- vides an atmosphere for the viewer to actively analyze the information and interpret it in their own way. author: Yuxuan Liu
paragraph: The last one is a 99-pages book that I tore by hand, and I describe it as “living traces”. This book asks: will physical traces stand forever? How does time influence the interpretation of Fuzzy Language? Physical traces can be split into two categories; one that recovers and gradually disappears throughout time, such as footprints on the snow or some scars on the body; another more severe, wrinkles and erosion. So, Fuzzy Language can never exist in static form but as a movement that keeps changing: it is a motion implying another motion. But normally we can only catch a fixed moment of Fuzzy Language, which is what we say as a mark or a trace. I tore every single page in a different time and ordered them by the size of the “scar” I made. I meant to follow the rule of the flipbook to display a little pe- riod of time, create the illusion of motion, and bring viewers the sense of erosion and recovery while they are flapping the page. Video link: https://vimeo.com/420506756

author: Zhiying Huang
paragraph: UGLINESS by Zhiying Huang ​ Received and approved: ________________________________________________ Lead Thesis Faculty Gaia Hwang ________________________________________________ Chairperson Santiago Piedrafita, May 2020 ________________________________________________ MFA Thesis Candidate Zhiying Huang, May 2020 2 Table of Content

author: Zhiying Huang
paragraph: Abstract 3 Thesis Question 4 Delimitation 4 Contextual Framework 5 Main Chapter 9 Contribution to the Discipline 21 Bibliography 22 3 Abstract Aesthetics is an essential part of design practice, whether designing compelling objects or engaging experiences. Historically, design has pursued optimal functionality and seamless, efficient interactions. Why do people have an appetite for beauty? Are we rendering aesthetic judgment within a technological culture that describes impossible ideals of beauty and perfection? Currently, in social media platforms, beauty has become a predictable form. There are, for instance, numerous functional and attractive templates for non-designers to employ, but few off-standard or ‘ugly’ designs. Umberto Eco says: “Ugliness is unpredictable and offers an infinite range of possibilities. Beauty God.”[1] is finite. Ugliness is infinite, like ​This thesis explores ugliness as a critical design tool to ​ better understand the potential for how we might relate to objects. I argue this paradigm shift might allow for more generative, intimate, and durable relationships with the artifacts in our lives. I will use speculative and experimental aesthetics as a methodology to explore potentially new sensibilities, capabilities, and functionalities of objects, how they are perceived, and the power they hold to shift perspective. 4 Thesis Question What are the powers of an ugly artifact? What is the potential of ugliness in a design context?

author: Zhiying Huang
paragraph: Delimitation This thesis explores the epistemological meaning of ugliness rather than its ontological idea. 5 Contextual Framework Ugliness as a label is used to define something currently unacceptable. Ugliness denotes the ​ unfamiliar, dirty, distorted, deformed, monstrous, or what can harm. Many humans fear the unfamiliar. When something is unknown or sits outside the norm and it ​ challenges common sense and standards, it is considered ugly. For example, a person who has ​ a deformity is labeled as ugly. While ugliness is used to define monstrosity in nature, there are many configurations of lives in nature that are distorted but considered beautiful. For example, DNA is twisted; bacteria are warped; organs inside human bodies are distorted at birth; and, the branches of trees may warp around objects or each other to adapt. Campanella[2] A Japanese band called Wednesday ​released a series of documentaries about ​ filming a music video called YAKUSHIMA TREASURE alongside with the Youtube documentary Re: SET. The documentary is set in the island of Yakushima in Kagoshima Prefecture, Japan; the EP and the documentary explore the theme of nature distorting and expanding and rebirth after death -the island was destroyed in a volcanic eruption and return to life afterward. The EP is a reflection on how life expands and contracts. The video uses the language of distortion as a visual metaphor at the border between ugliness and beauty. The lives of the mountain form themselves into unexpected shapes. There is no standard set for life on an island - it is a Word Heritage Site protected from human presence - life can grow freely. The music video combines the CG effect and the environment of the mountain, to create a Philosophical reflection of lives in this mountain. In the human-made world, ugliness is a label to define distorted, out of scale, out of context, History[3] lack of innocence, defeat the norm and monstrosity. In Ugliness: A Cultural Gretchen E ​ ​ ​ Henderson argues that ugliness is not only related to vision, but it is a discomfort of humans’ senses and experiences. For example, touching something with thorns, or mucus; Hearing a harsh sound/music; Inhaling a stinky smell; Tasting something disgusting, etc. Aesthetics.[4] Ronald Moore wrote about ugliness in the Vol. 4 Encyclopedia of ​Ronald states: ​ ​ “Although there is no doubt an opposition between beauty and ugliness, it is an opposition that can be understood in a good many ways; and although beauty marks an extreme of aesthetic positivity, ugliness sometimes presents itself in manifestations whose effects are not altogether negative." He also mentions three issues he had been focusing on in terms of the philosophical ​ ​ reflection on the nature of aesthetic ugliness. Ronald writes that questions are "(1) The conceptual problem of providing a correct analysis of ugliness, particularly in its relation to beauty. (2) The ontological problem of determining whether ugliness exists (i.e., whether there are any things that truly are ugly) and, if it does, how it becomes engaged in aesthetic judgment. (3) The critical problem of accounting for ugliness's salubrious effects both within and without the world of art." 6

author: Zhiying Huang
paragraph: "Ugliness is unpredictable and offers an infinite range of possibilities. Beauty is finite. Ugliness is infinite, like God." [1] ―Umberto Eco, On ugliness. ​ ​ ​​ Ugliness can cause strong feelings, and those feelings are easily recorded in memories. Ugliness leaves strong impressions, often stronger than beauty. Dorothy Parker said that beauty bone.[5] exists only on a skin level; in other words, ugliness dives deep into a human's ​The ​ instinctual reaction to ugliness is to reject it. But what if we can look deeper into ugliness instead Humans.[6] of turning away and avoid it? In his book, The ​Matt Haig mentioned "If you think ​ ​​ something is ugly, look harder. Ugliness is just a failure of seeing." ​This thesis argues that we ​ should observe ugliness with the eyes of a curious child who is trying to explore the world. In The Picture of Dorian Gray, Oscar Wilde writes "The ugly and stupid have the best of it in this ​ ​ ​ world. They can sit at their ease and gape at the play. If they know nothing of victory, they are at least spared the knowledge of defeat. They live as we all should live ― undisturbed, indifferent, hands."[7] and without disquiet. They never bring ruin upon others, nor ever receive it from alien ​ The ugly and the stupid are not expected to adhere to social norms and standards, and if this precludes access it can be thought as a form of freedom. The Chinese writer and translator Zhi Feng wrote that a German poet Rainer Maria Rilke stated the following in the book Auguste ​ Rodin: "Regarding the viewing of all things, the artist 'moulds one thing, that is: see everywhere, ​ no concealment, no neglect, no deception; know all the aspects, everything from the top and the bottom, Each crosses each other. Then there is an object, and then it is an island, completely separated from the erratic continent.’ The so-called ‘floating continent’ refers to the custom of instinct, and they often cover up things. Originally, it blurs the essence of things. Artists and poets must get rid of c ustoms, watch all things humbly and conscientiously, and discover the [8] essence of things." ​​ There are countless designs in the world, and the "Best Design" is always framed by a particular style, aesthetic, a pattern of design examples. The Karloff typeface is the production of a ​ Slovakian graphic and type designer called Peter Bilak. The font represents the close relationship between beauty and ugliness. Peter admits that dealing with the concept of ugliness has been more interesting than designing a beautiful and more traditional typeface. On Beauty ​ and Ugliness in Type design, Peter states: "The search for ugliness triggers a certain primal, ​ ​ voyeuristic curiosity, and from the designer’s perspective there is simply a lot more space to explore. Capturing beauty has always been considered the primary responsibility of the traditional artist, and even now it is rare to find examples of skilled and deliberate ugliness in type design, (although examples of inexperience and naïveté abound)." ​Thomas Hansard in ​​ 7 1825 called Karloff a special typeface a typeface that ‘defeats the norm,’ a ‘typographic monstrosity.’[9] ​ Nature is the core of the Alexander McQueen VOSS Show of 2001. The show portrays the idea that humans and other lives exist to decorate nature. The show used a custom-designed mirror: models could see themselves in the mirror, but they could not see the public. The models' emotions were augmented by this setup in the show. The show ends with an unexpected performance where a naked voluptuous woman wore a mask and a respirator, leaving the audience wondering about the meaning of this symbolism. Writer Katy Kelleher writes about the use of ugliness in fashion design in Ugliness Is ​ REVIEW:[10] Underrated: Ugly Fashion in PARIS ​"Even though there are problems with ugly ​ ​ ​​ fashion, I love how raw it is, how strange and how varied. I love that it flows and moves in weird ways. We can predict that hemlines will rise and fall, and we can predict that jeans will taper and flare, but who would have predicted the rise of Birkenstocks, the ubiquity of Ugg boots, the popularity of ironically worn Tevas, or the praise heaped upon Shia LaBeouf’s dadfits and Justin Bieber’s sleazecore? There is something organic about ugly fashion. It springs up, like weeds growing in sidewalk cracks, and spreads outward until it’s consumed the entire parking lot—or, at least, until it begins to look less ugly, until our eyes become used to the eyesores, and the cycle begins again." Katy continues, "But the real reason I adore ugly fashion is deeper than ​ ​ that. At my most generous, I think ugliness is a form of power. The disability-justice organizer and writer Mia Mingus argues that “ugliness is a pathway to intimacy.” We fear ugliness, she says, because we place too much value on beauty. And yet beauty is a limiting political construct built on our society’s prejudices. If we were to accept and celebrate ugliness, to embrace our individual “magnificence” over our facial symmetry, perhaps we could foster a stronger sense of empathy. In the words of Jia Tolentino, we could all stop jostling for space on the “narrow, precarious beauty bridge” and instead “howl like animals and jump right off." Yui ​ Takada mentions that designers should observe things from the front, the back, and the side in the talk show named YiXi. Yui is a graphic designer from Japan, he explores many unique aspects of his design work. In a talk show, Yui said he likes observing the overlooked, ignored, and ugly designs. He thinks chewing gum is not ugly or gross, he thinks that it is a sculpture crafted by a human's mouth. This way of engaging the world is impressive. Olafur Eliasson is a well known Danish-Icelandic installation artist. Olafur enhances the relationship, the connection between the viewers and his works “Do I trust my own eyes and my own capacity to engage in the world?,” Olafur Eliasson asked himself. The book of Chinese Elementary School Art Education Training for educators instructs to teach students to appreciate beauty and to hate the ugly, in order to train students to have a ‘noble aesthetic.’ “When guiding students to appreciate all kinds of beauty, they should pay attention to ​ guiding them to like and pursue beauty and noble things, hate low-level and ugly things, so that they can develop a healthy aesthetic taste and noble sentiment.” This approach rejects the ​ ​ ​ tradition of the Tao Te Ching, written by an ancient famous Chinese philosopher Laozi, “It is because every one under Heaven recognizes beauty as beauty, that the idea of ugliness 8 exists.”[11] It would be superficial if only beauty was appreciated but ugliness was ignored. ​ ​ Teaching children about ugliness would allow them to create their own definitions of beauty and of ugliness as such concepts change throughout history. A Chinese famous writer Feng ZiKai mentioned how artists should learn to observe the world as book.[12] children do in his ZiKai writes: “The concept of children is not as clear as that of adults, ​ ​ ​ but their intuition is stronger, so they have more anthropomorphic impressions and are more likely to see the truth of things.” Children are not indoctrinated on what is defined as ugly and ​ what is not; they are like artists. “Artists must see life in nature and find themselves in a grass ​ and a tree, so they must promote compassion, spread to nature, and affect nature with emotion.” Maintaining this approach to truth life is very important. ​ Due to the commercial nature of their practice, designers tend to replicate patterns and forms that have been produced and are familiar to their audience. Many ignore the potential of ugliness because of the influence of capitalism and the perceived need to make products that are viable in the marketplace. However, this negatively impacts humans’ unique sensibilities, especially teenagers. Yao Xiaonan mentions this in the Contemporary Development of Education[13] Aesthetic ​. If teenagers are just repurposing the same patterns, and copying other ​ people, they will lose the ability to self-reflect. For adults, ugliness should be a door let open, which can lead us to a deeper reflection about ourselves and things around us. Hegel said, “What sliver of sense and reason might be contained in otherwise frightening or foreign phenomena?” 9 Main Chapter(How did I do) As designers, we should be exploring the unknown aesthetics possibilities of both beauty and ​ ​ ugliness. As to the latter, ugliness has the extraordinary potential to engage us in a wide range of rich and meaningful experiences and emotions. We often rejected, ignored, and overlooked ​ ugly artifacts. This thesis argues that we can rediscover ugly artifacts as a design material that can generate new sensibilities and functionalities. Ugliness and ugly design can and should broaden our understanding of the world we live in by offering speculative and experimental aesthetics. Furthermore, ugliness can be cathartic; by stimulating strong emotion, it allows us to question the status quo.

author: Zhiying Huang
paragraph: This thesis argues that ugliness should also be used as a mode of inquiry, as a tool for self-reflection. This can be achieved in two steps. The first step is referred to as the Thinking process and includes a framework to explore the concept of ugliness based on the questions: ​ WHAT, WHY, and HOW. WHAT is about observing ugly artifacts and the uncomfortable feelings they generate in us. What emotion am I experiencing when engaging with an ugly item? Trying to describe that object and feelings in a verbal or visual way. WHY is about unpacking those emotions and contextualizing within our identities. Why do I feel uncomfortable? Is it because of an unpleasant memory in my childhood? Trying to recognize the connection between me and my feelings. HOW is about acting on the feelings ugliness generates: How do I act on these feelings and how can I respond to them through making. Using a new tool and methods to interpret my thoughts. During the making process, we gain new experiences and reach a new level of knowledge.

author: Zhiying Huang
paragraph: Writing a Manifesto This manifesto plays on the topic of memes in social media. It mimics an Instagram account that uses a mirror as a metaphor of the fact that the information presented to us by the feed algorithm is merely a reflection of what the system knows of themes. That creates a positive feedback loop that might negatively influence the new generation and should not be ignored. The text is a parody of the grammatically incorrect language of social media and uses imperative verbs as a means to manifest the discomfort generated by social media information overload. “We have been ABSORBING all kinds of information every day in this high-tech era. The data is flooding in our life. We should keep our mind soberly with the info, and to QUESTION the implication of the info instead of following the trend blindly. We should BREAK the chains of the trending; we should CREATE a new interpretation for our own sake, bring diversity to society”. 10

author: Zhiying Huang
paragraph: The Grid System This project puts under scrutiny the grid system in graphic design. Both designers and non-designers were confronted with nine questions. What makes the grid system so popular? How can the grid system impact people? Is there any relationship between the grid system and the social hierarchy? If we do not have a grid system, can we still get things organized? Can we be creative when we follow the grid system? The answers were designed as moveable pieces that could be interacted with to create new meaning. 11 Cockroach Oil Painting This oil painting communicates the main argument of this research: “Ugliness, as a label, is used to define something currently unacceptable.” Inspired by artist Jennifer Packer and her use of abstraction to discuss figure and background. The cockroach represents the correlation between ugliness and (un)acceptance. We define something ugly when we feel disturbed by it, and we are disturbed when we see something we define ugly. Cockroaches are considered gross, dirty, and full of bacteria despite actually displaying incredible agility, high reproducibility, and impressive survival skills. 12 The Rebellion Typeface The rebellion typeface aims to question tradition and generate new interpretations through the use of moving stamps. The Rebellion Typeface is designed using Futura Bold as a reference. It is made by carving rubber stamps. 13 Uncanny Healing Fears This project employs repetition as a mode of inquiry. For 100 days I repeated the same action: exploring fear and object to represent it. A series of interviews led to collecting 100 examples and stories about fears. I used an object to represent the fear symbolically, then put it into a bag and seal it. In one case, the interviewer discussed their fear of the uncanny. They reported having an unsettling dream: a familiar block around her house gradually felt no longer familiar and became uncanny. The keyword of this case was familiar and uncanny. Two famous Disney cartoon figures Donald Duck and Goofy Dog were modified. Their heads switched and suddenly, these two familiar figures became uncanny. 14

author: Zhiying Huang
paragraph: Ugliness in Typography design[14] In the AIGA article Lessons in the “New Ugly'' School of ​, Justin Zhang presents Darius ​ Ou a Singaporean graphic designer and author of a project called Autotypography. In this project, Ou investigates the principles of “good” design, and by stretching a typeface, remix it and render it formless.

author: Zhiying Huang
paragraph: Darius Ou, Autotypography. Photography by Weizhong Deng. 15 As a design response, I used Simplified Chinese characters as my base font and wrote the beauty and ugly. The project questions the standard of design and typographic visual ​ ​ ​ presentation in the past and present. The circles represent the rules and standards which define what a good design or ugly design is. The one on the top represents the past; the other one represents the present. I stretched and deconstructed the font. While fonts are stretching, it means the time is passing.

author: Zhiying Huang
paragraph: Zhiying Huang, Autotypography Experiment. Feb 24, 2019.

author: Zhiying Huang
paragraph: Ugliness in Senses Ugliness in the context of touch and smell is about the uncomfortable and unpleasant. Gretchen E Henderson claims that ugliness makes us question the culture and humans' origins, the birth, humans.[3] the component, the future of ​Meanwhile, Gretchen wrote, death and shock is a ​ common core of art. When different cultures and aesthetics come together; the word ugliness has changed its function, it became a noun more than an adjective and it could even become a verb. Leveraging my disgust for fresh organs, I designed a live performance. The thoughts and memories of my loved one were forcefully associated with a fresh pig heart and a pig tongue. 16

author: Zhiying Huang
paragraph: Fresh Pig Heart, live performance. Feb, 2019. I made a white box and put the heart and the tongue in two glass bottles separately. I played a song to create a sorrowful atmosphere. I sat on the ground and opened the white box (representing the coffin) and took out the tongue. I used the tongue to simulate my dog, licking my hand just like in old times. Then I took out the heart and kissed it for a minute, I cried. This performance generates a reflection about life; it crudely triggered emotions, and it was more powerful than looking at a picture of my loved one. Ugliness can produce very strong emotions. In this performance, ugliness became a verb.

author: Zhiying Huang
paragraph: Ugliness in Material Japanese designer Kenya Hara focuses on the material in his design works. In the chapter Haptic, Awakening the Senses in Designing Design, Kenya Hara introduces the idea of ​ ​ ​ designing the senses. Kenya Hara claims: “It’s not the question of how to create, but how to make someone sense something. We might call this creative awakening of the human sensors senses.’”[15] ‘the design of the In his Tokyo design exhibition Haptic, he invited many designers ​ ​ ​ ​ from different fields to design an object based on haptic considerations. The exhibit shows lots of exciting products, such as a lamp covered by a wig; it explored the relationship between people and senses. 17

author: Zhiying Huang
paragraph: Haptic, Awakening the Senses in Designing Design, Kenya Hara. Inspired by this exhibition, I decided to explore paper as material to express ugliness and wanted to explore how paper can lose its function. I resort to using moldy paper as a material to design: the musty paper was fragile, smelly, and possibly harmful. I let it rot, then I cut some pieces from it and used it as a background for a pamphlet cover.

author: Zhiying Huang
paragraph: Moldy Paper. Zhiying Huang, Mar 29, 2019. 18

author: Zhiying Huang
paragraph: Cover Inside Pages I repurpose these images by digitally modifying them and experimenting with the textures.

author: Zhiying Huang
paragraph: Moldy Paper Color Experiment. Zhiying Huang, Jul, 2019. 19 Ugliness in Paintings Eric Kandel argues that “Beauty does not occupy a different area of the brain than ugliness. them.”[16] Both are part of a continuum representing the values the brain attributes to ​ Since the cognition of beauty and ugliness trigger the same area in the human brain, can ugliness also be used as an inspiration like beauty? Can it inspire people within the design field? Ugh-Aha! (Ugly but Aha!) is an interactive piece in the form of a black notebook. I found eleven website[17] examples of ugly paintings from the Museum of Bad Art and printed them out at small ​ ​ scales. I conducted a participatory workshop with a selected group of participants. I asked them to choose some paintings from the eleven examples and to create a visual image in response to them in five minutes. Their responses are creative and speculative, the aesthetic and techniques that are drawn by the interviewees is unseen.

author: Zhiying Huang
paragraph: Ugliness in Technology Love, Death, Robot is a futuristic animation released by Netflix. The animation is based on the concept of a future where human brains can be separated from human bodies. My response used speculative design and argues that human brains might have new ways to manipulate their brains in the future. People might be able to wire their brains to control the intensity of their senses and emotions. I made a small box and put a fresh animal brain in the box. On both two sides of the inside box, there are five threads with different colors. Threads represent different senses and emotions. I also designed an app that can allow customers to adjust their brains by themselves. 20 21 Contribution to the Discipline As designers, we should be exploring the unknown aesthetics possibilities of both beauty and ​ ​ ugliness. As to the latter, ugliness has the extraordinary potential to engage us in a wide range of rich and meaningful experiences and emotions. In the book of Chinese Elementary School Art Education Training, teachers are instructed to teach students to appreciate beauty but refuse ugliness, as this is recognized to be the path to develop a ‘noble aesthetic’. Ugliness also can contribute to art education: understanding aesthetic and being able to appreciate ugliness reflects deeper thinking process abilities. We often rejected, ignored, and overlooked ugly artifacts. This thesis argues that we can rediscover ugly artifacts as a design material that can generate new sensibilities and functionalities. Ugliness and ugly design can and should broaden our understanding of the world we live in by offering speculative and experimental aesthetics. Furthermore, ugliness can be cathartic; by stimulating strong emotion, it allows us to question the status quo. This thesis argues that ugliness should also be used as a mode of inquiry, as a tool for self-reflection. 22
